<div class="dc-questions-index"><h2>Total questions: 900</h2><ul><li><strong><a href="#ai-ml" class="internal-link">AI &amp; ML</a> (162)</strong><ul><li><a href="#ai-ml-llm" class="internal-link">LLM</a> (123)</li><li><a href="#ai-ml-machine-learning" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a> (19)</li><li><a href="#ai-ml-tooling" class="internal-link">Tooling</a> (16)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#cloud" class="internal-link">Cloud</a> (3)</strong></li><li><strong><a href="#computer-science" class="internal-link">Computer Science</a> (302)</strong><ul><li><a href="#computer-science-algorithms" class="internal-link">Algorithms</a> (187)</li><li><a href="#computer-science-data-structures" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a> (109)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#data-persistence" class="internal-link">Data Persistence</a> (40)</strong><ul><li><a href="#data-persistence-nosql" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a> (13)</li><li><a href="#data-persistence-orms" class="internal-link">ORMs</a> (2)</li><li><a href="#data-persistence-sql" class="internal-link">SQL</a> (18)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#devops" class="internal-link">DevOps</a> (19)</strong><ul><li><a href="#devops-deployment-strategies" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a> (3)</li><li><a href="#devops-version-control-systems" class="internal-link">Version Control Systems</a> (2)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#networks" class="internal-link">Networks</a> (36)</strong><ul><li><a href="#networks-architecture-ops" class="internal-link">Architecture &amp; Ops</a> (8)</li><li><a href="#networks-protocols" class="internal-link">Protocols</a> (16)</li><li><a href="#networks-transport-sockets" class="internal-link">Transport &amp; Sockets</a> (8)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#programming" class="internal-link">Programming</a> (131)</strong><ul><li><a href="#programming-net" class="internal-link">NET</a> (131)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#security" class="internal-link">Security</a> (34)</strong><ul><li><a href="#security-authentication" class="internal-link">Authentication</a> (12)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#software-architecture" class="internal-link">Software Architecture</a> (142)</strong><ul><li><a href="#software-architecture-application-architecture" class="internal-link">Application Architecture</a> (9)</li><li><a href="#software-architecture-distributed-systems" class="internal-link">Distributed Systems</a> (34)</li><li><a href="#software-architecture-patterns" class="internal-link">Patterns</a> (85)</li><li><a href="#software-architecture-system-architecture" class="internal-link">System Architecture</a> (13)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#software-design" class="internal-link">Software Design</a> (31)</strong><ul><li><a href="#software-design-paradigms" class="internal-link">Paradigms</a> (9)</li><li><a href="#software-design-principles" class="internal-link">Principles</a> (12)</li><li><a href="#software-design-testing" class="internal-link">Testing</a> (9)</li></ul></li></ul><hr /><h1 id="ai-ml">AI &amp; ML</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do the six principles translate into concrete engineering work?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Fairness → slice-based evaluation, dataset audits, per-segment production monitoring</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliability and safety → adversarial testing, fallback paths, staged rollout via shadow mode</li>
<li dir="auto">Privacy and security → data minimization, anonymization, encryption, RBAC on models and data</li>
<li dir="auto">Inclusiveness → multilingual and accessibility evaluation, low-resource device testing</li>
<li dir="auto">Transparency → model cards, decision logging, explanatory UI for consequential outputs</li>
<li dir="auto">Accountability → human review gates, escalation ownership, rollback procedures</li>
<li dir="auto">The common thread: every principle is testable and monitorable — if it only lives in a policy document, it is not implemented<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Responsible AI.md" class="internal-link">Responsible AI</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is aggregate accuracy insufficient evidence that a system is fair?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Aggregate metrics average over the whole population, so strong majority-group performance can mask poor performance on minority groups</li>
<li dir="auto">A system can be 95% accurate overall while being 70% accurate for a specific demographic — the aggregate number hides exactly the disparity fairness is about</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is slice-based evaluation: measure the same metrics per demographic segment and treat a large gap as a defect, even when the aggregate looks healthy</li>
<li dir="auto">This mirrors the segmentation principle used elsewhere in monitoring: averages lie<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Responsible AI.md" class="internal-link">Responsible AI</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you reach for classic ML instead of an LLM API?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Classic ML wins when the task is a well-defined prediction with labeled data: classification, regression, ranking — millisecond latency and near-zero per-request cost at scale</li>
<li dir="auto">LLMs win when the task involves open-ended language understanding or generation, training data is scarce, or iteration speed matters more than unit cost</li>
<li dir="auto">A common production pattern: prototype with an LLM to validate the product, then distill the stable, high-volume part into a small fine-tuned model</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: classic ML trades upfront data and training effort for cheap, fast, predictable inference; LLMs trade per-call cost and latency for flexibility and zero training<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/AI &amp; ML.md" class="internal-link">AI &amp; ML</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does evaluation discipline matter more than model choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without held-out evaluation, every model swap, prompt change, or retraining run is a guess — improvements cannot be distinguished from noise or regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">Production failures are dominated by data and distribution problems (drift, leakage, segment regressions), which only evaluation and monitoring catch — not by raw model capability</li>
<li dir="auto">A weaker model with solid evaluation and a feedback loop improves over time; a stronger model without them silently degrades</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why every branch of this section has its own evaluation pages: <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">ML Evaluation</a> and the general <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">LLM Evaluation</a>, which RAG and agents specialize in <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">RAG Evaluation</a> and <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">Agent Evaluation</a><br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/AI &amp; ML.md" class="internal-link">AI &amp; ML</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-llm">LLM</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can Matryoshka dimensionality reduction lower embedding storage costs without significant recall loss?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use the <code>dimensions</code> parameter to truncate vectors to 256 or 512 dimensions. Models trained with Matryoshka objectives produce vectors where any prefix is independently meaningful. Truncation to 256 dims reduces storage by ~6x and speeds up ANN search proportionally. Validate by comparing recall@k on your evaluation set at 256, 512, and full dimensions — MRL models can retain strong retrieval quality at reduced dimensions, but the actual degradation is model-specific and corpus-dependent. If recall drops unacceptably, use a two-stage approach: retrieve at reduced dimensions, then re-rank candidates using full-dimensional vectors.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can switching to a higher-scoring embedding model cause recall to drop on existing queries?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Different embedding models produce vectors in different geometric spaces — cosine similarity between vectors from two different models is meaningless. If the corpus is not re-embedded with the new model, queries encoded with the new model are compared against vectors from the old model, producing degraded or random rankings. The fix is to re-embed the entire corpus with the new model before switching query-time inference. This is also why embedding caches must key by model name and version.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is domain-finetuning the embedding model justified over improving chunking or retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Finetuning is justified when retrieval failures are specifically caused by semantic mismatches on domain terminology — queries and relevant documents contain the same domain concepts but land far apart in vector space. If failures trace to split logical units (chunking issue), missing metadata filters (retrieval pipeline issue), or query ambiguity (query translation issue), finetuning the embedding model will not help. Diagnose by examining the top-k retrieved chunks for failed queries: if semantically relevant chunks exist but rank low, the embedding model is the bottleneck.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can LoRA still cause forgetting if the base weights are frozen?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The deployed output comes from the base plus the adapter’s updates. Narrow adapter training can steer that effective model away from capabilities outside the training distribution. The frozen base makes rollback easy; it does not guarantee unchanged behavior while the adapter is enabled.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should you estimate full fine-tuning memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Account separately for weights, gradients, optimizer states, activations, temporary buffers, precision, and sharding. Sequence length, batch, checkpointing, and optimizer choice can move the total enough that a generic multiplier is not a safe capacity plan.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does GRPO remove compared with PPO-style language-model training?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It removes the separately learned value model by estimating relative advantage from a group of sampled completions. It retains policy sampling, reward computation, clipped updates, and a reference-policy constraint.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is DPO’s operational advantage over reward-model RLHF?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It trains directly from preference pairs without a separate learned reward model or online policy-optimization loop. The tradeoff is that it cannot explore and score fresh outputs during the update.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is adjusting temperature and top_p simultaneously discouraged?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both reshape the same token probability distribution but through different mechanisms. Temperature scales the logit distribution (sharpening or flattening), while top_p truncates it to a cumulative mass threshold. Changing both creates unpredictable interactions — a low temperature already concentrates probability mass, so a low top_p on top of it may have no additional effect, while a high temperature with a low top_p creates conflicting signals. Tuning one while keeping the other at default keeps behavior predictable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a grounded response still contain unsupported claims despite citation tags?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Models can attach citation markers to claims without verifying entailment. The citation looks correct but the cited passage may not actually support the claim — it may be topically related but not evidentially sufficient. This is why citation generation alone is not grounding: a separate claim-to-source verification step (NLI or similar) is needed to confirm that each cited passage actually entails the claim it is attached to.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should constrained decoding be preferred over JSON mode for structured output?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Constrained decoding enforces a specific JSON schema at the token level during generation — the output is structurally compliant by construction. JSON mode only guarantees valid JSON without schema enforcement, so the model can return any valid JSON structure. Use constrained decoding when downstream systems depend on a specific schema (API contracts, database inserts, tool arguments). Use JSON mode when you want parsable output but the structure is flexible or exploratory.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does architecture matter when someone says “LLM”?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only transformers expose different inputs, objectives, and output paths. A BERT checkpoint is not a causal text generator, while T5 generates through an autoregressive decoder conditioned on encoder output.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What must match besides checkpoint tensor bytes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The architecture and configuration, tensor names and layouts, tokenizer and special-token IDs, adaptation or quantization metadata, and runtime operator implementations must agree. Verify the bundle with known-answer inference, not only a successful file parse.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is active parameter count not an inference-cost measurement?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It omits dense layers, memory traffic, token dispatch, interconnect communication, batching, and expert imbalance. Measure throughput and latency on the target serving stack.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start with prompting. Add RAG when the gap is current, private, or attributable knowledge. Fine-tune when a measured behavior gap remains—format, policy, style, or a narrow task that prompting cannot stabilize.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are “frontier is best” and “small is cheap” insufficient selection rules?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They describe common tendencies, not the result on a particular task and serving stack. Selection requires measured quality, safety, latency, and cost per successful task under the same workload.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Model Selection and Routing.md" class="internal-link">Model Selection and Routing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use a classifier instead of a cascade?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a classifier when difficulty can be predicted before generation and duplicate latency is expensive. Use a cascade when the first result produces a reliable, cheap failure signal. Evaluate both end to end because classifier misses and cascade retries fail differently.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Model Selection and Routing.md" class="internal-link">Model Selection and Routing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Agents</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do the five patterns form a progression, and how do you choose among them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They climb in complexity and in how much control moves from the developer to the model:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Prompt chaining</strong> — fixed sequence, developer owns every step. Use when the task splits into predictable stages.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Routing</strong> — one classification decision fans out to specialized paths. Use for distinct input categories that need different handling.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Parallelization</strong> — independent calls run concurrently and aggregate. Use for speed (sectioning) or reliability (voting).</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Orchestrator-workers</strong> — the subtasks themselves are decided at runtime. Use when you can't enumerate the steps in advance.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Evaluator-optimizer</strong> — a generate/critique loop refines toward criteria. Use when there is a clear quality signal and feedback improves the output.<br />
The rule is to start at the top and move down only when a simpler pattern demonstrably fails. Every step down buys flexibility at the cost of predictability, latency, and debuggability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Workflow Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Workflow Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What separates orchestrator-workers from parallelization when the diagrams look nearly identical?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both fan work out to several LLM calls and aggregate the results, so the topology is similar. The difference is <em>when the subtasks are known</em>. In parallelization the subtasks are fixed by the developer up front — you decide there are three sections or three votes before any call runs. In orchestrator-workers a central LLM reads the input and <em>decides at runtime</em> how to decompose it, how many workers to spawn, and what each should do. That runtime decision is what makes orchestrator-workers the bridge into <a class="internal-link" href="Multi-Agentic Systems">Multi-Agentic Systems</a>: it is the simplest pattern where the model, not the code path, controls the shape of the work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Workflow Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Workflow Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why add gates between steps in prompt chaining instead of a single prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Chaining trades one hard call for several easier ones, and the gates are where that trade pays off. Each step does a narrower job, so it is more reliable and easier to prompt than asking one call to do everything at once. The programmatic gates between steps — a length check, a schema validation, a "does this outline meet the criteria" test — catch failures early, before a bad intermediate result propagates and compounds downstream. The cost is added latency and more moving parts, so chaining is worth it only when the task genuinely decomposes into fixed subtasks; if it doesn't, a single well-prompted call is simpler and cheaper.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Workflow Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Workflow Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use a workflow instead of an autonomous agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use a workflow when the task decomposes into predictable steps with clear inputs and outputs at each stage</li>
<li dir="auto">Workflows are cheaper, faster, and more debuggable than autonomous agents</li>
<li dir="auto">Use an autonomous agent only when steps are unpredictable, the task is open-ended, and you have feedback mechanisms (tests, eval criteria) to catch errors</li>
<li dir="auto">Most production "agents" are actually workflows — and that is the right choice for the majority of use cases</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: workflows trade flexibility for reliability; agents trade reliability for adaptability<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do autonomous agents accumulate error, and how do you bound it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each step an agent takes is conditioned on the output of the last one, so a small early mistake — a misread tool result, a wrong assumption — gets carried forward and compounds, pushing the agent further off track with every loop. That compounding is the main reason to prefer a workflow when you can: fixed code paths don't drift. When you do need autonomy, you bound the damage with hard iteration caps, explicit gates that validate progress before continuing, transparency into the planning steps so you can see where it went wrong, and a human-in-the-loop escape hatch when the agent is blocked. The throughline is feedback: the agent needs a way to catch its own drift before it cascades.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a task a good fit for an autonomous agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Two things together: the task is genuinely open-ended — you can't predict the number of steps or write a fixed workflow for it — and it has a clear, checkable success signal the agent can use to judge its own progress. That's why coding works (tests pass or fail), customer support works (the issue is resolved or not), and research works (claims trace back to sources). Tasks with vague or delayed success criteria are where agents flail, because there's no feedback to correct the compounding error. If you can't define what "done" and "correct" look like in a way the system can check, the task isn't ready for an agent yet.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Evaluation</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the same model post very different scores on the same agent benchmark?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Agent benchmarks score a model <em>plus its scaffold</em> — planning loop, retries, tool design, prompt — and the scaffold contributes a large share of the result</li>
<li dir="auto">Two teams running the same base model with different harnesses get different SWE-bench numbers, so cross-paper comparisons are unreliable unless the scaffold is held constant</li>
<li dir="auto">Contamination and harness-specific prompt tuning add further variance over time</li>
<li dir="auto">The takeaway: use leaderboards to shortlist models, then re-evaluate candidates under <em>your</em> scaffold on <em>your</em> tasks</li>
<li dir="auto">Holding the scaffold constant for a fair comparison costs engineering setup, but without it the numbers don't mean what they appear to<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Agent Benchmarks.md" class="internal-link">Agent Benchmarks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is pass^k a more honest agent metric than pass@1, and when does it matter most?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">pass@1 averages success over independent attempts and hides variance; an agent that solves a task 6/10 times looks similar to one that solves it 10/10</li>
<li dir="auto">pass^k credits a task only if solved on all k tries, directly measuring reliability — the property production users actually experience</li>
<li dir="auto">It matters most for high-stakes or unattended tasks (payments, code merges) where one failure in k is unacceptable</li>
<li dir="auto">pass^k is typically well below pass@1, so reporting only pass@1 overstates production readiness</li>
<li dir="auto">Measuring pass^k costs k× the eval runs, so spend it on the tasks where variance is intolerable and use cheaper mean success for low-stakes breadth<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Agent Benchmarks.md" class="internal-link">Agent Benchmarks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why report tool-selection and argument accuracy separately instead of one tool-call score?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The two failures have different root causes and fixes: selection errors point at tool descriptions and prompt routing, argument errors point at grounding and schema design</li>
<li dir="auto">A single averaged number can look healthy while one axis is broken — 95% selection with 70% arguments averages to a misleading 82%</li>
<li dir="auto">They need different scorers: selection is reference/judge, arguments are exact for structured fields and semantic for free text</li>
<li dir="auto">Acting on the blended number wastes effort optimizing the half that is already fine</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-axis scoring costs more labels and more judge calls, but without it you can't tell which fix to ship — spend the granularity on the tools where a wrong call is expensive<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Tool-Call Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Tool-Call Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are deterministic schema checks necessary but not sufficient for tool-call evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">They catch malformed JSON, unknown tools, and wrong arity for free and pre-execution, so they can block a bad call before it runs</li>
<li dir="auto">They are blind to semantics: a call with perfect schema and a wrong order id or mis-scoped query passes every check and then executes against real state</li>
<li dir="auto">They cannot judge selection or necessity — whether a tool should have been called at all</li>
<li dir="auto">Pair them with reference argument matching (for structured fields) and a judge (for selection/necessity), and add confirmation steps for irreversible actions</li>
<li dir="auto">The semantic layers cost labels and judge calls, so gate them on the high-risk tools rather than running them on every read-only call<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Tool-Call Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Tool-Call Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you use reference-trajectory matching versus an LLM judge over the trace?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Reference matching fits tasks with a small, knowable set of correct paths; it is cheap, objective, and can assert hard constraints (subset = stayed in bounds, superset = required work done)</li>
<li dir="auto">It breaks when many paths are valid — strict matching then penalizes correct alternate solutions and measures conformance, not quality</li>
<li dir="auto">A judge handles open-ended tasks and rates plan quality, redundancy, and recovery that matching cannot see, at the cost of a model call per run and long-context bias</li>
<li dir="auto">Common setup: subset match as a cheap safety gate (no out-of-scope tools) plus a judge for path quality, backed by outcome and efficiency metrics</li>
<li dir="auto">Matching is cheap but brittle and quality-blind; judging is flexible but expensive and biased — choose by how enumerable the correct paths are<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Trajectory Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Trajectory Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a high trajectory-judge score be misleading, and how do you guard against it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">If the judge sees the task succeeded, outcome leakage makes it rationalize a messy path as good</li>
<li dir="auto">On long traces the judge skims the middle and rewards verbosity, so a bloated trajectory can outscore a clean one</li>
<li dir="auto">Guard by withholding the outcome from the path judge (or scoring path and outcome separately) and by validating against human labels on long runs</li>
<li dir="auto">Pair the judge with objective efficiency counters (steps, cost, redundant calls) that a biased judge cannot launder</li>
<li dir="auto">Separate, calibrated, step-localized judging costs more calls, so spend it on the long, high-stakes trajectories where path quality actually matters<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Trajectory Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Trajectory Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is outcome-only scoring insufficient for evaluating an agent, and what do you add?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A correct final state can be reached by a wasteful or wrong path — three failed tool calls before a lucky success scores identically to a clean solve</li>
<li dir="auto">Outcome-only hides cost, latency, and compounding error risk, so a more expensive or fragile agent looks equal to a cheaper reliable one</li>
<li dir="auto">Add process metrics: tool-call validity (deterministic), tool-selection and trajectory quality (judge or reference), and efficiency counters (steps, cost, latency)</li>
<li dir="auto">Add reliability: run each task k times and report pass^k, since stochastic trajectories make a single run an unreliable estimate</li>
<li dir="auto">Process and reliability scoring multiply eval cost (k runs, a judge call per trace), so spend it where path quality and variance actually affect users and keep cheap outcome+efficiency gates everywhere else<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect and measure agent non-termination and looping?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Non-termination shows up as runs that hit the step cap without reaching a terminal state; track cap-hit rate as a first-class metric</li>
<li dir="auto">Oscillation shows up as repeated identical or alternating tool calls; detect by hashing (tool, args) per step and flagging repeats within a trajectory</li>
<li dir="auto">Both inflate cost and latency long before they change task success, so latency/step-count distributions catch them earlier than outcome metrics</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigation: enforce step and cost caps, add progress checks, and make the agent's plan explicit so a judge can see where it stalled</li>
<li dir="auto">Tight caps cut runaway cost but can truncate genuinely hard tasks — set caps from the step-count distribution of known-good runs, not a round number<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Context Engineering</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does adding more context often make answers worse, not better?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Model attention is uneven: it concentrates on the start and end of the window and underweights the middle ("lost in the middle"), so critical evidence placed there is effectively ignored</li>
<li dir="auto">Overall quality degrades as input grows even when the added tokens are relevant — attention dilutes across more material (context rot)</li>
<li dir="auto">Irrelevant tokens actively compete with relevant ones for finite attention, and larger contexts cost more and add latency</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is signal density: fewer, higher-quality, well-ordered chunks beat a larger but noisier window — measure quality as you vary context size rather than assuming more helps<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/Context Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Context Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main techniques for keeping a long-running agent's context under control?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Compaction: summarize or prune older turns before they crowd out the task; cap tool-result size to only the fields needed</li>
<li dir="auto">Offloading: move large intermediate state to a scratchpad or filesystem and pass lightweight references back into the window</li>
<li dir="auto">Isolation: split work across sub-agents along context boundaries so each window stays focused</li>
<li dir="auto">Selection and ordering: retrieve few high-signal chunks and place the most important evidence at the start and end</li>
<li dir="auto">Accounting: track cumulative tokens per iteration and compact or terminate before the window fills, rather than letting the runtime truncate the oldest (often most important) messages<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/Context Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Context Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>RAG</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should retrieval cache keys be based on processed query text instead of raw embeddings?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Processed query text and transformation version are deterministic, auditable, and stable across embedding model upgrades. Raw embedding bytes are opaque, change with every model swap, and make cache invalidation on model upgrade impossible without full cache flush. Keying on processed text also aligns cache correctness with the query translation pipeline — if the translation changes, the key changes automatically.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is response caching riskier than embedding caching?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Embedding is a pure function: same text plus same model always produces the same vector. Response generation depends on the system prompt template, the retrieved evidence (which changes with index updates), the user's permissions, and the model version — all mutable. A cached response can become wrong when any of these inputs change without cache invalidation. Embedding cache entries only go stale when the source text or model version changes, which are infrequent and structurally detectable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is semantic caching safe to deploy, and when should it be avoided?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Semantic caching is safe when the domain is narrow, queries are repetitive, false positives have low cost, and you can calibrate a reliable similarity threshold on a held-out set. It should be avoided in high-stakes domains (medical, legal, financial) where a wrong cached answer causes harm, in multi-turn conversations where context changes the correct answer, and when the query distribution is too diverse to find a threshold that balances hit rate against false-positive rate.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is sampled LLM-as-judge scoring preferred over scoring every response in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Scoring every response doubles per-request cost and adds latency if synchronous.</li>
<li dir="auto">At production scale (thousands of queries/hour), full scoring is prohibitively expensive.</li>
<li dir="auto">Sampled scoring (5–20%) provides statistical coverage of the quality distribution at a fraction of the cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stratified sampling ensures rare but important query types (multi-hop, negation, edge-case domains) are represented.</li>
<li dir="auto">Async execution decouples scoring from user-facing latency — users never wait for the judge.</li>
<li dir="auto">Full scoring is reserved for offline evaluation runs against labeled eval sets.</li>
<li dir="auto">Lower sample rates cut cost but raise the odds of missing a localized regression in a small query cluster; start at 10–20% and reduce only once the distribution looks stable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should RAG alerting use relative regression thresholds instead of absolute quality targets?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Absolute thresholds ("faithfulness &gt; 0.9") are brittle across corpus changes, model updates, and query distribution shifts.</li>
<li dir="auto">A threshold calibrated at launch becomes meaningless after the corpus doubles or query mix evolves.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds ("no more than 5% drop from 7-day rolling baseline") adapt automatically because the baseline tracks current system state.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds prevent the failure mode where a team sets an ambitious absolute target, cannot reach it consistently, and silently disables the alert.</li>
<li dir="auto">Baselines must be recomputed after intentional pipeline changes (model swap, prompt update, index rebuild) to avoid false alarms on expected shifts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds can miss slow drift that stays inside the rolling window, so pair them with a periodic absolute floor check — a monthly look at whether the baseline itself is still acceptable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does monitoring differ from evaluation in a RAG system, and why do you need both?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Evaluation validates a pipeline configuration against a labeled dataset before deployment — it gates releases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring validates the pipeline continuously against live traffic after deployment — it catches production regressions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Eval sets are static snapshots; production traffic shifts continuously with new query patterns, corpus updates, and model provider changes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring catches failure modes that no static eval set anticipates: seasonal query shifts, silent model downgrades, load-dependent degradation.</li>
<li dir="auto">The feedback loop connects them: failing production traces identified by monitoring get added to the eval set, preventing recurrence in future releases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring alone catches problems only after users feel them; evaluation alone misses production-specific failures. You need both.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does parent-child chunking often improve answer completeness over child-only retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Child chunks maximize retrieval precision — small, focused units match specific queries well. But child-only context can miss adjacent constraints that the answer depends on (e.g., a policy rule in one child and its exception in a sibling). Parent expansion restores the surrounding context during generation, reducing partial or decontextualized answers. The tradeoff is increased context size and potential noise from the broader parent span.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team move from recursive to structure-aware chunking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Move when retrieval errors consistently trace back to broken structural units — split tables, bisected code blocks, or separated clause/exception pairs. Recursive splitting respects paragraph boundaries but is blind to document-specific structures. Structure-aware chunking preserves those units at the cost of format-specific parser investment and ongoing parser maintenance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is semantic chunking not always superior to simpler rule-based approaches?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Semantic chunking places boundaries at actual topic shifts, which sounds ideal. But it requires embedding every span during ingestion (high cost), the similarity threshold is sensitive to embedding model and domain (tuning burden), and threshold instability can cause over-fragmentation or oversized chunks. For documents with reliable structural markers (headings, sections), recursive or structure-aware chunking achieves similar boundary quality at a fraction of the ingestion cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is GraphRAG a better fit than plain vector retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When answers require explicit entity relations, dependency paths, or multi-hop joins that are hard to recover from independent text chunks. Examples: compliance tracing across policy documents, architecture dependency analysis, supply chain impact assessment. Skip GraphRAG for simple fact lookups where vector similarity suffices.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG Patterns.md" class="internal-link">RAG Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is hybrid search plus reranking usually added before GraphRAG or agentic RAG?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hybrid search and reranking fix the most common production failure first: the right evidence is missing or buried under noisy chunks. They reuse the same corpus and retrieval pipeline, so the integration cost is lower than building agents or knowledge graphs. GraphRAG and agentic RAG are justified only when evals show relationship reasoning or multi-tool orchestration is the actual bottleneck. The tradeoff is that hybrid search improves retrieval quality cheaply, while graph and agentic systems buy extra capability at a large indexing, latency, and observability cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG Patterns.md" class="internal-link">RAG Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does query translation often improve recall but sometimes hurt precision, and how do you detect the tradeoff?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Additional query variants cover vocabulary the original misses, hitting more document neighborhoods and improving recall</li>
<li dir="auto">Weak variants introduce concepts not in the original intent, pulling related-but-irrelevant documents into the candidate set</li>
<li dir="auto">Precision drop is invisible in aggregate recall metrics — recall goes up while noisy candidates are buried in the ranked list</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection: measure precision@k (not just recall@k) before and after translation, segmented by query type</li>
<li dir="auto">If recall@20 improves but precision@5 drops, translation helps coverage but hurts candidates that reach the generator</li>
<li dir="auto">Key mitigation: always include the original query as a variant and discard translations that drift too far from the original intent<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is decomposition a better choice than multi-query, and when does it hurt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Decomposition fits when the original query has distinct sub-problems requiring separate evidence — comparisons, multi-entity questions, timeline questions</li>
<li dir="auto">Multi-query fits when the question has a single intent but user phrasing may not match document terminology</li>
<li dir="auto">Decomposition hurts on single-intent questions: sub-questions fragment a coherent topic and lose the constraints that make the original specific</li>
<li dir="auto">The test: if sub-questions can be answered independently and concatenated answers address the original, decomposition fits</li>
<li dir="auto">If sub-questions only make sense in context of the original question, use multi-query instead</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: decomposition adds a synthesis LLM call and risks context fragmentation; multi-query only adds retrieval calls<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can HyDE outperform direct query embedding for vague questions but fail on specific factual queries?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Vague queries produce sparse, underspecified embeddings equidistant from many document clusters — poor retrieval signal</li>
<li dir="auto">HyDE generates a hypothetical answer paragraph, creating a denser point in embedding space closer to real answer documents</li>
<li dir="auto">The encoder's bottleneck filters hallucinated details while preserving the correct semantic neighborhood</li>
<li dir="auto">For specific factual queries (error codes, versions), the LLM may hallucinate wrong details — different error code, different version</li>
<li dir="auto">The hallucinated embedding steers retrieval toward documents matching the wrong specifics</li>
<li dir="auto">Direct query embedding, while sparse, preserves exact tokens that <a class="internal-link" href="Retrieval#Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)" aria-label="Retrieval &gt; Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)" data-tooltip-position="top">keyword search</a> in a hybrid setup can catch</li>
<li dir="auto">HyDE failure is stealth: retrieved documents look topically relevant but answer the wrong specific question<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can reranking improve offline <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">nDCG</a> without visible quality improvement for end users?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The improvement may be in ranking positions that the generator does not use. If the generator only reads the top-3 chunks, improvements at positions 4–5 are invisible to users. Evaluate whether the reranker changes the top-k composition that actually enters the prompt, not just overall <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">nDCG</a>. Also verify that the eval set reflects production query distribution — gains on eval-set query types may not represent the queries users actually send.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does reranking hurt retrieval quality instead of helping?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the reranker is out-of-distribution for your domain. A reranker trained on short web passages may misjudge relevance on long technical documents, internal terminology, or multilingual content — demoting actually relevant documents. Also when the candidate count is too small: if first-stage recall@N is already low, the reranker only reorders noise. Always compare <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">recall and precision</a> before and after reranking on domain-specific queries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is ColBERT faster than a cross-encoder at query time despite also using token-level scoring?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">ColBERT pre-computes per-token document embeddings at index time and stores them. At query time, only the query tokens need encoding (one forward pass regardless of candidate count). Scoring is a MaxSim matrix operation over pre-stored vectors, not a full transformer forward pass per document. Cross-encoders must run a complete forward pass for each query-document pair because they jointly encode the concatenated input. The tradeoff is that ColBERT needs multi-vector storage, which uses more space and requires specialized indexes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can vector-only retrieval underperform on technical support workloads?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Technical support queries often include exact identifiers: error codes, version strings, API paths, and SKU IDs. Embedding models capture meaning, not specific tokens — a query for "error E4392 in v2.3" may retrieve content about error handling in general rather than the specific code. The failure is subtle because returned chunks are topically related, so the LLM synthesizes a plausible but wrong answer. Keyword search catches these exact tokens because they are rare in the corpus (high BM25 weight), which is why hybrid retrieval is essential for these workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does hybrid retrieval perform worse than single-mode retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the weaker search mode contributes more noise than signal to the fused list. On homogeneous corpora where one mode dominates (e.g., scientific documents with consistent terminology and natural-language queries), the non-dominant mode pulls in marginally relevant candidates that dilute fused results. In production benchmarks, vector-only has beaten hybrid on scientific corpora because keyword search on specialized vocabulary introduced noise. Always evaluate hybrid against single-mode baselines on your actual corpus before committing to the added complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HNSW recall degrade silently as the vector database grows?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HNSW navigates a graph to find approximate nearest neighbors, not an exhaustive scan. The <code>ef_search</code> parameter controls how many candidate nodes the search visits. At small corpus sizes, a moderate <code>ef_search</code> finds most true neighbors. As the corpus grows, the graph becomes denser and the same <code>ef_search</code> misses more true neighbors — the search path does not explore enough of the graph to find them. Latency stays stable because the search still visits the same number of candidates, and no errors are raised. The only signal is less relevant retrieved chunks. Detection requires explicit <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">Recall@k</a> monitoring against a ground-truth test set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should advanced RAG patterns be introduced incrementally instead of all at once?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each pattern adds independent failure modes and observability needs. Incremental rollout isolates impact, allows A/B measurement against baseline, and prevents compounding complexity from masking root causes. Start with the pattern that addresses your highest-frequency failure mode.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does fine-tuning beat adding more retrieval sophistication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the failure is behavioral, not factual: the model retrieves the right evidence but keeps producing the wrong format, tone, or policy behavior despite prompt iteration. Retrieval upgrades cannot fix behavior encoded in weights. Conversely, fine-tuning cannot fix missing or stale knowledge — it bakes in a snapshot that starts aging immediately and provides no source traceability. Diagnose first: if faithfulness is high but style or policy compliance is low, fine-tune; if evidence is missing or wrong, improve retrieval.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When a RAG answer is wrong, how do you tell whether retrieval or generation is at fault?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Split the pipeline and score the two halves separately, because the fixes are opposite.
