First check whether the right evidence was retrieved at all: if the relevant chunk never made it into the context, it's a retrieval failure — improve chunking, hybrid search, or reranking, and no amount of prompt tuning will help. If the evidence <em>was</em> present but the answer ignored or contradicted it, that's a generation/faithfulness failure — tighten the prompt, add groundedness checks, or use a stronger model. This is exactly why RAG evaluation reports retrieval and generation as separate metrics; a single end-to-end accuracy number hides which half to fix.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do vector databases use approximate nearest-neighbor search instead of exact search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Exact (brute-force) search compares the query against every stored vector — correct, but O(N) per query and far too slow once there are millions of vectors</li>
<li dir="auto">ANN indexes (HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ) trade a small, measurable amount of recall for orders-of-magnitude lower latency, returning most of the true nearest neighbors in sub-millisecond time</li>
<li dir="auto">The recall given up is silent: there is no error, and latency stays stable, so it only shows up as slightly worse retrieved context unless you measure ANN recall against brute-force ground truth</li>
<li dir="auto">The right operating point is chosen by tuning index parameters (<code>ef_search</code>, <code>nprobe</code>) on the recall–latency curve for your corpus and SLA<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use pgvector or an existing search engine instead of a dedicated vector database?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When you already run the database (Postgres, OpenSearch) and the corpus is modest: keeping vectors beside your relational or lexical data avoids operating a second system and simplifies hybrid search</li>
<li dir="auto">pgvector gives you transactional consistency and joins with existing data, which a standalone vector DB cannot</li>
<li dir="auto">Reach for a dedicated vector database when scale, filtered-search recall under narrow filters, or specialized indexes (IVF-PQ, DiskANN) exceed what the add-on supports</li>
<li dir="auto">The tradeoff is operational simplicity (one system) versus peak scale and index flexibility (dedicated engine) — start with the add-on and migrate only when a real limit is hit<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HNSW recall degrade as the corpus grows, and how do you catch it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">HNSW finds neighbors by walking a proximity graph, exploring a number of candidates set by <code>ef_search</code>; as the graph grows denser, a fixed <code>ef_search</code> covers proportionally less of it and misses more true neighbors</li>
<li dir="auto">Latency stays constant (the search visits the same number of candidates) and no error is raised, so infrastructure dashboards show everything healthy while retrieval quality silently drops</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection requires an explicit Recall@k check against brute-force ground truth on a scheduled query set</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is to re-tune <code>ef_search</code> upward as the corpus grows, accepting a little more latency to hold recall<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h5>Evaluation</h5><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does ANN recall degrade silently as the corpus grows while latency stays flat?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">At fixed <code>ef_search</code>/<code>nprobe</code>, the search explores a constant amount of work regardless of corpus size</li>
<li dir="auto">As more vectors crowd the graph, that fixed search path covers a proportionally smaller fraction of it, so true neighbors are missed more often</li>
<li dir="auto">Latency is driven by the search budget, not corpus size, so it stays constant and gives no signal that recall has dropped</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection requires explicit ANN recall checks against brute-force ground truth on a scheduled query set — dashboards watching only latency will not catch it</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: re-tune <code>ef_search</code>/<code>nprobe</code> as the corpus grows, or move to a parameterization that scales the search budget with index size</li>
<li dir="auto">Raising the search budget restores recall but increases p99 latency — pick the knee of the recall-vs-latency curve rather than maxing either<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Component-Level Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Component-Level Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is token-level IoU more informative than Recall@k when comparing chunking strategies?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Recall@k only asks whether a relevant chunk appeared; it ignores how much irrelevant text rode along inside that chunk</li>
<li dir="auto">A strategy can hit high Recall@k by returning large, noisy chunks that waste context-window tokens on irrelevant content</li>
<li dir="auto">Token IoU scores the overlap between retrieved tokens and the gold evidence span, penalizing both missed evidence (recall) and noise (precision) in one number</li>
<li dir="auto">Concrete gap: the default 800/400 OpenAI config reached 87.9% token recall but 1.4% token precision — Recall@k would call it a success while IoU exposes the waste</li>
<li dir="auto">Use IoU when context budget or generator attention dilution matters; fall back to ablation via Recall@k and Faithfulness when building token-span ground truth is too expensive</li>
<li dir="auto">IoU needs <code>(query, gold_span)</code> labels, which cost more to produce than binary relevance — invest only where chunk efficiency materially affects cost or answer quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Component-Level Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Component-Level Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can aggregate retrieval metrics improve while individual user segments degrade?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Aggregate metrics average across query types and tenants, masking localized regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">A pipeline change improving average Recall@5 can simultaneously degrade recall by double digits on a specific tenant's query cluster</li>
<li dir="auto">RAG workloads are heterogeneous — different query types, document formats, and languages have different retrieval characteristics</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is segment-level evaluation: slice by tenant, language, query cluster, and document source type</li>
<li dir="auto">Flag any segment that degrades beyond the threshold, even when the aggregate improves</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-segment evaluation does cost more to maintain (more ground-truth labels, more compute per release), but it catches the most common RAG evaluation failure in production — choose granularity based on how heterogeneous your query population is<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation Metrics.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation Metrics</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Given high Faithfulness (0.91) and low Context Recall (0.54), which pipeline layer do you fix first and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">High faithfulness means the model correctly uses the context it receives — generation is not the problem</li>
<li dir="auto">Low context recall means retrieval misses roughly half the necessary evidence — the retrieval layer is the bottleneck</li>
<li dir="auto">Retrieval quality is a hard ceiling on answer quality: the generator cannot use evidence it never sees</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix retrieval first: add hybrid search (BM25 + dense), expand k, review metadata filters, check embedding domain fit</li>
<li dir="auto">Do not touch prompts or generation settings until recall improves — optimizing generation against incomplete evidence is wasted effort</li>
<li dir="auto">After retrieval fix, re-measure both: if faithfulness drops as recall improves, the additional context is confusing the model — add re-ranking or improve prompt grounding</li>
<li dir="auto">Improving recall often decreases precision (more chunks = more noise), so pair recall improvements with re-ranking to maintain context quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation Metrics.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation Metrics</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why decompose RAG evaluation into separate retrieval, generation, and end-to-end layers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because a single "quality" score tells you something broke but not where, and the fix is completely different per layer. Retrieval metrics ask whether the right evidence even reached the generator; generation metrics ask whether the answer is faithful to that evidence and actually addresses the question; end-to-end asks whether the user's task got solved. The decomposition is what separates the two failures that look identical from outside: perfect retrieval with a model that ignores the context, and flawless generation over irrelevant documents the retriever never should have returned. Without it you chase retrieval tuning that can't fix a generation bug — and burn a sprint doing it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What belongs in RAG evaluation specifically versus general LLM evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RAG reuses the whole general eval stack — LLM-as-a-judge, deterministic checks, golden sets, synthetic generation, the online/A-B loop — and adds only what's genuinely RAG-shaped on top. That specific part is three things: retrieval-quality metrics (did the right chunks arrive, ranked well), faithfulness/groundedness (is the answer supported by the retrieved evidence rather than the model's parametric memory), and the labeling problem of which chunks count as relevant when a query maps to several. Everything else is inherited. Keeping that line clean is what stops the eval system from being rebuilt per domain — the general machinery lives once, and RAG, agents, and plain prompts each specialize it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When several chunks are relevant to one query, how do you decide which retrieval metric to score with?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">First classify <em>why</em> they are relevant — substitutable (any one fully answers) versus complementary (the answer needs all of them combined)</li>
<li dir="auto">Substitutable: score HitRate@k and MRR — success is one good chunk arriving early; strict recall or MAP wrongly penalizes not retrieving redundant copies</li>
<li dir="auto">Complementary: score Recall@k and Context Recall — every required chunk must arrive; HitRate is misleading because one-of-three looks like a pass but yields an incomplete answer</li>
<li dir="auto">When chunks differ in usefulness rather than count, use graded labels (0/1/2) and nDCG@k, the only common metric that consumes grades and rewards ranking the best chunk first</li>
<li dir="auto">Set k from how many chunks the generator actually consumes; for complementary cases k must be at least the number of required chunks or recall is capped below 1 by construction</li>
<li dir="auto">Graded multi-chunk labels cost far more annotation effort than single-chunk binary labels — invest only where the generator genuinely fuses multiple sources (multi-hop, comparison), and keep single-label binary sets for simple lookup queries<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Retrieval Evaluation Sets.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval Evaluation Sets</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do synthetically generated retrieval eval sets often report worse recall than the system delivers in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Chunk-anchored generation labels only the source chunk as relevant, but the synthesized query is frequently answerable by other unlabeled chunks (duplicated text, overview paragraphs, near-identical FAQ entries)</li>
<li dir="auto">The retriever returns a genuinely relevant chunk, but because it is not in the ground truth, Precision@k and MRR score it as a miss — a false negative in the labels, not a retrieval failure</li>
<li dir="auto">This deflates absolute numbers and, worse, can rank a better retriever below a worse one, corrupting model-selection decisions</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: after generation, run a second pass (the retriever plus an LLM judge over top-k) to label additional chunks that also answer the query, or discard ambiguous queries</li>
<li dir="auto">Secondary cause: lexical leakage — the LLM copies rare phrasing from the source chunk, inflating exact-match and BM25 scores on queries no real user would type</li>
<li dir="auto">The dedup/judge pass does add LLM cost per query and some noise of its own, but without it synthetic retrieval metrics are systematically pessimistic and unreliable for ranking pipelines<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/Evaluation/Retrieval Evaluation Sets.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval Evaluation Sets</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Evaluation</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the minimum useful set of deterministic checks for a tool-using agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">(1) Allowlisted tools/actions only, (2) strict output schema validation, (3) PII scanning on outputs, (4) injection-resistant formatting, (5) length constraints. These catch the most common failure modes cheaply before any expensive evaluation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Deterministic Checks.md" class="internal-link">Deterministic Checks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is a golden test set a scoring stage?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No. It is a versioned regression dataset. Exact predicates, semantic judges, and human raters score outputs generated from its cases; the regression gate compares those results with a pinned baseline or threshold.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a deterministic check have zero classification error?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Only when it evaluates an exact product predicate, such as schema validity or membership in an allowed set, and the implementation matches that contract. Deterministic PII, toxicity, and content heuristics still have false positives and false negatives.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a strong offline score insufficient to ship?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A fixed corpus cannot reproduce every traffic shift, multi-turn interaction, or user outcome. Use offline regression as a release gate, then estimate production impact with monitoring and controlled online experiments.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why keep a frozen holdout slice separate from the golden set you iterate on?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Iterating prompts against the golden set until scores improve turns it into a training set — improvements stop measuring generalization</li>
<li dir="auto">The holdout slice is never used for tuning, so it stays an unbiased estimate of real quality</li>
<li dir="auto">Use the main golden set for day-to-day iteration; check the holdout only at release gates</li>
<li dir="auto">If holdout and golden-set scores diverge (golden improves, holdout does not), you have overfit to the golden set — refresh it from production traffic</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: the holdout "wastes" labeled cases you cannot tune on, but without it you cannot trust any of your numbers<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md" class="internal-link">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a new failure belong in the golden set versus a targeted eval suite?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Add it to a targeted suite when it represents a specific failure mode you want fast, focused signal on (injection, PII leakage, groundedness) — small suites run quickly and localize regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">Add it to the golden set when it represents the normal operating range: a realistic user request that the system must keep handling correctly</li>
<li dir="auto">Production incidents usually warrant both: a targeted case reproducing the exact failure, and a generalized case covering the query pattern</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: targeted suites give precision but narrow coverage; the golden set gives breadth but slower, noisier signal — incidents are cheap opportunities to grow both<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md" class="internal-link">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I prefer LLM-as-a-judge over classic metrics, and how do I know the judge is trustworthy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use judges for open-ended generation where semantics matter and deterministic metrics (exact match, BLEU) can't capture quality.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use classic metrics for deterministic outputs or when you need hard guarantees.</li>
<li dir="auto">The key signal: if a human would need to read the answer to evaluate it, a judge model probably should too.</li>
<li dir="auto">Measure judge trustworthiness by checking agreement with a small human-labeled set.</li>
<li dir="auto">Track drift over time by re-running a fixed gold dataset after model updates.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: judge reliability is not assumed — it must be validated and maintained like any other test harness.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I prefer pairwise comparisons over rubric scorecards?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Pairwise works best when iterating rapidly and the goal is "better than baseline" rather than a specific threshold.</li>
<li dir="auto">Scorecards work better when you need hard pass/fail criteria, want to track specific dimensions over time, or need to gate a release on a minimum score.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pairwise results aggregate into win-rates or Elo ratings, which are useful for comparing prompt versions or model checkpoints.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: relative preference is cognitively easier for both humans and models than assigning an absolute score, so pairwise tends to produce more consistent verdicts on subjective quality.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the most dangerous pitfalls when using LLM-as-a-judge in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Verbosity bias: judges prefer longer answers even when shorter ones are correct. Mitigate with a conciseness dimension and length caps.</li>
<li dir="auto">Position bias: in pairwise, judges favor whichever answer appears first. Always randomize A/B order.</li>
<li dir="auto">Hidden coupling: using the same model as judge and candidate inflates scores. Use a different judge model.</li>
<li dir="auto">Calibration drift: judge behavior shifts as the underlying model is updated. Maintain a gold dataset and re-run calibration periodically.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: these biases are systematic, not random — they silently corrupt your eval signal and can cause you to ship regressions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does low statistical power mean?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A real effect often fails to reach the decision threshold, producing a false negative. If an underpowered result is significant, its estimate may be unusually large because only extreme samples crossed the threshold; confirm both practical size and uncertainty.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Online Evaluation and AB Tests.md" class="internal-link">Online Evaluation and AB Tests</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must analysis follow the assignment unit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Randomization makes assigned units independent across variants. Repeated events within one unit remain correlated, so counting them as independent understates uncertainty and can create a confident-looking result from little independent evidence.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Online Evaluation and AB Tests.md" class="internal-link">Online Evaluation and AB Tests</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why keep assignment and exposure separate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Assignment records intent; exposure records that the variant could affect behavior. Joining outcomes to actual exposure avoids attributing an effect to users who qualified but never reached the changed surface.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Online Evaluation and AB Tests.md" class="internal-link">Online Evaluation and AB Tests</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are relative regression thresholds preferable to absolute quality targets for release gates?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Absolute thresholds are brittle across data changes, model updates, and workload shifts</li>
<li dir="auto">A threshold set during initial launch becomes meaningless after the data doubles or input distribution shifts</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds (no more than N% regression from baseline) adapt automatically because the baseline tracks the current system state</li>
<li dir="auto">They prevent the failure mode where a team sets an ambitious absolute target, cannot reach it, and disables the gate entirely</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds do require a consistent baseline measurement maintained across releases, which adds CI/CD complexity — but that cost is far lower than the risk of shipping regressions absolute thresholds can't catch after the first data evolution<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Building an Evaluation Set.md" class="internal-link">Building an Evaluation Set</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team invest in a human-annotated golden set versus relying on synthetic generation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Synthetic generation bootstraps evaluation quickly and covers breadth at low cost</li>
<li dir="auto">LLM-generated inputs cluster around patterns the model finds easy to generate, missing adversarial cases, ambiguous inputs, and domain edge cases</li>
<li dir="auto">A golden set is worth the investment when the system serves high-stakes decisions (medical, legal, financial) where evaluation failures have direct business or safety impact</li>
<li dir="auto">Golden sets also serve as regression gates — known past failures are captured permanently, preventing recurrence (see <a class="internal-link" href="Golden Test Set and Regression Runs">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a>)</li>
<li dir="auto">In practice, combine both: synthetic for broad coverage, golden for regression gating on known failure modes</li>
<li dir="auto">Golden sets do cost annotator time (typically 2-4 hours per 100 examples) and ongoing maintenance as the data evolves — invest proportionally to the cost of an undetected evaluation failure in your domain<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Building an Evaluation Set.md" class="internal-link">Building an Evaluation Set</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Harness Engineering</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does MCP use a host-client-server architecture instead of letting the LLM talk to servers directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The host mediates all communication between the LLM and MCP servers. This is a deliberate security boundary — the host can enforce policies (which tools the model can call, which resources it can read), require user approval for sensitive actions, and aggregate capabilities from multiple servers without any server being aware of the others. If the LLM talked to servers directly, there would be no central point to enforce access control, log actions, or prevent a compromised server from manipulating the model. The client-per-server model also isolates failures — one crashed server does not affect others.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose function calling over MCP even for a shared tool?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the tool involves sensitive business logic that should not be externalized (e.g., pricing calculations, compliance checks), when you need tight coupling between the tool and the application's internal state (e.g., in-memory session data), or when the overhead of running a separate server process is not justified. Function calling is also simpler to debug — the tool definition and implementation live in the same codebase. MCP's value is reusability across clients, so if only one client will ever use the tool, function calling is the better choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes MCP servers harder to secure than traditional REST APIs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three factors compound. First, tool descriptions are untrusted input that directly influences LLM behavior — a vector that traditional APIs do not have. Second, MCP servers often get broad access (database connections, file system access, API keys) because they need to fulfill diverse model requests, violating least-privilege by default. Third, the protocol lacks built-in permission scoping — OAuth authenticates and authorizes the client's access to the server, but does not restrict which specific tools or operations the client can invoke. Traditional REST APIs typically have endpoint-level authorization, rate limiting, and input validation as standard practice. MCP servers need all of these but the ecosystem is young enough that many servers ship without them.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is tool design often more impactful than prompt engineering in agentic systems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">In a single LLM call, the prompt is the entire interface — prompt quality is everything</li>
<li dir="auto">In an agentic system, the model interacts with tools across multiple loop iterations — each tool call is a decision point where errors compound</li>
<li dir="auto">Wrong tool selection, malformed arguments, or unhelpful error messages cascade across steps</li>
<li dir="auto">Anthropic's SWE-bench team spent more time improving tool interfaces than prompts, because tool quality directly determined task success rate</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: tool design effort is amortized across all agent runs; prompt tweaks are fragile and session-specific<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How would you decide between one broad tool and many narrow tools?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Narrow tools reduce parameter count and ambiguity — fewer hallucinated arguments per call</li>
<li dir="auto">Too many tools degrades selection accuracy — MCPGauge measured 9.5% accuracy drop from irrelevant tool presence alone</li>
<li dir="auto">Split when a tool serves genuinely different use cases needing different descriptions and parameters</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep together when operations share context and the model would naturally chain them</li>
<li dir="auto">As tool count grows and selection confusion appears, apply routing or tool filtering to keep the active set focused</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: per-call reliability (narrow) versus selection accuracy (fewer options)<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a tool safe for caching in an agent loop versus unsafe?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Cache-safe: read-only (no side effects), deterministic output for same inputs within TTL, acceptable staleness</li>
<li dir="auto">Examples: database lookups, weather API calls, search queries — all safe with short TTLs</li>
<li dir="auto">Cache-unsafe: mutates state (create/update/delete), result depends on rapidly changing context, caching would mask failures</li>
<li dir="auto">Critical failure mode: caching a write operation returns cached "success" without executing — silent data inconsistency</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: cache hit rate and latency savings versus freshness and correctness guarantees<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is harness engineering, and how does it differ from context engineering?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Harness engineering designs everything the model acts <em>through</em>: the tool surface (which tools exist, how they're named and scoped), the wiring protocol (MCP), and the execution environment (sandboxes, permissions, approval boundaries)</li>
<li dir="auto">Context engineering decides what the model <em>sees</em> in its window; harness engineering decides what it <em>can do</em>; loop engineering decides how it iterates over time</li>
<li dir="auto">They intersect — tool schemas consume context budget, tool results feed the loop — but the design questions differ: capability and safety boundaries versus signal selection and ordering<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Harness Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Harness Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the runtime, not the prompt, the right place to enforce what an agent may do?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The model never executes tools directly — it only emits structured calls; the runtime executes, so controls placed there cannot be talked around</li>
<li dir="auto">Prompt-level rules fail under prompt injection or poisoned tool descriptions; code-level sandboxes, permission gates, and human-approval boundaries hold regardless of what enters the context</li>
<li dir="auto">Practical layering: sandbox execution to bound blast radius, auto-approve read-only calls, gate state-mutating ones by policy, require a human for irreversible actions<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Harness Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Harness Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does harness quality deserve as much investment as prompt quality for agent reliability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Agents hit the harness on every loop iteration, so tool-surface flaws compound: one ambiguous name or vague error causes a wrong turn that later steps build on</li>
<li dir="auto">Precise contracts and structured errors let the model self-correct; sloppy ones produce failures that masquerade as model failures</li>
<li dir="auto">Harness fixes amortize across every run and every agent sharing the surface; prompt tweaks are flow-specific and fragile<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Harness Engineering/Harness Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Harness Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Loop Engineering</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the ReAct pattern outperform chain-of-thought reasoning alone for tasks requiring external knowledge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Chain-of-thought generates reasoning traces but has no mechanism to verify claims against external reality. When the model encounters a factual question it must rely on parametric memory, which produces confident but fabricated answers. ReAct interleaves reasoning with tool calls — the model can search, look up, or compute before continuing its chain. This grounds each step in real data, cutting hallucination. The original paper showed this on HotpotQA: CoT alone frequently hallucinated intermediate facts, while ReAct retrieved them. The tradeoff is latency and cost — each tool call adds a round trip and tokens.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the three most critical production safeguards for an agent loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ol>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Iteration cap</strong> — prevents infinite loops and cost runaway. Without a hard limit a confused agent loops indefinitely. Set per-request, not globally. 2. <strong>Token budget tracking</strong> — each iteration grows the context. Track cumulative tokens and terminate or summarize before hitting the window limit. Without this the model silently loses earlier context and quality degrades. 3. <strong>Tool call validation</strong> — validate function names and arguments against the schema before execution. A hallucinated tool call that passes through can trigger unintended side effects — database writes, payments, external API calls. Return errors to the model so it can self-correct.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></li>
</ol>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a simple prompt chain preferable to an agent loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the task decomposes into a fixed, predictable sequence of steps. A prompt chain — step A then validate then step B then validate then step C — is cheaper, faster, more debuggable, and produces more consistent results. The agent loop adds value only when you cannot predict the steps in advance: the model must decide dynamically which tools to call and in what order based on intermediate results. Most production systems that call themselves agents are actually prompt chains, and that is the right choice for the majority of use cases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does loop engineering add on top of a model with tools, and why is it its own discipline?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A model with tools is still one call; the loop is the runtime that iterates it: observe → decide → act, repeated until a stop condition</li>
<li dir="auto">Because each iteration conditions on the last, errors compound — the loop design (caps, gates, feedback) determines whether mistakes stay small or cascade</li>
<li dir="auto">It owns the time dimension the other rungs lack: prompt engineering shapes one instruction, context engineering what the model sees, harness engineering what it can do; the loop shapes behavior over many turns</li>
<li dir="auto">Concretely it decides: when to stop (iteration/token/cost budgets), how to verify progress (gates, self-checks, ground truth), and when to escalate to a human<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Loop Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Loop Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main ways to bound a loop, and why are prompt-level stop instructions not enough?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Hard iteration caps per request, cumulative token/cost budgets with early termination, and checkable stop criteria (no tool calls, tests pass, valid artifact)</li>
<li dir="auto">Prompt instructions ("stop if repeating yourself") reduce waste but are advisory — the model can ignore them, so they complement rather than replace hard limits enforced by the runtime</li>
<li dir="auto">Define fallback behavior at the cap upfront: return best partial result with a warning, or escalate to a human — never silently truncate</li>
<li dir="auto">The motivation is compounding error and cost runaway: a documented unbounded run burned 9.7M tokens across 369 repeated tool calls without converging (see <a class="internal-link" href="Agent Loop">Agent Loop</a>)<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Loop Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Loop Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do loop engineering and context engineering divide the work of managing a long run's history?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Context engineering decides WHAT belongs in the window: selection, ordering, structure, what a summary must preserve</li>
<li dir="auto">Loop engineering decides WHEN to act: compaction cadence tied to the token budget, when to offload artifacts to external storage, when to terminate instead of compact</li>
<li dir="auto">The loop makes it a runtime problem — history grows every iteration, so thresholds and triggers must be part of the loop design, not a one-time assembly choice</li>
<li dir="auto">Durable state (plan files, scratchpads re-read each turn) bridges the two: it survives compaction and keeps the loop anchored to the goal<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Loop Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Loop Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is multi-agent coordination justified over a single agent with more tools?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Justified under three conditions: context pollution (subtask degrades main agent reasoning), parallelization (independent paths need concurrent execution), specialization (20+ tools degrade selection accuracy, or conflicting behavioral modes needed)</li>
<li dir="auto">If none apply, single agent wins: 3–10× fewer tokens, lower latency, single linear trace for debugging</li>
<li dir="auto">Many teams investing months in multi-agent discover equivalent results from better prompting on one agent</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: multi-agent buys context isolation and parallelism at the cost of coordination overhead and debugging complexity<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does context-centric decomposition outperform problem-centric decomposition?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Problem-centric (code agent + test agent + review agent) forces constant coordination — each agent needs context from the others, creating lossy handoffs</li>
<li dir="auto">Context-centric splits along natural context boundaries — agent handling a feature also handles its tests because it already has the context</li>
<li dir="auto">Introduce a new agent only when context genuinely cannot fit in one window</li>
<li dir="auto">Reduces handoff count, cuts token overhead, prevents compounding information loss at each transfer</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: context-centric may produce broader agents (more tools per agent), but avoids the "telephone game" of multi-hop handoffs<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes multi-agent failures harder to diagnose than single-agent failures?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Semantic opacity: natural language errors pass as "valid" data between agents — no schema violations, no exceptions raised. A hallucinated fact from Agent A becomes trusted input for Agent B</li>
<li dir="auto">Non-linear traces: multiple interleaving reasoning chains with handoffs instead of one sequential trace, making root cause analysis harder</li>
<li dir="auto">Emergent behavior: agent interactions produce outcomes no single agent's instructions predict</li>
<li dir="auto">Known anti-pattern: two agents entering a politeness loop, each thanking the other, consuming budget without task progress — correct behavior per agent, catastrophic in combination</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: multi-agent gains specialization but loses the single-trace debuggability that makes single-agent failures straightforward to fix<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Loop Engineering/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Prompt Engineering</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is automated prompt optimization worth the setup cost?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It is worth it when prompt quality directly impacts key metrics and manual iteration is too slow.</li>
<li dir="auto">You need repeatable optimization across many tasks or datasets, not one-off prompt crafting.</li>
<li dir="auto">You have an evaluation loop (datasets, acceptance metrics, or human labels) to score changes objectively.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without measurement infrastructure, automation usually adds complexity without reliable gains.<br />
Why this matters: the optimization loop only pays off when evaluation discipline exists.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Automated Prompt Optimization.md" class="internal-link">Automated Prompt Optimization</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is PAL's key insight compared to chain-of-thought prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">PAL moves intermediate reasoning from free-form text to executable code.</li>
<li dir="auto">The interpreter, not the language model, performs deterministic computation.</li>
<li dir="auto">This reduces arithmetic/symbolic errors common in text-only reasoning traces.</li>
<li dir="auto">It naturally connects to tool-use agents that route subtasks to calculators, Python, or external systems.<br />
Why this matters: it separates language understanding from computation for better reliability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Automated Prompt Optimization.md" class="internal-link">Automated Prompt Optimization</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you start with zero-shot versus few-shot?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start zero-shot for simple, well-specified tasks (basic classification, extraction, rewrite).</li>
<li dir="auto">Move to one-shot/few-shot when output format is inconsistent or class boundaries are ambiguous.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use one-shot first for lightweight steering, then increase shots only if failure patterns persist.<br />
Why this matters: it minimizes prompt complexity and token cost while improving reliability only where needed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a good few-shot example set?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Consistent input and output structure.</li>
<li dir="auto">Coverage of real label space and edge cases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Examples representative of production distribution, not synthetic easy cases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Minimal but sufficient count (often 1-5) to show mapping clearly.<br />
Why this matters: demonstration quality usually matters more than raw quantity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main failure modes of few-shot prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Breakdown on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning.</li>
<li dir="auto">Sensitivity to example order and formatting drift.</li>
<li dir="auto">Context-window pressure from too many demonstrations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Poor transfer when task needs external facts not present in model knowledge.<br />
Why this matters: these limits tell you when to pivot to other techniques instead of adding more shots.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose prompt chaining instead of a single large prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Choose chaining when the task has clear stages with different goals.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use chaining when intermediate validation reduces business or safety risk.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer chaining when you need step-level observability for debugging.</li>
<li dir="auto">Accept the latency and token overhead when reliability matters more than speed.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use a single prompt for simple, low-risk tasks with stable behavior.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the main risk of generated knowledge prompting, and how do you mitigate it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The generated background facts can be wrong or fabricated.</li>
<li dir="auto">Treat generated facts as untrusted intermediate context.</li>
<li dir="auto">Constrain the final prompt to use only explicit generated facts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Validate critical claims against trusted context before final output.</li>
<li dir="auto">Add abstain or reject behavior when facts are inconsistent or low confidence.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a practical meta prompting workflow for improving a weak prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Collect real failures and cluster them by error type.</li>
<li dir="auto">Ask the model to critique the current prompt against those failures.</li>
<li dir="auto">Request a revised prompt with explicit constraints and output schema.</li>
<li dir="auto">Evaluate on a held-out test set, not only the examples used for revision.</li>
<li dir="auto">Version prompts and keep rollback criteria if quality regresses.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do prompt anatomy and model settings have to be designed together?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prompt text defines intent and constraints, settings define sampling behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">A precise prompt can still fail with overly random settings.</li>
<li dir="auto">Conservative settings can still produce poor output if instructions are ambiguous.</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliable systems tune both and evaluate with task-specific metrics.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer few-shot prompting over pure instruction prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When output format is strict or hard to describe in words.</li>
<li dir="auto">When label boundaries are subtle and examples clarify decision edges.</li>
<li dir="auto">When consistency matters more than novelty.</li>
<li dir="auto">Start with minimal examples, then add edge cases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How would you debug a prompt that is accurate but too verbose and expensive?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Tighten output indicator with length limits and schema.</li>
<li dir="auto">Lower <code>max tokens</code> and add stop sequences.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep <code>temperature</code> low for deterministic concise tasks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Evaluate token usage and failure rate after each change.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Prompt Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Chain-of-Thought usually help, and when can it hurt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Helps on multi-step arithmetic, logic, and planning where intermediate state tracking matters.</li>
<li dir="auto">Helps when tasks are decomposable into small correct sub-steps.</li>
<li dir="auto">Can hurt on simple factual queries by adding unnecessary tokens and extra room for drift.</li>
<li dir="auto">Can also hurt if the first bad assumption propagates through a single chain.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this tests whether the candidate understands mechanism-level fit, not just a slogan like "CoT is always better".<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain the cost vs. accuracy tradeoff in self-consistency.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Accuracy often improves because multiple sampled chains reduce single-path bias.</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost and latency increase roughly with sample count N.</li>
<li dir="auto">Diminishing returns appear after a small N; tune N against SLA and budget.</li>
<li dir="auto">Works best when there is a clear way to compare or vote on final answers.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this checks practical engineering judgment under production constraints.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Tree of Thoughts justified over CoT or self-consistency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use ToT when success requires explicit exploration of alternatives and backtracking.</li>
<li dir="auto">Typical cases: planning, combinatorial puzzles, multi-constraint decision paths.</li>
<li dir="auto">Not justified for easy tasks where CoT already reaches target quality.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose search strategy (BFS/DFS/beam) based on branching factor, depth, and compute budget.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this evaluates whether the candidate can map algorithmic search cost to task difficulty.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompt Engineering/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Safety</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the minimum useful guardrail set for a production LLM application?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">(1) Allowlisted tools/actions only, (2) strict output schema validation, (3) PII scanning on outputs, (4) prompt injection detection on inputs, (5) an abstention/escalation path for uncertainty. These five controls catch the most common failure modes at low cost. Add content safety classifiers and human-in-the-loop for high-risk domains.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Guardrails.md" class="internal-link">Guardrails</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you test guardrails?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Build a red-team test suite: injection attempts ("ignore all previous instructions"), jailbreaks ("you are DAN"), data exfiltration attempts, and out-of-scope requests. Run the suite on every model or prompt change. Track pass rates over time — a regression in the red-team suite is a deployment blocker.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Guardrails.md" class="internal-link">Guardrails</a></em></p>
