</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can RAG-grounded systems still hallucinate significantly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does RLHF increase hallucination risk while perceived quality improves?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you separate retrieval failure from generation hallucination in a RAG pipeline?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is prompt injection fundamentally harder to prevent than SQL injection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Excessive Agency (LLM06) compound with Prompt Injection (LLM01)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should system prompts be treated as public rather than secret?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do security failures and reliability failures differ, and why does the distinction matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Security failures (<a class="internal-link" href="OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM" aria-label="OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM" data-tooltip-position="top">OWASP Top 10</a>) have an adversary crafting input — prompt injection, data exfiltration, excessive agency; they are bounded by controls, isolation, and least privilege</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliability failures (<a class="internal-link" href="Hallucinations">Hallucinations</a>) have no attacker — the model produces confident but unsupported output because it optimizes likelihood, not truth; they are bounded by grounding, verification, and evaluation</li>
<li dir="auto">The mitigations barely overlap: a perfect content filter does nothing for hallucination, and RAG grounding does nothing for an injection attack — a complete system needs both tracks<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Safety.md" class="internal-link">Safety</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are prompt-level guardrails insufficient on their own?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A model instruction is advisory: it can be overridden by a higher-priority injected instruction or a poisoned tool/retrieved description (the instruction-vs-data confusion at the heart of prompt injection)</li>
<li dir="auto">Durable controls are structural and live outside the model — input validation, content isolation, permission gates, sandboxed execution, and output checking in code/infrastructure</li>
<li dir="auto">Defense in depth: assume any single layer can be bypassed, so critical actions are gated where the model cannot talk its way past them (see <a class="internal-link" href="Harness Engineering">Harness Engineering</a>)<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Safety.md" class="internal-link">Safety</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the safety concern map onto the four steering rungs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prompt/Context: separate trusted instructions from untrusted data to blunt injection; ground with <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering/RAG/RAG" data-tooltip-position="top">RAG</a> to reduce hallucination</li>
<li dir="auto">Harness: enforce sandboxing, least privilege, and permission gates in code, not the prompt</li>
<li dir="auto">Loop: place human-approval boundaries around irreversible or low-confidence actions</li>
<li dir="auto">Safety is orthogonal — it is designed into every rung and measured by <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">Evaluation</a>, not handled at one step<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Safety/Safety.md" class="internal-link">Safety</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-machine-learning">Machine Learning</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between data drift and concept drift?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Data drift: the input distribution P(X) changes (users ask different questions, new product categories appear). The model's learned relationship P(Y|X) may still be valid.<br />
Concept drift: the relationship P(Y|X) changes (what constitutes spam evolves, fraud patterns shift). The model's learned relationship is no longer valid — retraining is required.<br />
Cost of confusing them: retraining on new data for concept drift is necessary; retraining for benign data drift wastes resources and may reduce performance on the original distribution.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Data Drift.md" class="internal-link">Data Drift</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect drift when labels are delayed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use proxy metrics: escalation rate, user complaints, re-contact rate, or model confidence distributions. Monitor input feature distributions (PSI, KS test) as an early warning signal. When labels arrive, compute actual performance metrics and compare to baseline.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Data Drift.md" class="internal-link">Data Drift</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should an ML pipeline be designed to ship weekly batch churn scoring now while preserving a path to real-time scoring later?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start with batch to ship value, but define a stable feature contract and a single preprocessing implementation shared by batch and online</li>
<li dir="auto">Store features and predictions with versioned schema so you can backtest and replay later</li>
<li dir="auto">Use a model registry with stage promotion and rollback, and keep training code runnable in CI</li>
<li dir="auto">Plan the online boundary now: which features are available at request time and which require async enrichment</li>
<li dir="auto">Add monitoring from day one so you have drift and label delay visibility before moving to real time<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What should be checked first when a binary classifier shows 98 percent accuracy but support tickets rise, and which metric should be optimized next?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Check class balance and the confusion matrix; high accuracy can hide poor recall on the minority class</li>
<li dir="auto">Inspect label quality and leakage sources, especially time based joins and post event signals</li>
<li dir="auto">Pick metrics based on cost: optimize precision if false positives are expensive, recall if misses are expensive, or use PR AUC for heavy imbalance</li>
<li dir="auto">Tune the decision threshold using a cost curve or expected value, not the default 0.5</li>
<li dir="auto">Run slice based evaluation to find the segments where the model fails and decide whether to collect more data or add features<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What tradeoffs and rollout plan are appropriate when the best model exceeds a strict API latency budget?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Measure end to end latency budget including preprocessing, network, and tail latencies, then decide if you can meet SLO with scaling or caching</li>
<li dir="auto">Consider a smaller model, distillation, quantization, or a two stage setup where a cheap model gates the expensive one</li>
<li dir="auto">Use canary or A B rollout with guardrails on latency and key business metrics, plus automated rollback</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep a safe fallback decision path, such as a baseline model or rules, for overload and error conditions</li>
<li dir="auto">Align evaluation with production: test on representative traffic and monitor training serving skew after launch<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you fine-tune a small model instead of prompting an LLM for an NLP task?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tune when you have labeled training data and the task is well-defined (classification, NER, sentiment).</li>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tuned small models (BERT, DistilBERT) run at millisecond latency on-device vs seconds for LLM API calls.</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-request cost is near zero for on-device inference vs $0.01–$0.10+ per LLM call at scale.</li>
<li dir="auto">LLMs win when the task is complex (multi-step reasoning, summarization), training data is scarce, or rapid iteration matters more than unit cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tuning costs you labeled data and training effort upfront, then pays it back as millisecond latency and near-zero per-request cost at scale; prototype with an LLM, then check whether production volume justifies the fine-tune.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing.md" class="internal-link">Natural Language Processing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do multilingual NLP models underperform monolingual ones, and when is that acceptable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models split their capacity across 100+ languages, so per-language representation quality is lower.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monolingual models concentrate all parameters on one language, achieving higher accuracy on that language's benchmarks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models are acceptable when you need coverage across many languages and per-language accuracy can be slightly lower.</li>
<li dir="auto">For high-stakes tasks (medical NER, legal classification), the accuracy gap may be unacceptable — use language-specific models.</li>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models trade per-language accuracy for breadth of coverage; accept that when you serve many languages on limited ML infrastructure, but validate explicitly on the languages you actually care about.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing.md" class="internal-link">Natural Language Processing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why start every new AI feature in Shadow Mode?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Shadow Mode lets you validate accuracy on real production data without any risk of automated errors. You measure false positive rate, edge cases, and distribution shift before giving the model any authority. Skipping Shadow Mode means discovering failures in production where they have real consequences.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Spectrum Of Automations.md" class="internal-link">Spectrum Of Automations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What signals indicate it is safe to move from Partial to Full Automation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Precision on automated cases is consistently above your acceptable error threshold across multiple weeks. Monitoring dashboards show stable metrics with no degradation. The cost of errors is low enough that automated mistakes are recoverable. You have alerting in place to detect metric drift quickly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Spectrum Of Automations.md" class="internal-link">Spectrum Of Automations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Evaluation</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a model with high ROC-AUC still produce unusable probabilities?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">ROC-AUC measures ranking — whether positives score above negatives — and is invariant to any monotonic transform of the scores</li>
<li dir="auto">That invariance means the absolute probability values can be arbitrarily distorted (all squashed near 0.9, say) without changing AUC at all</li>
<li dir="auto">Downstream logic that compares <code>p</code> to a threshold or computes <code>p × value</code> then makes wrong decisions despite the perfect ranking</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is to measure calibration separately (reliability diagram, Brier, ECE) and apply post-hoc calibration; AUC will never surface the problem<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does calibration not matter, and when is it essential?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It does not matter when only the ranking or the top-k is used: search results, recommendation shortlists, lead prioritization — there a strong ROC-AUC or PR-AUC is sufficient</li>
<li dir="auto">It is essential when the probability feeds a decision: expected-value thresholds, cost-sensitive actions, abstention/escalation gates, risk scores shown to humans, or probability averaging across an ensemble</li>
<li dir="auto">It is also essential when probabilities from different models or time periods are compared, since miscalibration makes them non-comparable</li>
<li dir="auto">Rule of thumb: if a human or a formula reads the number, calibrate it; if only the order is consumed, you may not need to<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is temperature scaling the default calibration method for neural networks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Modern deep nets are systematically overconfident, and the miscalibration is largely a global scaling of the logits rather than a per-class distortion</li>
<li dir="auto">Temperature scaling fits a single scalar T that divides the logits before softmax, which is exactly the right shape for that global overconfidence</li>
<li dir="auto">Because it preserves the argmax, accuracy is unchanged — you fix probabilities without touching the model's decisions</li>
<li dir="auto">It needs only a small held-out set and one parameter, so it rarely overfits; the limitation is that it cannot repair calibration that varies by region or class<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you optimize precision vs recall, and how do you operationalize the choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Optimize precision when false positives are expensive: blocking legitimate users (and churning them), flooding a manual review queue with false alarms, or triggering expensive downstream actions (automated account lockouts). Optimize recall when misses are dangerous: fraud slipping through, safety violations in content moderation (regulatory exposure), or medical screening (missed diagnosis). Operationalize by setting a hard constraint on the priority metric (e.g., "recall ≥ 0.95"), then maximizing the other. Freeze the threshold and monitor both metrics daily with alerting on ≥5% drift.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Classification Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Classification Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you pick a classification threshold in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ol>
<li dir="auto">Define the business constraint: "recall must be ≥ 0.95" or "FP rate must be ≤ 0.02." 2. Plot the precision-recall curve on a held-out validation set. 3. Find the threshold that satisfies your constraint while maximizing the complementary metric. 4. Validate on a separate golden test set (not the validation set used for selection). 5. Freeze the threshold in production config (not hardcoded). 6. Set up monitoring: if the metric drops ≥5% on weekly golden-set runs, trigger re-evaluation. A static threshold degrades as the data distribution shifts, so schedule quarterly threshold reviews or move to dynamic thresholding with confidence calibration.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Classification Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Classification Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ol>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you distrust a single evaluation metric?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the metric does not encode the cost asymmetry of the decision, for example accuracy on imbalanced classes</li>
<li dir="auto">When the test set does not represent production traffic, for example a random split on time-dependent data</li>
<li dir="auto">When the metric looks good overall but fails on critical slices or cohorts</li>
<li dir="auto">When the metric is a ranking metric but you need calibrated probabilities for downstream logic<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the offline–online gap the central risk in ML evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Offline metrics are computed on a frozen sample; production traffic shifts in distribution, user behavior, and edge-case mix that the sample never captured</li>
<li dir="auto">A change can improve the offline number while leaving real outcomes flat — or reverse them — because the offline set rewards patterns that do not generalize</li>
<li dir="auto">Leakage, non-representative splits, and metric-proxy mismatch all widen the gap silently</li>
<li dir="auto">The mitigation is layered: offline metrics as a release gate, production monitoring to catch drift, and controlled experiments to confirm a real effect before trusting it<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does ROC-AUC measure?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It measures ranking quality across thresholds.</li>
<li dir="auto">A higher ROC-AUC means positives usually get higher scores than negatives.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I use PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use PR-AUC when positives are rare, like fraud or anomaly detection.</li>
<li dir="auto">PR-AUC shows how precision changes as recall increases, which is key when false positives are costly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can high accuracy be misleading on imbalanced data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">If negatives dominate, a model can predict mostly negatives and still get high accuracy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Check PR-AUC, precision, and recall to understand real positive-class performance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-tooling">Tooling</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do hierarchical instruction files often outperform a single giant root file?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Rules stay close to the code they govern, reducing ambiguity in mixed-language or mixed-architecture repos</li>
<li dir="auto">Subdirectory instructions can refine local constraints without polluting global context</li>
<li dir="auto">Smaller scoped files usually improve relevance-to-token ratio in the agent prompt</li>
<li dir="auto">The tradeoff is governance complexity: teams need clear precedence and periodic cleanup<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the fastest way to detect that an instruction file is harming agent quality?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Repeated command failures from outdated scripts or wrong paths</li>
<li dir="auto">Inconsistent code style across tasks despite explicit rules</li>
<li dir="auto">Frequent edits that violate architecture constraints the file claims to enforce</li>
<li dir="auto">Rapid fix is to trim to high-signal rules, remove contradictions, then re-add specifics incrementally<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team keep instructions minimal and rely more on repository inspection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Early prototype phases where architecture and conventions are changing weekly</li>
<li dir="auto">Small codebases where implicit conventions are obvious from a few files</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams that cannot maintain rule quality yet; stale detail is worse than concise accurate guidance</li>
<li dir="auto">As complexity grows, move from minimal to layered instructions before inconsistency becomes expensive<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is an agentic coding tool not just "better autocomplete"?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Autocomplete predicts local tokens, while coding agents execute multi-step plans across files and tools</li>
<li dir="auto">Agents can run verification commands and revise output based on failures, which autocomplete does not do autonomously</li>
<li dir="auto">The mechanism changes risk: agent output must be governed with approvals, tests, and repository rules</li>
<li dir="auto">Net benefit comes from closed-loop execution, not from raw text quality alone<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team prefer terminal agents over IDE agents?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefer terminal agents when workflows are command-heavy, scriptable, and CI-first</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer IDE agents when developers rely on interactive navigation, visual diffing, and editor-native refactors</li>
<li dir="auto">The deciding factor is operational fit with current engineering process, not vendor marketing claims</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams can mix both if shared instruction files and validation gates keep behavior consistent<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What controls reduce production risk when adopting coding agents?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Repository instruction files to encode architecture and testing constraints</li>
<li dir="auto">Hooks and policy checks to block unsafe operations automatically</li>
<li dir="auto">Bounded execution: cost limits, step limits, and explicit approval points</li>
<li dir="auto">Mandatory verification (build, tests, lint) before merge, regardless of which model produced the patch<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should destructive-operation controls live in PreToolUse rather than PostToolUse?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design hook pipelines that improve quality without making the agent unusably slow?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use an LLM-based hook instead of a deterministic script?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does MCP reduce integration complexity for agent tooling ecosystems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without a shared protocol, each client-service pair needs custom integration code, creating N x M scaling</li>
<li dir="auto">MCP changes this to N + M: each client implements MCP once, each service exposes one MCP server</li>
<li dir="auto">The shared primitives (tools, resources, prompts) standardize capability discovery and invocation</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: protocol standardization simplifies interoperability but adds runtime/server management overhead<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Plugins.md" class="internal-link">Plugins</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team intentionally limit plugin count even if more integrations are available?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When accuracy and latency degrade from too many tool schemas in context</li>
<li dir="auto">When the security team cannot properly review or monitor additional server dependencies</li>
<li dir="auto">When task scope is narrow and extra plugins create more selection noise than value</li>
<li dir="auto">Good practice is progressive enablement: start minimal, add only tools that measurably improve outcomes<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Plugins.md" class="internal-link">Plugins</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do skills usually improve agent reliability more than repeating instructions in every prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Skills are persistent and reusable, so important constraints are present every time instead of being forgotten in ad-hoc prompts</li>
<li dir="auto">They standardize behavior across contributors and sessions, reducing output variance</li>
<li dir="auto">Structured frontmatter plus explicit usage guidance improves skill selection and timing</li>
<li dir="auto">Skills are version-controlled artifacts, so teams can review and evolve them like code<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Skills.md" class="internal-link">Skills</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose project-scoped skills over user-global skills?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Choose project scope when conventions are repository-specific and must be shared by all contributors</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose project scope when CI, linters, or architecture rules are tightly coupled to that codebase</li>
<li dir="auto">Use user-global skills for personal productivity preferences that should not override team policy</li>
<li dir="auto">If a rule would surprise teammates in a PR review, it should be project-scoped, not global<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Skills.md" class="internal-link">Skills</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between a terminal-first coding agent and an IDE-first coding agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start with team workflow: terminal-first fits script-heavy and CI-centric teams, IDE-first fits interactive editing-heavy teams</li>
<li dir="auto">Compare observability: terminal-first often exposes every command clearly, while IDE-first may optimize UX but hide low-level steps</li>
<li dir="auto">Validate integration needs: if your process depends on PR workflows, issue trackers, and editor navigation, IDE integration can reduce friction</li>
<li dir="auto">Check policy controls: hooks, rules files, and approval gates matter more than UI style in regulated or production-sensitive environments<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are agent instructions and hooks more important than model quality in long-running repos?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Instructions encode project constraints (architecture, naming, testing expectations) that the base model does not know</li>
<li dir="auto">Hooks create automatic guardrails, catching policy violations before merge</li>
<li dir="auto">Better models can still make wrong local decisions without repo-specific constraints</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliable behavior over months depends on repeatable controls, not one-off prompt quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the practical difference between a coding agent and a code review agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Coding agents optimize change creation: plan, edit, run checks, iterate</li>
<li dir="auto">Review agents optimize change evaluation: identify risk, gaps, regressions, and missing tests</li>
<li dir="auto">They complement each other: one increases delivery speed, the other protects quality and maintainability</li>
<li dir="auto">Mature teams use both to keep throughput high without lowering merge standards<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="cloud">Cloud</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the key operational difference between IaaS and PaaS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">With IaaS you manage the OS, runtime, and middleware — you patch the OS, configure security groups, and handle scaling policies. With PaaS the provider manages all of that; you only deploy application code and data. The tradeoff: IaaS gives full control (custom OS, GPU, specific runtime versions); PaaS eliminates operational overhead at the cost of flexibility. Start with PaaS for new web applications; move to IaaS only when PaaS cannot meet your requirements.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Cloud/IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, CaaS.md" class="internal-link">IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, CaaS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does CaaS fit between IaaS and PaaS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">CaaS (e.g., AKS, EKS) sits between them: you manage container images and deployments, but the provider manages the Kubernetes control plane and underlying OS. It gives more control than PaaS (you define your own container images, resource limits, and networking) while eliminating the Kubernetes control plane management burden of IaaS. Use CaaS when you need container portability or multi-service orchestration that PaaS cannot provide.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Cloud/IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, CaaS.md" class="internal-link">IaaS, PaaS, SaaS, CaaS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose a compute abstraction — VMs, containers, or serverless?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Serverless functions minimize ops for spiky, event-driven work; billing usually combines request count with execution duration and allocated resources, while cold starts, duration limits, and runtime constraints reduce control</li>
<li dir="auto">Containers fit custom runtimes and long-lived services, but the operating cost depends on the platform: a managed serverless container service can own orchestration and scale-to-zero mechanics, while managed Kubernetes still leaves cluster policy, upgrades, capacity, and workload operations to the platform team</li>
<li dir="auto">VMs are the escape hatch for legacy workloads, specialized OS/kernel needs, or lift-and-shift: most control, most ops</li>
<li dir="auto">Decide by workload shape, runtime limits, scaling model, and which layer the team is prepared to operate; the container image alone does not imply Kubernetes or VM-level operations<br />
<em><a href="Home/Cloud/Cloud.md" class="internal-link">Cloud</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="computer-science">Computer Science</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Big O drop constant factors and lower-order terms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because it describes growth as <code>n → ∞</code>, where the fastest-growing term dominates everything else and the machine-specific constants wash out. <code>n² + 100n + 500</code> is <code>O(n²)</code>: past some size the <code>n²</code> term buries the rest. This makes the notation a hardware-independent way to compare <em>scaling</em>, at the deliberate cost of saying nothing about behaviour at small <code>n</code>, where the dropped constants are exactly what decides the winner.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Big O Notation.md" class="internal-link">Big O Notation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between average-case and amortised complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Average case is the expected cost of one operation over a distribution of <em>inputs</em> — a hash lookup is <code>O(1)</code> average because random keys spread across buckets. Amortised is the cost of one operation averaged over a <em>sequence</em> of operations on the same structure — a dynamic-array append is <code>O(1)</code> amortised because the occasional <code>O(n)</code> resize is paid for by the many cheap appends around it. One averages over inputs, the other over a run of operations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Big O Notation.md" class="internal-link">Big O Notation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why include the call stack in space complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because recursion consumes memory per active frame, and that memory is bounded by the recursion depth. An algorithm can allocate no heap yet still be <code>O(n)</code> in space if it recurses <code>n</code> deep — and it fails by overflowing the stack, not by running slowly. A recursive DFS on a 100k-node chain is the canonical example; converting it to an explicit-stack loop moves the same <code>O(n)</code> to the heap where it won't overflow.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Big O Notation.md" class="internal-link">Big O Notation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is an algorithm with worse Big O the right choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When inputs are small and bounded, so the dropped constants dominate — insertion sort inside a hybrid sort below ~16 elements, or a linear scan over 10 items instead of building a hash set. Big O is an asymptotic statement; below the crossover point a "worse" class with a smaller constant and better cache behaviour is genuinely faster. Narrow candidates by complexity class, then measure on representative data.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Big O Notation.md" class="internal-link">Big O Notation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does algorithmic complexity matter less than constant factors?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When input sizes are small and bounded (e.g., iterating over 10 HTTP headers), constant factors and cache locality dominate. A theoretically better algorithm with higher overhead (setup cost, memory indirection) can be slower than a simpler one on small inputs.<br />
This is why .NET's <code>Array.Sort</code> uses insertion sort for small subarrays inside its introspective sort implementation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Computer Science.md" class="internal-link">Computer Science</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide between optimizing data structure choice versus algorithm choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start with the data structure. The right structure often eliminates the need for a clever algorithm — a <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code> gives O(1) lookup without binary search, a <code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code> gives ordered iteration without explicit sorting. Optimize the algorithm when the structure is fixed by external constraints (e.g., searching within a sorted array from an external source).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Computer Science.md" class="internal-link">Computer Science</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="computer-science-algorithms">Algorithms</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is an algorithm? How is its efficiency measured?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">An algorithm is a finite, ordered set of steps that transforms input into the required output.</li>
<li dir="auto">Time complexity describes how runtime grows as input size increases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Space complexity describes extra memory needed as input grows.</li>
<li dir="auto">Big O notation is used to compare growth classes independent of hardware details.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why it matters: complexity awareness helps avoid implementations that fail under production scale.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Big O not enough to choose the fastest algorithm in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Big O describes asymptotic growth and ignores constant factors, cache locality, branch prediction, and real input distributions. Quick Sort is O(n log n) average like Merge Sort but often faster on arrays due to cache-friendly in-place partitioning.<br />
Measure on representative data after narrowing candidates by complexity class.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between worst-case, average-case, and amortized complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Worst-case guarantees behavior under adversarial or degenerate inputs (important for security and SLAs). Average-case describes typical behavior under random inputs. Amortized complexity describes cost spread over a sequence of operations (e.g., dynamic array resizing is O(1) amortized even though individual resizes are O(n)).<br />
Choose based on whether you control the input distribution and whether tail latency matters.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Graph Algorithms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does admissibility guarantee, and what does consistency add on top?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Admissibility (<code>h(n)</code> never exceeds the true remaining cost) makes A<em> return an optimal path: when the goal is popped its <code>f</code> equals its actual cost, and every frontier node's <code>f</code> is a lower bound on any path through it, so nothing cheaper is hidden. Consistency (<code>h(n) ≤ cost(n, n') + h(n')</code>) additionally forces <code>f</code> to be non-decreasing along a path, so a node's first pop is already optimal — graph-search A</em> can close it and never reopen it, expanding each node at most once.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/A-Star Search.md" class="internal-link">A-Star Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">An inadmissible heuristic returns a longer path with no error. What causes that?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Overestimating the remaining cost for a node on the true optimal path inflates that node's <code>f</code>. A<em> then pops the goal through a cheaper-looking detour before it expands the node on the real shortest path. The search still terminates and returns a valid path — just not the minimum-cost one — because the inflated <code>f</code> reordered the frontier against the optimum. Weighted A</em> (<code>f = g + ε·h</code>, <code>ε &gt; 1</code>) does this on purpose for a path bounded within <code>ε</code> of optimal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/A-Star Search.md" class="internal-link">A-Star Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is memory the usual failure mode, and what do IDA<em> and weighted A</em> trade for it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A<em> keeps every generated node in the open frontier and closed set, <code>O(nodes stored)</code>, which on a large state space exhausts memory before running out of time. IDA</em> keeps only the current path (<code>O(L)</code> memory) and re-expands nodes across rising <code>f</code>-cost thresholds, paying repeated work for a small footprint. Weighted A<em> keeps A</em>'s structure but scales <code>h</code> to shrink the frontier, trading a bounded loss of optimality for fewer stored nodes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/A-Star Search.md" class="internal-link">A-Star Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the articulation-point rule for a non-root vertex versus the DFS root, and why do they differ?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A non-root <code>u</code> is a cut vertex when it has a tree child <code>v</code> with <code>low[v] &gt;= disc[u]</code>: nothing in <code>v</code>'s subtree reaches above <code>u</code>, so <code>u</code> is that subtree's only exit. The root has no ancestor, so the same inequality is vacuously true for its first child and would over-report. The root is a cut vertex only when it has two or more tree children, since those subtrees can reach each other only through the root.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Articulation Points and Bridges.md" class="internal-link">Articulation Points and Bridges</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the bridge test strict (<code>low[v] &gt; disc[u]</code>) while the articulation test is not (<code>low[v] &gt;= disc[u]</code>)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>low[v] == disc[u]</code> means the deepest a back edge from <code>v</code>'s subtree reaches is exactly <code>u</code>. That back edge bypasses the edge <code>(u, v)</code>, so the edge is not a bridge — hence strict <code>&gt;</code>. The same back edge still routes all of the subtree's traffic through the vertex <code>u</code>, so <code>u</code> remains a cut vertex — hence non-strict <code>&gt;=</code>. The one operator carries the entire difference between cut edges and cut vertices.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Articulation Points and Bridges.md" class="internal-link">Articulation Points and Bridges</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do parallel edges break the usual parent check, and what corrects it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The common guard <code>if (v == parent) continue;</code> skips every edge back to the parent vertex. With two parallel edges between <code>u</code> and <code>v</code>, it discards the second edge too, so <code>v</code>'s subtree appears to have no route up and <code>(u, v)</code> is reported as a bridge — although the duplicate edge is itself the route keeping them connected. Skipping only the specific parent edge by id leaves the duplicate as a back edge that lowers <code>low[v]</code> and cancels the false bridge.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Articulation Points and Bridges.md" class="internal-link">Articulation Points and Bridges</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why exactly <code>V−1</code> rounds, and what does a relaxation on the <code>V</code>-th round prove?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Without a negative cycle, every shortest path is simple and spans at most <code>V−1</code> edges. Round <code>k</code> finalizes every shortest path of at most <code>k</code> edges, so <code>V−1</code> rounds finalize all of them. A relaxation still possible on the <code>V</code>-th round means a path is shortening past that <code>V−1</code>-edge limit, which only a negative cycle permits, so the extra round is the negative-cycle detector rather than wasted work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is the actual negative cycle recovered after detection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Take a vertex that relaxed on the <code>V</code>-th round; it lies on a negative cycle or is reachable from one. Walking <code>pred</code> back <code>V</code> times cannot exit a cycle once inside it, so the walk ends on a cycle vertex. Following <code>pred</code> from there until it returns to that vertex lists the cycle.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">After a reachable negative cycle is detected, what are the correct distances?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Vertices reachable through the cycle have distance <code>−∞</code>, because each lap lowers the total without bound; vertices with no path have <code>+∞</code>; the rest are finite. The <code>V−1</code>-round numbers for the <code>−∞</code> region are a mid-descent snapshot, so reporting them as finite distances is the common bug.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does bidirectional search cut <code>O(b^d)</code> to <code>O(b^(d/2))</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single BFS reaching depth <code>d</code> expands a frontier that grows as <code>b^d</code>. Splitting the path into two halves lets a forward search and a backward search each stop at depth <code>d/2</code>, so each explores about <code>b^(d/2)</code> vertices and the total is <code>2·b^(d/2)</code>. The reduction lands on the exponent, which is why it compounds with depth: every extra level of separation that would multiply a one-sided search by another factor of <code>b</code> only adds half a level to each frontier. The same halving applies to space, since both sides must be held in memory to detect the meeting.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is stopping at the first frontier collision correct on an unweighted graph but wrong on a weighted one?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Unweighted BFS expands level by level, settling distances in nondecreasing order, so the first vertex shared by both visited sets already lies on a shortest path. With weights the frontiers advance by cumulative cost, and the first shared vertex can sit on an expensive path while a cheaper meeting is one relaxation away elsewhere. The weighted version must track the best <code>gF[u] + gB[u]</code> and keep expanding until the two frontiers' combined radius reaches that best, proving no unexpanded path can beat it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What must the graph and the goal provide before bidirectional search applies?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The target must be a concrete vertex, because the backward search needs a starting point — a goal given only as a predicate has nothing to expand backward from. The graph must expose predecessors, either as a reverse adjacency list or as an invertible move operator, since the backward frontier walks edges in reverse. Without both, the second frontier cannot reach depth <code>d/2</code> and the halving disappears.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does finding all components need an outer loop over every vertex?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single DFS or BFS from one source only reaches that source's component. In a disconnected graph, vertices in other components are never touched by that flood. Scanning every vertex and starting a new flood from each still-unlabelled one is what guarantees full coverage; each such vertex is provably in a component no earlier flood reached, so it starts a new component id.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is union-find preferable to a traversal for components?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When edges are not all available up front, or when <code>connected(a, b)</code> queries interleave with edge additions.
