Union-find merges endpoints in <code>O(α(V))</code> amortised and answers connectivity immediately, with no re-traversal after each new edge. A traversal would have to re-run from scratch after every change. The trade-off is that union-find reports set membership and counts, but listing a component's members needs a final grouping pass over the roots.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do DFS and BFS produce the same components but different from strongly connected components?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Connected components depend only on which vertices are mutually reachable, which is order-independent, so DFS and BFS partition identically. Strongly connected components are defined on <em>directed</em> graphs and require a path each way between every pair; a single undirected-style traversal cannot detect that, which is why the directed case needs Tarjan's or Kosaraju's two-way-reachability algorithm.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does BFS return a fewest-edge path while DFS does not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The FIFO queue dequeues nodes in nondecreasing distance from the source, so the first time BFS reaches a node it is along a path with the minimum number of edges. DFS's LIFO stack commits to one branch, so it can reach a node through a longer branch before a shorter one would surface; the first path it records carries no length guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What sets BFS versus DFS auxiliary space, and when does each lose?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">BFS's queue holds a full distance layer, so its space is <code>O(V)</code> and grows with graph width — a wide, shallow graph can put most vertices in the frontier at once. DFS's frontier is the current root-to-node path, <code>O(h)</code>; a deep, narrow graph makes that path long, and recursive DFS then overflows the call stack.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a visited set required for termination, and when must a node be marked?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Without it, a cycle re-adds a node to the frontier forever and the traversal never ends. A node must be marked at discovery, when it enters the frontier, because between discovery and expansion it can be reached from several neighbours; marking only at expansion lets duplicates accumulate in BFS, and in iterative DFS forces a second <code>visited</code> check at pop time.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does directed cycle detection need three states rather than a visited flag?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A visited flag cannot tell a back edge, into a node still on the current DFS path and therefore closing a cycle, from a cross or forward edge into a node whose subtree already finished. Tracking unvisited, in-progress, and done marks a cycle only when an edge leads into an in-progress node.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the settle-once rule require non-negative edge weights?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When a node is popped it is treated as final. The proof that this is safe relies on the tail of any alternative path — from the first unsettled node it reaches onward — having non-negative length, so that first node's tentative distance already bounds the whole path. A negative edge lets that tail subtract cost, so a later path can beat a node's settled distance and the reported distance is wrong.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the <code>if settled[node] continue</code> guard fix?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A binary heap has no <code>decrease-key</code>, so relaxing a node pushes a new pair and leaves the old larger one behind. The guard skips a node the second time it is popped, after a cheaper pair already settled it. Without the guard, the stale pair re-relaxes that node's edges from an outdated distance and can lower a frontier neighbour incorrectly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an array scan beat a binary heap on a dense graph?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">With a heap, each of the <code>E</code> relaxations may cost <code>O(log V)</code>, giving <code>O((V + E) log V)</code>. On a dense graph <code>E ≈ V²</code>, so the heap term dominates at <code>O(V² log V)</code>. Scanning all vertices to pick the minimum is <code>O(V)</code> per step and <code>O(V²)</code> overall, which drops the <code>log V</code> factor entirely.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you pick BFS over DFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">BFS is preferred for shortest path by edge count in unweighted graphs.</li>
<li dir="auto">DFS is preferred for deep traversal tasks like cycle detection and topological ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">BFS uses more memory on wide graphs because of the frontier queue.</li>
<li dir="auto">Both are O(V+E), so pick by the property you need, not by speed: BFS guarantees shortest paths but its frontier can hold a whole layer; DFS uses depth-bounded memory but gives no distance guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Dijkstra not valid with negative edges?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Dijkstra assumes once a node is finalized, its best distance is known.</li>
<li dir="auto">Negative edges can later produce a shorter route to a finalized node.</li>
<li dir="auto">Bellman Ford handles negative edges by repeated relaxation.</li>
<li dir="auto">Dijkstra is faster (O((V+E) log V)) but only valid with non-negative weights; Bellman–Ford accepts negative edges at O(V·E) — pay the slower cost only when weights can go negative.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Adjacency list or adjacency matrix?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Adjacency list is the default for sparse graphs (most real-world graphs): O(V+E) space and efficient neighbor iteration.</li>
<li dir="auto">Adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space but answers "is there an edge A→B?" in O(1).</li>
<li dir="auto">In .NET, <code>Dictionary&lt;T, List&lt;T&gt;&gt;</code> is a common adjacency-list implementation.</li>
<li dir="auto">The list saves memory and speeds traversal on sparse graphs; the matrix trades O(V²) memory for constant-time edge checks, so reach for it only on dense graphs or edge-query-heavy workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Greedy Best-First Search neither optimal nor complete?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It orders the frontier by <code>h(n)</code> alone and never accounts for <code>g(n)</code>, the cost already spent. A neighbor that is close in straight-line distance but reached by a long detour is expanded before a farther-looking neighbor that sits beside the goal, so the returned path can be far from shortest — not optimal. On an infinite graph a monotonically improving <code>h</code> can lead expansion down a branch that never reaches the goal — not complete. A finite graph with a visited set terminates, but the path it returns can still be long.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a concave obstacle around the goal cause thrashing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Every cell inside the pocket is geometrically near the goal, so all of them score a low <code>h</code> and the frontier keeps selecting barrier-hugging cells. The direct heading is blocked, and the accumulated <code>g</code> that would reveal the long way around is never read, so expansion oscillates along the wall before escaping. It is the h-only ordering, not the map, that has no way to notice the pocket is a dead pull.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the visited set guarantee, and what does it not fix?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It stops a node from being enqueued twice, which prevents cycles from looping forever and guarantees termination on a finite graph. It does not make the returned path optimal, and it cannot bound an infinite graph where <code>h</code> keeps improving down a fruitless branch.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do residual back-edges make greedy augmenting paths exact?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A greedy first path can commit flow to an edge the optimum routes around, and conservation then blocks every forward correction. Each unit sent <code>u → v</code> creates a residual back-arc <code>v → u</code> of equal capacity, and a later augmenting path can traverse it to retract and reroute that flow. The loop therefore cannot stop until no <code>s → t</code> path remains — which, by max-flow min-cut, is the maximum. Without the back-arcs the algorithm can wedge strictly below it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is the minimum cut recovered once the flow is maximal?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Let <code>S</code> be the vertices reachable from <code>s</code> in the final residual graph; <code>t</code> is not among them. Every original edge from <code>S</code> to <code>T</code> is saturated, so the sum of their capacities equals the flow value. The cut side comes from residual reachability, but the reported edges are the original forward edges out of <code>S</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Edmonds–Karp <code>O(V·E²)</code> while Ford–Fulkerson is only <code>O(E·\|f\|)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Ford–Fulkerson bounds iterations by the flow value because each augmentation adds at least one unit — a bound that inflates with large capacities and fails outright on irrational ones. Edmonds–Karp augments along the fewest-edge path via BFS; the BFS distance to each vertex never decreases, so each edge is the bottleneck <code>O(V)</code> times, capping augmentations at <code>O(V·E)</code>. With <code>O(E)</code> per BFS that is <code>O(V·E²)</code>, independent of capacity magnitudes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Dinic or push–relabel earn its extra complexity over Edmonds–Karp?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Dinic wins whenever the graph is large or the workload is bipartite matching: same model, <code>O(V)</code> phases instead of <code>O(V·E)</code> augmentations, and an <code>O(E·√V)</code> unit-capacity bound. Push–relabel wins on dense graphs, where its <code>O(V²·√E)</code> / <code>O(V³)</code> bounds beat Dinic's <code>O(V²·E)</code>, accepting a harder implementation and a less direct min-cut readout.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are Prim's and Kruskal's optimal even though they never reconsider an edge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The cut property: for any partition of the vertices, the minimum-weight edge crossing it lies in some MST. Prim's applies it to the (in-tree, out-of-tree) cut; Kruskal's to the cut between the two components an edge would join. Each added edge is therefore provably safe, so a chain of greedy choices reaches a global optimum without backtracking.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What guarantees the edge Kruskal's adds is the safe one?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Edges are processed in ascending weight, so when an edge joining two different components is reached, every lighter edge has already been consumed or rejected. Nothing lighter connects those two components, which makes this edge the minimum one crossing the cut between them — exactly the edge the cut property certifies.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the tree path between two vertices be longer than their shortest path?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An MST minimizes the total weight of all its edges, not the distance between any given pair. In the triangle <code>A–B = 3</code>, <code>B–C = 3</code>, <code>A–C = 4</code>, the MST drops <code>A–C</code> and routes <code>A</code>→<code>C</code> through <code>B</code> at cost 6, versus the direct edge's 4. Pairwise shortest paths come from Dijkstra over the full graph, not from an MST.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do the algorithms produce on a disconnected graph?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No MST exists. Kruskal's returns a spanning forest — one minimum tree per component — and Prim's from a single start reaches only that vertex's component. Both finish with fewer than <code>V − 1</code> edges, and that shortfall is how the disconnection is detected.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">In Tarjan's algorithm, what does <code>low[v] == disc[v]</code> mean, and why does it identify an SCC root?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>disc[v]</code> is <code>v</code>'s discovery index; <code>low[v]</code> is the smallest discovery index reachable from <code>v</code>'s subtree through tree edges and at most one edge to an on-stack vertex. Equality means nothing in the subtree found a route back to a vertex discovered before <code>v</code>, so <code>v</code> is the entry point of its component. Popping the stack down to <code>v</code> emits exactly that SCC.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why may only vertices still on the stack lower <code>low[v]</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The stack holds vertices whose component is not yet finished. An edge to an on-stack vertex stays inside the component under construction, so it legitimately extends the reach recorded by <code>low</code>. An edge to an already-popped vertex points into a finished component the search can never climb back through; taking its discovery index would drag <code>low[v]</code> below <code>disc[v]</code>, break the root test, and merge two separate SCCs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Kosaraju's second DFS run on the transpose in decreasing finish order?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">First-pass finish times form a reverse topological order of the condensation, so the last vertex to finish lies in a source SCC. Reversing every edge turns that source into a sink — reachable from the start vertex but with no edge leading out to a not-yet-emitted component. Each second-pass DFS therefore fills one SCC and stops. Removing either the finish order or the transpose destroys that isolation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the condensation of a digraph always a DAG, and what does that buy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each SCC is a maximal mutually reachable set. A cycle between two distinct components would make every vertex in both mutually reachable, so they would already be one component — a contradiction. With no cycles left, the condensation admits a topological order, which lets DAG-only techniques (topological sort, DAG dynamic programming, 2-SAT implication solving) run on graphs that originally contained cycles.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a topological order exist only for a DAG?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A valid order must place <code>u</code> before <code>v</code> for every edge <code>u → v</code>. A cycle <code>a → b → … → a</code> would require <code>a</code> before <code>b</code> and <code>b</code> before <code>a</code> at once, which is impossible. So an order exists iff the graph is acyclic, and the construction that finds the order doubles as the acyclicity test.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does each construction detect a cycle?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Kahn's only enqueues a vertex when its in-degree reaches 0. A vertex on a cycle keeps at least one incoming edge from another cycle member, so its count never reaches 0 and it is never emitted; an output shorter than <code>V</code> means a cycle remains. The DFS construction reports a cycle when it follows an edge to a vertex still open on the recursion stack — a descendant reaching an ancestor.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the topological order generally not unique, and how is a deterministic one obtained?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Whenever two or more vertices have in-degree 0 at the same time, either may be emitted next, so most DAGs admit many orders. Keying Kahn's frontier with a priority queue instead of a plain queue fixes the choice — for example always taking the smallest label yields the lexicographically smallest order.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the <code>k</code>-loop the outermost of the three?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">After stage <code>k</code>, <code>dist[i][j]</code> is defined as the shortest <code>i</code>→<code>j</code> path using intermediates only from <code>{0..k}</code>, so <code>k</code> names a stage that must complete over the entire matrix before the next begins. The relaxation reads <code>dist[i][k]</code> and <code>dist[k][j]</code> expecting the previous stage's values; with <code>i</code> or <code>j</code> outermost those cells belong to an unfinished stage, and the recurrence consumes half-updated data. The output is still finite and still returned, so the error is silent.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the in-place update on a single matrix correct, and what does that save?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For finite shortest paths with no negative cycles, neither <code>dist[i][k]</code> nor <code>dist[k][j]</code> can improve during stage <code>k</code>, because a shortest path routed through <code>k</code> never uses <code>k</code> as an intermediate of its own two legs. Reading and writing the one matrix therefore returns the same values a separate previous copy would have held. Keeping a distinct matrix per stage would cost <code>Θ(V³)</code> space; the observation drops it to <code>Θ(V²)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Floyd-Warshall surface a negative cycle, and how does that differ from Bellman-Ford?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">After the sweep, <code>dist[w][w] &lt; 0</code> is a negative-cycle witness: a negative closed walk leaves <code>w</code> and returns to <code>w</code>, even if the simple cycle lies elsewhere on that walk. Any pair that can reach and leave <code>w</code> has no finite shortest distance and must be marked <code>−∞</code> or rejected. Bellman-Ford instead runs one additional relaxation from a chosen source and, with predecessors, can extract a concrete cycle, which is what arbitrage-style problems need.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Paradigms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What turns brute-force enumeration into backtracking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A feasibility test applied to a partial candidate. Brute force builds every complete configuration and tests it at the end; backtracking tests the prefix after each choice and, on a violation, discards every configuration sharing that prefix without generating them.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must a choice be undone after its subtree is explored?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The partial candidate is a single mutable buffer shared by all branches to avoid copying. After a subtree is exhausted the choice is reverted so the next sibling starts from the state its parent established. An un-reverted choice leaks into siblings and silently corrupts the enumeration.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the worst-case class still exponential despite pruning?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Pruning removes subtrees but does not shrink the complete search tree, which has <code>O(b^d)</code> nodes. When no prefix can be rejected before a leaf, every candidate is still visited. Pruning improves the constant and the practical node count, not the asymptotic class.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the bounding function be optimistic?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The bound gates pruning: a subtree is discarded when its bound cannot beat the incumbent. If a maximisation bound under-estimates a subtree's true best, that subtree can be pruned while it still holds the optimum, and the search returns a suboptimal incumbent labelled as proven-optimal — a silent correctness failure. The requirement is exactly A<em> search's admissibility: the estimate may only err optimistically. An LP relaxation satisfies it because a fractional optimum can only meet or exceed the integer one.<br />
</em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a>*</p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What determines how much of the tree is actually explored?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Two factors independent of the worst-case class: the tightness of the bound and how early a strong incumbent is found. A tight bound rejects more subtrees per comparison; an early incumbent raises the bar every later bound must clear. Exploration order feeds the second — best-first reaches a strong incumbent with the fewest expansions but stores a large frontier, while depth-first reaches some incumbent fast in <code>O(depth)</code> memory.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Does branch-and-bound lower the complexity class of an NP-hard problem?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No. The worst case stays exponential; on an adversarial instance the bound prunes nothing and it reduces to brute-force enumeration. It improves the constant factor and the typical case, often by orders of magnitude, and returns a certificate of optimality — but not polynomial time. On very large instances the practical answer is an approximation, not a proven optimum.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What two properties must hold for DP to apply, and what does each guarantee?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Optimal substructure — an optimal solution is composed of optimal solutions to subproblems — makes combining sub-answers valid. Overlapping subproblems — the same subproblem recurs — makes caching worthwhile. Without the first, the recurrence yields a wrong optimum; without the second, a memo never gets a second hit and only adds overhead, which is the divide-and-conquer regime.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does DP's running time come from?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The number of distinct states multiplied by the work to combine each state from its sub-states. LCS over lengths m and n has <code>m·n</code> prefix-pair states with <code>O(1)</code> work each, giving <code>O(m·n)</code>. The state count is also the memory bound, unless a rolling array drops rows that are no longer read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can a correct recurrence still produce wrong answers once implemented?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">If the state omits an argument the answer depends on, two different subproblems map to the same cache slot and the second read returns a stale value with no error raised. Bottom-up, the analogous failure is filling a cell before its dependencies, reading uninitialised sub-answers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does greedy solve coin change for <code>{1, 5, 10, 25}</code> but not <code>{1, 3, 4}</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Standard denominations are constructed so the largest coin not exceeding the remainder always belongs to some optimal solution — the greedy-choice property holds, and an exchange argument confirms it. For <code>{1, 3, 4}</code> making <code>6</code>, the largest-coin rule yields <code>4 + 1 + 1</code> (three coins) while <code>3 + 3</code> (two coins) is optimal, so the first greedy commitment is in no optimal solution and the property fails. The loop is identical in both cases; only the denomination set decides correctness.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does an exchange argument establish?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">That the greedy first choice is contained in some optimal solution. Starting from an arbitrary optimal solution, one of its choices is swapped for the greedy choice and shown not to reduce quality; repeating the swap transforms it into the greedy solution without ever getting worse, so greedy is at least as good as any optimum. Combined with optimal substructure, induction extends the result to the entire run.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is fractional knapsack inside greedy but 0/1 knapsack outside it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In fractional knapsack the highest value-per-weight item can always be taken — cut to fit the remaining capacity — so it belongs to an optimal solution and the greedy-choice property holds. In 0/1 knapsack an item is all-or-nothing, so a high-ratio item can be excluded from every optimal packing when a different indivisible combination uses its capacity for more total value; the greedy-choice property fails even though the problem keeps optimal substructure — the <code>max(OPT(cap, items∖{i}), v_i + OPT(cap − w_i, items∖{i}))</code> recurrence that the <code>O(nW)</code> DP exploits. The failing property is the greedy choice, not the substructure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does the Master Theorem apply, and how does it produce merge sort's <code>Θ(n log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It applies to a fixed number <code>a</code> of equal-size subproblems <code>n/b</code>, with recurrence <code>T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)</code>. Merge sort has <code>a = 2</code>, <code>b = 2</code>, and <code>f(n) = Θ(n)</code>. Because <code>n^(log_2 2) = n</code>, Case 2 applies and the <code>Θ(n)</code> work at each of <code>Θ(log n)</code> levels totals <code>Θ(n log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does independence mean, and what extra conditions does parallel execution require?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Independence means each subproblem can be solved without another subproblem's result; it does not require disjoint storage. Parallel execution additionally requires safe access to shared data, synchronization before <code>Combine</code>, and enough work per task to repay scheduling overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do production divide-and-conquer sorts stop recursing above the base case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Small-range cutoffs reduce call and partition overhead by handing tiny ranges to insertion sort. They do not prevent stack overflow from large, repeatedly unbalanced partitions. Introsort treats that separately: it tracks partition depth and switches to heapsort when its depth budget is exhausted.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do overlapping subproblems differ from shared storage?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Overlap means the same logical state is reachable from multiple recursion branches and would be solved repeatedly without caching. Shared storage is an implementation detail: independent subproblems may safely read the same immutable data, while overlapping states may be represented in separate allocations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the relationship between memoization and dynamic programming?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Memoization is DP's top-down implementation: write the recurrence and cache each subproblem's result. Bottom-up tabulation is the iterative alternative. Repeated, overlapping states are what make memoization useful; without them, the cache adds overhead but does not make the recurrence incorrect. Optimal substructure is a separate requirement for optimization problems: an optimal solution must be constructible from optimal solutions to its subproblems. Memoization additionally evaluates only the states recursion reaches, while tabulation usually fills the planned table in dependency order.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Memoization.md" class="internal-link">Memoization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must a memoised function be pure, and what breaks if it isn't?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The cache returns a stored result for repeated arguments without re-running the function. If the output also depends on hidden state — a global, the clock, an I/O read — a cache hit hands back a value computed under conditions that may no longer hold, and side effects the caller expected simply don't happen on a hit. Only same-input-same-output, side-effect-free functions are safe to memoise.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Memoization.md" class="internal-link">Memoization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the most common correctness bug when memoising a recurrence?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An incomplete cache key. If the key omits an argument the result depends on — caching a <code>(i, capacity)</code> knapsack state on <code>i</code> alone — two different subproblems map to the same slot and the second read returns a stale value, silently. The key must be the full state, the same requirement DP calls state design.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Memoization.md" class="internal-link">Memoization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a memoised recurrence be rewritten as bottom-up tabulation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the recursion is deep enough to risk a stack overflow (a long chain of dependencies), when essentially every state gets visited so laziness saves nothing, or when a rolling-array reduction can cut memory that the recursive form can't exploit. Tabulation runs in tight iterative loops with <code>O(1)</code> stack, at the cost of computing states a lazy memo might have skipped.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Memoization.md" class="internal-link">Memoization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>n &amp; (n - 1)</code> clear only the lowest set bit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Subtracting 1 borrows through the trailing zeros and flips the lowest set bit to 0, leaving every higher bit unchanged; the two values differ exactly in that bit and the zeros beneath it. AND keeps the shared high bits and clears the low region. Iterating it touches each set bit once, so Kernighan's popcount runs in <code>O(number of set bits)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does XOR-ing an entire array isolate a single unpaired value?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">XOR is commutative and self-inverse: <code>a ^ a == 0</code> and <code>a ^ 0 == a</code>. Every value appearing an even number of times cancels to 0 regardless of order, so the accumulator ends holding only the value with no partner — <code>O(n)</code> time, <code>O(1)</code> space, no auxiliary set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What distinguishes an arithmetic right shift from a logical one, and when does the choice matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An arithmetic shift copies the sign bit into the vacated high positions (<code>&gt;&gt;</code> on signed types in C# and Java), so <code>-8 &gt;&gt; 1 == -4</code>. A logical shift zero-fills (<code>&gt;&gt;&gt;</code>, or <code>&gt;&gt;</code> on unsigned types). The difference only shows on values with the high bit set; using the arithmetic shift where zero-fill was intended leaves stray 1s in the high bits.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>n &amp; -n</code> isolate the lowest set bit only under two's-complement?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>-n</code> is computed as <code>~n + 1</code>, which inverts every bit above the lowest set bit while reproducing that bit and its trailing zeros; AND with <code>n</code> keeps just that bit. The identity is a property of two's-complement negation and does not hold under a sign-magnitude representation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a meeting between the pointers equivalent to the existence of a cycle?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Once both pointers are inside a loop, the fast pointer gains one node per step, so their separation modulo the cycle length runs through zero and they must coincide. With no loop the fast pointer reaches <code>null</code> and the walk ends with no meeting. Neither direction admits a false result, so a meeting is exactly a cycle.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">After the first meeting, why does resetting one pointer to the head locate the cycle entry?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">At the meeting the slow pointer has gone <code>d</code> steps and the fast <code>2d</code>; the surplus <code>d</code> must be a whole number of laps, <code>k·λ</code>. That places the meeting node <code>μ mod λ</code> steps before the entry. A pointer restarted at the head reaches the entry in <code>μ</code> steps; the other, stepped <code>μ</code> times from the meeting node, covers the remaining distance plus whole laps and lands on the entry at the same time.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does this pattern extend to <code>Find the Duplicate Number</code>, and what plays the role of <code>next</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The array of <code>n + 1</code> values in <code>[1..n]</code> is read as edges <code>i → nums[i]</code>. Because some value repeats, two indices point to the same node, which forces a cycle; the cycle's entry is the duplicated value. The successor function <code>next(i) = nums[i]</code> replaces the list's <code>next</code> pointer, so the same detection and entry-finding phases apply in <code>O(1)</code> space without mutating the array.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a hash set of visited nodes the better choice than fast/slow?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both detect a cycle in <code>O(n)</code> time; the set costs <code>O(n)</code> space and Floyd costs <code>O(1)</code>. The set returns the entry as the first repeated node with no second phase and gives the full set of visited nodes for free. It wins when that memory is affordable and the visited set or immediate entry is wanted; Floyd wins when memory is tight or the structure is read-only.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does sorting by start reduce merging to a single linear sweep?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">After ascending starts, every interval later in the list starts at or after the current one. If <code>next.start &gt; current.end</code>, no later interval can reach back to <code>current</code> either, so <code>current</code> is final and can be emitted. One comparison per interval decides overlap, turning an <code>O(n²)</code> all-pairs check into an <code>O(n)</code> pass on top of the <code>O(n log n)</code> sort.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why take <code>max(current.end, next.end)</code> when extending rather than <code>next.end</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>next</code> can be entirely nested inside <code>current</code> — merging <code>[1,10]</code> with <code>[2,3]</code> should stay <code>[1,10]</code>. Assigning <code>current.end = next.end</code> would shrink the block to <code>[1,3]</code> and lose coverage. The <code>max</code> keeps <code>current.end</code> at the furthest right edge merged so far, which is the value the next overlap test depends on.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the interval convention change the result, and where does it show up in code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Closed intervals count touching endpoints as overlap (<code>[1,2]</code> and <code>[2,3]</code> merge); half-open intervals do not. The convention is the difference between <code>&lt;=</code> and <code>&lt;</code> in the overlap test. Choosing wrong yields off-by-one merges that only fail on boundary-touching inputs. Meeting-room problems usually want half-open so back-to-back bookings share a room.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a sweep-line over events or an interval tree replace sort-then-merge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A sweep-line over separated start/end events is needed when the answer is a concurrency count — maximum overlap or minimum rooms — rather than merged ranges. An interval tree replaces both when intervals are inserted and queried dynamically, trading an <code>O(n log n)</code> build for <code>O(log n)</code> overlap queries against a changing set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a monotonic stack <code>O(n)</code> when the code reads as a nested loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Every index is pushed exactly once and popped at most once, so the total number of pops across the whole scan is bounded by <code>n</code>. The inner "pop while" therefore does <code>O(n)</code> work summed over all outer iterations, not <code>O(n)</code> per iteration. Charging each pop to the unique element that performed it gives the amortized linear bound.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must a monotonic deque store indices rather than values?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A window maximum is evicted precisely when its position falls out of range, so the front check compares stored indices against the window bound. A deque of raw values has discarded the positions and cannot tell when a candidate has left the window, returning a stale maximum from a slot no longer in scope.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the monotone direction relate to which query is answered?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The direction of the stack fixes the query. A decreasing stack (values fall bottom to top) resolves each popped index against the arriving larger value, yielding the next <em>greater</em> element; reversing the pop comparison makes the stack increasing and yields the next <em>smaller</em> element. Choosing the direction backwards produces a fully populated result that answers the opposite question.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>prefix[r + 1] - prefix[l]</code> yield the sum of <code>a[l..r]</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>prefix[r + 1]</code> is the sum of every element before index <code>r + 1</code>, i.e. <code>a[0..r]</code>, and <code>prefix[l]</code> is the sum of <code>a[0..l-1]</code>. Their difference cancels the shared head <code>a[0..l-1]</code> exactly, leaving <code>a[l] + ... + a[r]</code>. The <code>prefix[0] = 0</code> sentinel lets <code>l = 0</code> use the same formula with no special case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes prefix sums unsuitable once the array is updated between queries?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Writing one element changes every running total from that index onward, so the whole tail of <code>prefix</code> is invalid and must be rebuilt in <code>O(n)</code>. With <code>q</code> interleaved updates this degrades to <code>O(nq)</code>. A Fenwick tree (point update and prefix query in <code>O(log n)</code>) or a segment tree (range update and query) keeps updates cheap instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a difference array relate to a prefix sum?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It is the dual. A prefix sum makes range <em>queries</em> <code>O(1)</code> by storing running totals; a difference array makes range <em>updates</em> <code>O(1)</code> by storing deltas at the two endpoints (<code>diff[l] += v</code>, <code>diff[r + 1] -= v</code>). A single prefix-sum pass over the difference array then reconstructs the final values in <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a sliding window <code>O(n)</code> and not <code>O(n·k)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each index is added to the aggregate once, when the right boundary passes it, and removed at most once, when the left boundary passes it. Total boundary movement is bounded by <code>2n</code> and each move is <code>O(1)</code>, so window width never enters the cost — the aggregate is adjusted by its two boundary elements rather than rescanned.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
