</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What property must the aggregate have, and which aggregate violates it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The new window's value must be reconstructable in <code>O(1)</code> from a single entering or leaving element. A running sum and a per-key frequency count are; a window maximum is not, because removing the element that held the maximum leaves no cheap way to recover the next-largest element still inside the window. That case uses a monotonic deque of candidate indices instead of a scalar.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do negative numbers break sum-based sliding windows?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The contraction rule "shrink while the sum exceeds the target" relies on the sum rising on every entry and falling on every removal, which makes the smallest valid window length monotone as the right boundary advances. Negatives remove that monotonicity — a longer window can carry a smaller sum — so a failed window can become valid by extending. Those problems use prefix sums plus a hash map rather than a moving window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">To find the <code>k</code> largest elements, why is the heap a min-heap rather than a max-heap?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The size-<code>k</code> min-heap keeps its root as the smallest of the <code>k</code> best elements seen so far — the weakest survivor. A new element is relevant only when it beats that root, which a min-heap exposes as an <code>O(1)</code> peek, and when it does the root is the correct element to evict. A max-heap would surface the largest retained element, which is never the one to drop, so it cannot drive the scan.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the streaming heap <code>O(n log k)</code> rather than <code>O(n log n)</code>, and where does that matter beyond speed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The heap is capped at <code>k</code> entries, so each insert or replace is <code>O(log k)</code>, giving <code>O(n log k)</code> over <code>n</code> elements. The bound beats a full sort when <code>k ≪ n</code>, but the decisive property is the <code>O(k)</code> resident set: it never needs all <code>n</code> elements in memory, so it is the only option that runs on a stream too large to hold.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What disqualifies Quickselect for a top-<code>k</code> problem, and what is its worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Quickselect needs the entire array addressable and mutates it in place, so a streaming or immutable input rules it out. On a static array it is <code>O(n)</code> average from geometric partition shrink, but degrades to <code>O(n²)</code> when pivots are consistently bad, such as a naive pivot on sorted input. A randomized pivot makes that improbable; median-of-medians guarantees <code>O(n)</code> worst case at a larger constant.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the converging two-sum require sorted input?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The move "advance left or retreat right" is justified only because raising <code>left</code> can only increase the sum and lowering <code>right</code> can only decrease it. Sorted order guarantees that monotonicity. On unsorted data the relationship is gone, so a pointer move can step over the pair that answers the query.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When <code>a[left] + a[right]</code> is too small, which pairs become impossible and why is <code>left++</code> safe?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Every pair that keeps the current <code>left</code> and uses any partner at or below <code>right</code> is even smaller than the current sum, so no pair anchored at <code>left</code> can reach the target. That entire column is eliminated, and <code>left++</code> discards it without losing a possible answer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a hash set buy over two pointers for two-sum?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A hash set removes the sorted-input precondition and finds a complement in <code>O(n)</code> time on unsorted data. The cost is <code>O(n)</code> extra memory and the loss of ordered traversal, which two pointers keep for free when the array is already sorted.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Cyclic Sort <code>O(n)</code> despite an inner loop that can revisit an index?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A swap fires only when it moves a value into a home that did not already hold it, and a placed value never moves again. With <code>n</code> values, at most <code>n − 1</code> swaps occur across the whole run; the outer walk adds <code>n</code> steps. Amortising over "each swap finalises one element" gives <code>O(n)</code>, not <code>O(n²)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What precondition does the method require, and why does it fail on arbitrary arrays?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Pure Cyclic Sort requires a permutation of <code>1..n</code>: every value is in range and unique, so each has exactly one home. Guarded anomaly variants relax uniqueness, and sometimes range membership, by checking collisions or invalid homes before a final mismatch scan. Arbitrary integers have no defined index mapping, so the swap target is undefined.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes the guard <code>a[home] != v</code> rather than <code>i != home</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When a duplicate exists its home already holds an equal value. Comparing values recognises that slot as satisfied, which both terminates the loop and marks the duplicate. Comparing indices never sees the slot as done, so the two equal values swap forever.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What precondition makes binary search on the answer valid, and how is it checked?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The feasibility predicate must be monotone in <code>x</code>, and the supplied upper bound must be feasible. For the minimizing form, <code>feasible</code> has a possibly empty false prefix followed by a true suffix; the search returns the first true value, even when it is <code>lo</code>. Monotonicity is verified by argument, not code: show that increasing the candidate can only make the condition easier to satisfy, so it never reverses. If the predicate can flip back and forth, no boundary exists and the search can discard the half that holds the answer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the time <code>O(n · log(range))</code> rather than tied to the input length the usual way?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The log factor counts probes over the <code>hi − lo + 1</code> integer candidates in <code>[lo, hi]</code>, halving that count each time. Every probe runs <code>feasible</code>, typically an <code>O(n)</code> pass over the input, giving <code>O(n · log(range))</code>. The log is over the value range, so a <code>10^18</code>-wide answer space is still only about 60 probes — which is why the search wins when checking a candidate is far cheaper than computing the optimum directly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the termination differ between an integer answer and a real-valued one?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Over integers, <code>lo &lt; hi</code> with the <code>mid + 1</code> update collapses the interval to one value and stops exactly. Over reals the interval never reaches a single point, so termination comes from a fixed iteration count — roughly 100 halvings drops the interval below double precision — instead of an <code>eps</code> comparison, whose correct value is problem-dependent and can stall on floating-point rounding.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does ternary search not substitute for this pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Ternary search optimises a unimodal objective — a value that rises then falls (or the reverse) with one extremum — by discarding an outer third each step. Binary search on the answer needs a monotone yes/no predicate with one boundary. The preconditions differ: a monotone predicate has no peak to find, and a unimodal objective has no single true/false flip, so neither reduction applies to the other's input.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Search Algorithms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Binary Search require sorted data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The comparison at <code>mid</code> must prove that an entire half cannot contain the target. Ordering supplies that proof: if <code>a[mid] &lt; target</code>, every earlier value is also too small. Without ordering, moving either boundary is a guess and can discard the answer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you find the first occurrence of a duplicated value?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A first-occurrence variant stores <code>mid</code> as the current answer and continues through the left half with <code>right = mid - 1</code>. The stored candidate becomes the result when the range is empty. This removes the plain search's early exit in exchange for a defined duplicate policy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a hash lookup a better fit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hash-based lookup fits repeated exact-match searches where ordering is irrelevant and the additional memory is acceptable. Binary Search retains an advantage when sorted order already exists or supports adjacent operations such as lower bounds, insertion points, and range queries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Linear Search require no precondition on its input?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It tests each element independently and never compares two elements to each other or computes a position, so it needs neither ordering nor an index. The cost of that generality is that it cannot skip any element it has not yet read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the average comparison count for a present versus an absent target?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A present target with uniform position averages <code>(n + 1) / 2</code> comparisons. An absent target always performs all <code>n</code>, so a miss, not a hit, is the true worst case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can no preprocessing beat <code>O(n)</code> for a single search over unsorted data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Any correct method must at least read the elements it rules out, and reading them is the whole cost. With one query there is nothing to amortize a build against, so preprocessing only pays back across repeated searches.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does building an index beat repeated linear scans?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the same collection is searched many times: <code>q</code> scans cost <code>O(n·q)</code>, while a one-time <code>O(n log n)</code> sort plus <a class="internal-link" href="Binary Search">Binary Search</a>, or an <code>O(n)</code> hash build plus average <code>O(1)</code> lookups, amortizes to <code>O(n log n + q log n)</code> or <code>O(n + q)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the first decision before picking a search algorithm?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Check whether data is sorted, because that immediately enables Binary Search.</li>
<li dir="auto">Identify data shape: array, graph, or text stream, because each has specialized methods.</li>
<li dir="auto">Decide whether worst-case guarantees or average speed matters more.</li>
<li dir="auto">Checking these preconditions first avoids picking an algorithm whose assumptions your data violates — the most common source of wrong or slow searches.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is one search algorithm never best for all cases?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Different algorithms optimize for different constraints such as ordering, memory, and preprocessing.</li>
<li dir="auto">Workload shape changes the winner: single lookup, repeated queries, or many patterns.</li>
<li dir="auto">Correctness constraints can force specific methods, for example sorted input for Binary Search.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every choice trades preprocessing and memory against query speed; the senior move is to weigh those for the actual workload instead of reaching for a default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does preprocessing (sorting or indexing) pay off versus a plain linear scan?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A one-off search over unsorted data is just O(n) — sorting first (O(n log n)) would cost more than it saves.</li>
<li dir="auto">Once many queries hit the same data, a single sort or index build is amortized across all of them and each query drops to O(log n) or O(1).</li>
<li dir="auto">Indexes (hash maps, B-trees) trade memory and write cost for fast reads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Preprocessing front-loads cost and memory to make repeated queries cheap, so justify it by query volume, not by instinct.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is exponential search <code>O(log i)</code> rather than <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Doubling stops as soon as <code>bound</code> reaches or passes the target's position <code>i</code>, after about <code>log i</code> steps, and for <code>i &gt; 0</code> the bracket it leaves spans at most <code>i</code> elements, so the closing binary search is another <code>O(log i)</code>. Neither phase inspects the whole array, so the cost tracks the answer's position, not the array length — strictly better than <code>O(log n)</code> when the target is near the front and no worse when it is near the end.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Exponential Search.md" class="internal-link">Exponential Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the high end of the bracket be clamped, and what breaks without it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The final doubling makes <code>bound</code> the first power of two at or beyond the target, so it can land past the last valid index. Bisecting <code>[bound/2, bound]</code> without clamping the upper end to <code>min(bound, n − 1)</code> reads outside the array; on an unknown-length finite source, the probe must report the terminal boundary or safely treat an out-of-range position as greater than the target. <code>bound *= 2</code> can also overflow a 32-bit index into a negative probe.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Exponential Search.md" class="internal-link">Exponential Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the smaller of the two probe values mark a discardable third?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Under strict unimodality <code>f</code> rises to the peak then falls. The probe returning the smaller value sits farther down a slope, on the side away from the peak, so the interval beyond it lies entirely on that slope and cannot contain the maximum. Two probes are needed because a single point on a non-monotone function cannot reveal which side the peak is on.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does golden-section search change, and when does it matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It places the probes at the golden ratio so one probe of each step reuses an evaluation from the previous step. Golden-section retains ≈0.618 of the interval per step versus ternary's 0.667 — a slightly <em>faster</em> shrink — while reusing one of the two probe evaluations, so it costs one new function call per step instead of two. It dominates ternary on both axes at once whenever evaluating <code>f</code> is expensive — a simulation or a measurement rather than an array read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What input makes the discard rule return a wrong answer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A non-unimodal function. With two humps, probes straddling the valley can discard the third holding the global maximum and return a local one. A flat maximum still permits finding any maximizer, but it cannot support a unique-peak contract or recover the entire maximizing interval without extra boundary work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What property of a value distribution forces the linear worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A value distribution where ratios do not predict index ratios. In <code>a[i] = 2^i</code>, choosing the target near <code>max/n</code> places it at index <code>n − 1 − Θ(log n)</code> while the first interpolation fraction is only about <code>1/n</code>, so early probes stay near <code>lo</code>. The range then advances by only a few positions per probe and can require <code>Θ(n)</code> work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Interpolation Search.md" class="internal-link">Interpolation Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can it not run on arbitrary comparable keys?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The probe computes <code>(target - a[lo]) * (hi - lo) / (a[hi] - a[lo])</code>, which needs subtraction and a ratio with numeric meaning. Ordering-only types such as strings under a custom comparator support comparison but not that arithmetic, so no position can be estimated and only comparison-based search applies.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Interpolation Search.md" class="internal-link">Interpolation Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>m = √n</code> the block size that minimizes total work?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The cost is <code>n/m</code> jumps to reach the target's block plus up to <code>m</code> steps to scan it, so <code>f(m) = n/m + m</code>. Its derivative <code>−n/m² + 1</code> is zero at <code>m = √n</code>, where the two phases are equal and the total is <code>2√n</code>. Larger blocks lengthen the scan; smaller blocks multiply the jumps. A constant block size leaves the jump phase linear in <code>n</code>, so the bound degrades to <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Jump Search.md" class="internal-link">Jump Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What breaks when Jump Search runs on unsorted input?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The jump phase assumes <code>a[block] &lt; target</code> proves the target lies further ahead, which requires monotonic order. On unsorted data a block end can exceed the target while the matching value sits in an earlier, already-skipped block, so the scan examines the wrong block. The failure is a silent false negative rather than a crash.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Jump Search.md" class="internal-link">Jump Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>String Matching</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is search cost <code>Θ(n + z)</code> independent of the number of patterns?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All patterns share one trie, so common prefixes collapse into shared paths and the text drives a single walk through the automaton. Each character makes one forward move plus failure hops that amortize to <code>O(1)</code> over the whole scan, and the output walk emits exactly the <code>z</code> matches found. Nothing in the loop scales with <code>k</code>, the pattern count, so a thousand signatures cost the same per character as one.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a failure link point to, and what invariant holds after reading <code>i</code> characters?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A state's failure link points to the state for the longest proper suffix of its string that is still a prefix of some pattern — KMP's longest-prefix-suffix relation lifted onto the trie. That link maintains the invariant that after <code>i</code> characters the automaton sits at the state whose string is the longest suffix of the first <code>i</code> characters that is a prefix of some pattern, which is precisely the state from which every match ending at <code>i</code> can be read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do output links add, and what fails silently without them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single state can complete several patterns when a shorter one is a suffix of a longer one, such as <code>he</code> ending inside <code>she</code>. Output links chain each state to the nearest shorter pattern that also ends there, and walking the chain enumerates every simultaneous match. Without them the scan is still correctly positioned but reports only the pattern the current node ends, silently dropping the nested ones — the classic loss of <code>he</code> when matching <code>{ he, she, hers }</code> in <code>ushers</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does adding a pattern after construction force a rebuild?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Failure and output links encode suffix relationships across the entire pattern set and are resolved together by the single construction BFS. A new pattern can change the longest-suffix target of many existing states, so the links are no longer valid and must be recomputed. That is why the automaton fits a dictionary built once and reused across texts rather than one that changes per query.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the text index never move backward, and what does that buy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">On a mismatch the algorithm only lowers the match length <code>j</code> via <code>π[j-1]</code>; it never decrements the text index <code>i</code>. Because <code>j</code> can fall back at most as much as it climbed, total comparisons stay at <code>2n</code>, giving the <code>Θ(n + m)</code> bound. A monotonic text pointer also lets the search run over a stream that cannot be rewound.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>π[j]</code> encode, and how is it used on a mismatch?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>π[j]</code> is the length of the longest proper prefix of <code>pattern[0..j]</code> that is also a suffix of it. On a mismatch after matching <code>j</code> characters, <code>j</code> resets to <code>π[j-1]</code>, which realigns that shared prefix/suffix against the text so no already-matched characters are re-read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">On what input does KMP's guarantee actually pay off, and where does it gain nothing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It pays off on repetitive input such as <code>text = aⁿ</code>, <code>pattern = aᵐ⁻¹b</code>, where naive search degrades to <code>Θ(n·m)</code> while KMP stays <code>Θ(n + m)</code>. It gains nothing on large alphabets: unlike Boyer-Moore it reads essentially every character and cannot skip.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the standard bug when building the failure table?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Resetting the length pointer to <code>0</code> on a mismatch instead of falling back through <code>failure[k-1]</code>. That corrupts entries where the prefix overlaps itself — <code>AABAAAB</code> builds as <code>[0,1,0,1,2,1,0]</code> rather than <code>[0,1,0,1,2,2,3]</code> — and the search then misses matches that depend on the longer overlap.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does comparing right-to-left let Boyer-Moore skip characters it never reads?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A mismatch at the pattern's last position exposes a text character together with its offset. If that character is absent from the pattern, no alignment that places any pattern character over it can match, so the pattern jumps clear past it — up to <code>m</code> positions — without comparing the characters in between. Left-to-right scanning learns nothing about the text ahead, so it can never justify a jump larger than one on the same information.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does each shift rule contribute, and why do most implementations drop the good-suffix rule?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The bad-character rule aligns the pattern's rightmost copy of the mismatching text character, giving large skips on large alphabets. The good-suffix rule reuses an already-matched suffix, which helps on repetitive patterns the bad-character rule handles poorly. The algorithm takes the larger shift, so it is never worse than either alone. The good-suffix table's index arithmetic is error-prone for a small real gain, so production code such as GNU <code>grep</code>'s fixed-string search ships Boyer-Moore-Horspool with the bad-character rule only.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the plain worst case <code>O(n·m)</code>, and what recovers a linear bound?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The sublinear behavior depends on frequent large skips, which vanish when text and pattern share long repeated runs — as with an all-equal pattern <code>aaaa</code> over <code>aaaa…a</code>, where every alignment is a full match over all <code>m</code> characters and the good-suffix rule then shifts by only one. Galil's rule remembers how much of the pattern is already known to match after a shift and skips re-comparing those positions, bounding total comparisons at <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does KMP never re-examine a text character after a mismatch?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Its prefix (failure) function records, for every pattern position, the length of the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix. On a mismatch the pattern shifts so that this already-matched prefix aligns, so the text pointer only ever advances. That is what turns the naive <code>O(n·m)</code> scan into <code>O(n + m)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/String Matching.md" class="internal-link">String Matching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Boyer-Moore's <code>O(n/m)</code> best case actually realised, and when does it degrade?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Sublinear scanning appears with long patterns over large alphabets, where a mismatched character usually isn't in the pattern at all and the bad-character rule shifts by nearly the full pattern length. It degrades toward <code>O(n·m)</code> on small alphabets or highly repetitive text; the Galil rule caps the worst case at <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/String Matching.md" class="internal-link">String Matching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why choose Rabin–Karp over a linear automaton method?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because it fingerprints windows of the text with a rolling hash rather than building an automaton, it extends naturally where the automaton methods don't: matching many equal-length patterns at once (compare each window's hash against a set), plagiarism detection, and 2-D pattern matching. The price is that a hash collision forces a character-by-character verification, so its worst case is <code>O(n·m)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/String Matching.md" class="internal-link">String Matching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is Aho-Corasick related to KMP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It is KMP lifted from a single pattern to a set. The patterns are stored in a trie, and each node's failure link points to the longest proper suffix that is a prefix of some pattern — the multi-pattern analogue of KMP's failure function. One pass over the text then reports every occurrence of every pattern in <code>O(n + Σmᵢ + matches)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/String Matching.md" class="internal-link">String Matching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does sliding the window keep the hash update at <code>O(1)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The window hash is a base-<code>b</code> polynomial mod <code>p</code>. Moving one position right subtracts the outgoing character's weighted term <code>T[i]·b^(m-1)</code>, multiplies by <code>b</code> to shift the rest up one place, and adds the incoming <code>T[i+m]</code> — a fixed count of modular operations, independent of <code>m</code>. Recomputing from the <code>m</code> characters instead would make each step <code>O(m)</code> and the scan <code>O(nm)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a hash match still require a character comparison?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The hash maps <code>m</code>-character strings onto residues mod <code>p</code>, a many-to-one map, so two different windows can share a value. A match on the hash means only that the strings might be equal; the <code>O(m)</code> character check confirms it and stops a collision from being reported as a match.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What turns the expected <code>Θ(n + m)</code> into the <code>Θ(nm)</code> worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A hash match at almost every position, each forcing an <code>O(m)</code> verification. It arises with genuine matches everywhere (text <code>aaaa</code> searched for <code>aa</code>) or with a weak or small modulus that makes collisions frequent. A large prime modulus keeps spurious matches rare, which is the low-collision assumption behind the average bound.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>z[i]</code> measure, and why does the Z-box keep the whole pass linear?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>z[i]</code> is the length of the longest substring starting at index <code>i</code> that also matches a prefix of the string. The box <code>[l, r]</code> is the match interval with the largest <code>r</code>; a position inside it copies its value from the mirror <code>z[i-l]</code> when that mirror ends before the edge, spending no comparisons. Direct comparisons occur only while extending past <code>r</code>, and each either fails once or pushes <code>r</code> one step right. Since <code>r</code> never retreats and stops at <code>|S| - 1</code>, total comparisons are <code>Θ(|S|)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should the concatenation separator lie outside the input alphabet?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">To keep the cap: a separator absent from <code>P</code> and <code>T</code> holds every text-region <code>z[i]</code> to at most <code>|P|</code>, so <code>z[i] == |P|</code> and <code>z[i] &gt;= |P|</code> coincide and no match spans the pattern/text join. If the separator also appears in the input, a genuine occurrence can extend across the join and produce <code>z[i] &gt; |P|</code> — a strict <code>== |P|</code> test would then drop it. The shipped <code>&gt;= |P|</code> test survives this (a text-region <code>z[i] &gt;= |P|</code> is always <code>|P|</code> real characters of <code>T</code> matching <code>P</code>); a sentinel outside the alphabet is what lets the simpler <code>==</code> formulation stay correct too.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Inside the box, when can <code>z[i]</code> be copied from the mirror, and when must it be recomputed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When <code>z[i-l] &lt; r - i + 1</code> the mirrored match ends strictly before the box edge, so it is fully verified and <code>z[i] = z[i-l]</code>. When <code>z[i-l] &gt;= r - i + 1</code> the mirror only guarantees a match up to <code>r</code>; the characters past <code>r</code> were never compared, so <code>z[i]</code> is reset to the box remainder <code>r - i + 1</code> and extended from <code>r + 1</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/String Matching/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Sorting Algorithms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the <code>swapped</code> flag detect, and what does omitting it cost?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A pass that completes with no swap means no adjacent pair is out of order, so the array is sorted and the loop can stop. On already-sorted input this ends the sort after one <code>O(n)</code> pass. Without the flag the double loop always runs its full <code>Θ(n²)</code> comparisons, so sorted input costs as much as random input and the <code>O(n)</code> best case is gone.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bubble Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bubble Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a large value reach its final slot in one pass while a small value at the tail takes many?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A left-to-right pass carries the running maximum forward through consecutive swaps, so a large value can cross the whole array in a single pass. The same pass only ever compares a given element with its left neighbor once, so a small value ("turtle") near the end moves toward the front by at most one index per pass and needs about one pass per position it must travel.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bubble Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bubble Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is bubble sort stable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A swap happens only on a strict <code>a[i] &gt; a[i+1]</code>. Equal keys never satisfy that test, so they are never exchanged and their original relative order is preserved through every pass.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bubble Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bubble Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does the <code>Θ(n)</code> average bound actually come from?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">From the assumption that keys are roughly uniform over a known range with <code>m ≈ n</code> buckets. Under it, each bucket holds <code>O(1)</code> elements in expectation, so the sum of per-bucket sort costs <code>Σ tᵢ²</code> is <code>Θ(n)</code>. The bound is conditional: it is a property of the input distribution, not of the algorithm alone.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the sorted buckets be concatenated with no comparison between buckets?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bucket <code>i</code> covers a strictly lower slice of the range than bucket <code>i + 1</code>, so every key in one bucket is smaller than every key in the next by construction. Reading internally sorted buckets in index order therefore emits a globally sorted sequence, and no per-bucket sort ever inspects a key outside its own bucket.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What input drives Bucket Sort to <code>Θ(n²)</code>, and why is the result still correct?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A skewed distribution — Zipfian, exponential, or duplicate-heavy — that drops most keys into one bucket. That bucket's inner <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a> gains nothing from the partition and costs <code>Θ(n²)</code>. The partition still separates ranges correctly, so the output stays sorted; only the running time collapses.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Counting Sort avoid the <code>Ω(n log n)</code> comparison lower bound?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The bound counts the yes/no answers a comparison sort needs to separate <code>n!</code> orderings, and a pairwise comparison is its only source of information. Counting Sort never compares keys — it reads each key's value directly as an array index — so the argument does not apply to it. That indexing costs <code>Θ(n + k)</code> and only works for integer keys over a bounded range.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes the placement pass stable, and when does that matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">After the prefix sum, <code>count[v]</code> is one past the last slot for value <code>v</code>. Consuming the input tail-first and decrementing before each write puts the last-seen equal key in the highest slot of its block and earlier ones beneath it, preserving input order. A forward pass reverses equal keys. Stability is optional standalone but required when Counting Sort is the digit pass inside LSD <a class="internal-link" href="Radix Sort">Radix Sort</a>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the cost <code>Θ(n + k)</code> in every case rather than <code>O(n + k)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The two input scans and the prefix-sum sweep run to completion regardless of input order, so no arrangement adds or removes work. Lower and upper bounds coincide, which makes the notation tight — <code>Θ</code>. The only lever on cost is <code>k</code>, the key range, not the data.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a small <code>n</code> still make Counting Sort the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When <code>k ≫ n</code>. The count array holds one cell per representable key value, not per element, so sorting eight 64-bit integers by raw value needs a <code>2^64</code>-cell array. The <code>+ k</code> term dominates and the allocation fails. Radix Sort keeps each digit pass's range small; a comparison sort removes the range dependence altogether.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does build-heap cost <code>O(n)</code> rather than <code>O(n log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bottom-up sift-down moves each node down only as far as its own subtree height. Most nodes are near the leaves and barely descend; only the few near the root can travel <code>log n</code>. Summing height × count across the levels converges to <code>O(n)</code>. Inserting <code>n</code> elements one at a time, by contrast, pays up to <code>O(log n)</code> each and totals <code>O(n log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort.md" class="internal-link">Heap Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does heap sort's instability come from?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The extraction swaps. Moving the root to the end and sifting a new root down relocates elements by heap geometry, not by input order, so two equal keys can be swapped past each other with nothing to restore their original sequence. Merge sort's merge step, choosing the left element on ties, keeps equal keys in input order.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort.md" class="internal-link">Heap Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What keeps the prefix sorted after each insertion?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The inner loop shifts every prefix element greater than the key one slot right and stops at the first element <code>&lt;= key</code>. The key is written into that gap, so nothing to its left is larger and everything to its right was already ordered; <code>a[0..j]</code> is sorted for the next step.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is reverse-sorted input the worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each key is smaller than every element already placed, so it shifts the entire prefix before landing at the front. The shifts sum to <code>n(n-1)/2</code>, making the run <code>O(n²)</code>—the maximum possible number of inversions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does binary insertion sort not lower the asymptotic time?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Binary search finds the insertion slot in <code>O(log j)</code> comparisons, but the elements between that slot and the key must still be shifted right one at a time. The shifts stay <code>O(n²)</code>, so the total is unchanged; only comparison-heavy workloads gain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do Timsort and Introsort fall back to insertion sort on small partitions?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">On a few dozen elements the quadratic term is small and bounded, while insertion sort allocates nothing, accesses memory sequentially, and reaches <code>O(n)</code> on the nearly-sorted runs those algorithms produce. Below the crossover it beats the recursion and constant-factor overhead of an <code>O(n log n)</code> sort.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is merge sort <code>O(n log n)</code> on every input, with no faster best case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The split is by position, not by value, so the recursion tree has the same <code>⌈log₂ n⌉</code> levels for sorted, reversed, or random input, and every level merges all n elements. Element values only decide which read head is taken at each step, not how many merges run, and there is no early-exit test on an already-sorted input.<br />
