<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What single line makes the array merge stable, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The comparison <code>a[i] &lt;= a[j]</code>, which emits the left run's element on a tie. The left run holds elements that appeared earlier in the original array, so taking it first preserves the original order of equal keys. Using <code>a[i] &lt; a[j]</code> instead emits the right element on ties and makes the sort unstable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a linked-list merge sort need only <code>O(1)</code> extra space while the array version needs <code>O(n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A linked-list merge re-links existing nodes by pointer, so it never copies elements into a scratch array and never needs index access to <code>a[mid]</code>. The array merge must write its output somewhere while both inputs are still being read, which forces an <code>O(n)</code> buffer. Both still carry <code>O(log n)</code> recursion-stack space.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does an in-place block merge trade to remove the <code>O(n)</code> buffer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It keeps the <code>O(n log n)</code> time bound but replaces the single linear copy with rotations and block swaps, adding a constant-factor slowdown and making stability harder to preserve. The buffer exists to avoid overwriting unread input; removing it means doing that bookkeeping with in-place moves instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the two sides of a partition be sorted without ever combining them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Partitioning places the pivot at its final sorted index and guarantees every element to its left is not greater and every element to its right is greater. No element on one side belongs on the other, so the two subranges are independent sorting problems. Correct placement of each pivot is the only merge step quick sort performs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Quick Sort.md" class="internal-link">Quick Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What input drives quick sort to <code>O(n²)</code> with a first- or last-element pivot, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Two different inputs, failing for two different reasons. Ordered data — already-sorted or reverse-sorted — makes a fixed first- or last-element pivot the extreme of its range, so each partition peels off one element into an <code>n − 1</code> side; <code>n</code> levels of <code>O(n)</code> work give <code>O(n²)</code>. A randomized or median-of-three pivot removes that case. Input dominated by one repeated key degenerates independently of the pivot: Lomuto sends every element <code>≤ pivot</code> to the left, so identical keys pile onto one side no matter which pivot is chosen — randomization does not help, and three-way (Dutch-flag) partitioning is the fix.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Quick Sort.md" class="internal-link">Quick Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is quick sort's worst-case stack <code>O(n)</code>, and how is it bounded to <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Degenerate partitions nest the recursion <code>n</code> deep, and a naive version that recurses into both sides holds all those frames. Recursing into the smaller side first and iterating on the larger (tail-call elimination) keeps at most <code>O(log n)</code> frames live, because the smaller side is at most half the range.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Quick Sort.md" class="internal-link">Quick Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must each per-digit pass be stable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each pass sorts on one digit and trusts that ties on that digit are already ordered by the less-significant digits sorted in earlier passes. A stable counting sort preserves that established order; an unstable one reorders the ties and destroys the work of every prior pass, producing output that is sorted only on the final digit. Stability is a correctness requirement here, not an optimization.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Radix Sort.md" class="internal-link">Radix Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a comparison sort beat Radix Sort?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When keys are wide, variable-length without a bound, or not decomposable into digits. The <code>d</code> passes carry a real cost, so once <code>d</code> grows relative to <code>log₂ n</code> — long strings, big integers — a tuned <a class="internal-link" href="Quick Sort">Quick Sort</a> wins on wall-clock time. Keys exposed only through a comparator have no digit to bucket on, so radix does not apply at all.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Radix Sort.md" class="internal-link">Radix Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are the comparisons <code>Θ(n²)</code> even on already-sorted input?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The suffix scan is unconditional: pass <code>i</code> compares the running minimum against all <code>n − 1 − i</code> remaining elements, with no early-exit test and no check for existing order. The <code>n(n−1)/2</code> total depends only on <code>n</code>, not on arrangement, so a sorted array costs exactly what a reversed one does. The algorithm is non-adaptive.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Selection Sort.md" class="internal-link">Selection Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is standard selection sort unstable, and what does the stable fix cost?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The swap that places the suffix minimum can carry an equal-keyed element across its partner: <code>[5a, 3, 5b, 1]</code> becomes <code>[1, 3, 5b, 5a]</code>, reversing the two 5s. Restoring stability means shifting the intervening elements instead of swapping, which raises writes to <code>Θ(n)</code> per pass and forfeits the linear-write property that was the reason to use it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Selection Sort.md" class="internal-link">Selection Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between Merge Sort and Quick Sort in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Merge sort gives reliable <code>O(n log n)</code> worst-case behavior and stable ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">Quick sort is often faster in practice on in-memory arrays due to cache behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">Quick sort has worst-case <code>O(n^2)</code> if pivot strategy is poor, so randomized or introspective variants are safer.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why it matters: this choice affects latency tail risk, memory usage, and correctness when stable ordering is required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Insertion Sort still a good choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It is strong on very small arrays because constant overhead is tiny.</li>
<li dir="auto">It performs well on nearly sorted data where shifts are minimal.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is commonly used as a base case inside hybrid production sort implementations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why it matters: knowing this avoids overengineering and explains hybrid sort internals in interviews.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does .NET's built-in Array.Sort use, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">.NET uses an introspective sort (IntroSort): it starts with Quick Sort for fast average performance, switches to Heap Sort when recursion depth exceeds a threshold (to guarantee O(n log n) worst case), and uses Insertion Sort for small partitions (to exploit its low overhead on nearly-sorted data).<br />
This hybrid approach demonstrates why production sort implementations combine multiple algorithms rather than using a single one.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Tim sort reach <code>Θ(n)</code> on some inputs while its worst case is still <code>Θ(n log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Run detection scans for maximal ascending or strictly-descending stretches and merges only across their boundaries. An already-ordered array (ascending, or descending and reversed in place) is a single run, so the scan finishes in one <code>Θ(n)</code> pass with no merges. Unstructured input yields <code>~n / minrun</code> short runs that still merge across <code>~log n</code> balanced levels, giving <code>Θ(n log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do the run-stack size invariants guarantee, and what breaks without them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For the top three run lengths <code>X, Y, Z</code> (Z deepest), Tim sort keeps <code>Z &gt; Y + X</code> and <code>Y &gt; X</code>, merging when either fails. This bounds the size ratio of adjacent runs so every merge joins near-equal lengths, which is what caps total merge work at <code>Θ(n log n)</code>. Without the bound, a merge could repeatedly join a tiny run into a huge one and drift toward quadratic cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What did the 2015 formal-verification effort reveal, and how did it tie back to the run stack?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">de Gouw et al. proved the merge-collapse routine only restored the invariant among the top runs, leaving deeper violations reachable by a crafted run-length sequence. Because the run-length stack was pre-sized assuming the invariant held, the violation could overflow it into an <code>ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException</code>. The stopgap enlarged the stack; the real fix widened the invariant check to also test the run below the top three.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must descending-run detection use strict <code>&gt;</code> rather than <code>&gt;=</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A descending run is reversed in place. If detection used <code>&gt;=</code>, it would reverse stretches of equal keys and silently swap their relative order, breaking stability. Strict descent guarantees equal keys never sit inside a run that gets reversed, so the reversal preserves input order.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes introsort switch to heap sort, and why is <code>2⌊log₂ n⌋</code> the threshold?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The switch fires when the current partition's recursion depth reaches <code>2⌊log₂ n⌋</code>. Balanced quicksort bottoms out near <code>⌊log₂ n⌋</code> levels, so twice that depth is reached only when partitions stay badly unbalanced — the path toward <code>O(n²)</code>. Finishing that partition with heap sort caps its cost at <code>O(n log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does introsort provide no general stability guarantee?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Quicksort partitioning and heap sort may both move equal keys past one another. An insertion-only small input can preserve their order, but once either other strategy runs the order is not guaranteed, so callers cannot rely on stability across input sizes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why doesn't an input that is bad for the pivot rule always trigger the fallback?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The depth limit reacts to cumulative recursion depth, not to any single partition's balance. Imbalance that never sustains past <code>2⌊log₂ n⌋</code> levels stays in the quicksort phase and is sorted at quicksort's normal constants; the fallback bounds sustained degeneration, not one bad split.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">The heap-sort branch almost never runs — why keep it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Runtimes sort untrusted input, so quicksort's <code>O(n²)</code> path is a denial-of-service vector. The branch existing at all is what turns the bound into a contract: whether or not it fires on a given input, no input can exceed <code>O(n log n)</code>. The ceiling is a correctness property, not a speedup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Shell sort faster than a plain insertion sort when its final pass is a full insertion sort?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The coarse gap passes move far-out-of-place elements <code>h</code> slots at a time, so most long-distance disorder is cleared before the <code>h = 1</code> pass runs. Insertion sort is near-linear on nearly-sorted input, so the final pass has little left to shift. The speedup comes from relocating <em>where</em> the shift work happens — into cheap coarse passes — not from a cheaper comparison.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the gap sequence, rather than the input, decide Shell sort's complexity class?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A pass only guarantees the array is <code>h</code>-sorted, and how much disorder survives into the next pass depends on how the gaps interleave positions. For power-of-two lengths, Shell's <code>n/2^k</code> schedule keeps every gap even until the last, so parity classes do not mix and the worst case is <code>Θ(n²)</code>; Hibbard reaches <code>Θ(n^1.5)</code>; Ciura's tuned gaps are fast in measurements but have no tight nontrivial bound. The sequence is a tunable parameter, so Shell sort is a family of algorithms rather than one.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Shell sort unstable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A shift relocates an element by a whole gap <code>h</code>, so it can carry a key past an equal key that lies between them. No later pass records or restores their original relative order, so records that compare equal can emerge reversed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can Shell sort not offer a contractual <code>O(n log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The sequences with the best proven worst-case bounds are not the fastest: Pratt's 3-smooth gaps prove <code>Θ(n log² n)</code> but run slowly due to many passes, while the fast Ciura sequence has no tight nontrivial bound, and the optimal general sequence is an open problem. A workload needing a guaranteed bound uses <a class="internal-link" href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort" aria-label="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort" data-tooltip-position="top">heap sort</a> or <a class="internal-link" href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort" aria-label="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort" data-tooltip-position="top">introsort</a> instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a turtle, and how does the gap remove it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A turtle is a small value near the end of the array. A bubble-style adjacent pass moves it left one position at a time, so it needs <code>O(n)</code> passes to reach the front — the asymmetry that keeps bubble sort quadratic. A wide initial gap compares distant pairs, so one swap can carry the turtle up to <code>gap</code> positions toward the front, and the shrinking gap then resolves the remaining local disorder.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Comb Sort.md" class="internal-link">Comb Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is comb sort's sub-quadratic behavior not a guarantee?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Measurements on random input can look near <code>O(n log n)</code> with a <code>1.3</code> shrink factor, but that is not a proven average-case bound. No structural argument prevents <code>Θ(n²)</code>, and adversarial inputs still reach it. The combsort11 special case for gaps of <code>9</code> and <code>10</code> exists precisely because the constant was tuned by experiment rather than derived.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Comb Sort.md" class="internal-link">Comb Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="computer-science-data-structures">Data Structures</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a data structure? Which ones do you know? Which of them exist in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A data structure is a way to organize related data into a collection-like object. Examples include arrays, lists, queues, stacks, linked lists, dictionaries/hash tables, hash sets, graphs, and trees. .NET provides built-in implementations for many of these (for example <code>Array</code>, <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Stack&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code>, <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code>, and <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> when you need ordered, index-based access and the primary operations are iteration or positional lookup. Use <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code> when you need fast lookup, insertion, and deletion by a unique key. Use <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code> when you only need membership testing and set operations (union, intersection, difference) without associated values.<br />
The wrong choice shows up as O(n) scans that should be O(1) lookups.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does collection choice matter more than micro-optimization?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Switching from O(n) linear search to O(1) hash lookup reduces work by orders of magnitude at scale. No amount of loop unrolling or SIMD on the O(n) path matches that.<br />
Focus on algorithmic complexity first, then optimize constant factors within the chosen structure if profiling shows it matters.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you use <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> over <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Almost never in practice. <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> (backed by a contiguous array) has better cache locality, lower memory overhead per element, and faster iteration. <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> only wins when you need frequent insertions/deletions in the middle of a very large collection and already hold a reference to the node.<br />
In most .NET code, <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> is the correct default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Composite Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the recency list be doubly linked rather than singly linked?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A <code>get</code> promotes a node from the middle of the list to the head, which means unlinking it in <code>O(1)</code>. Splicing a node out needs its predecessor. A doubly-linked node exposes <code>prev</code> directly, so the rewrite is constant time. A singly-linked list would scan from the head to find the predecessor — <code>O(n)</code> — collapsing the cache's constant-time guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do the map and the list divide the work, and why store the key inside the node?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The map answers "where is key <code>k</code>" in <code>O(1)</code>; the list answers "what is the recency order" and supports <code>O(1)</code> move-to-head and remove-from-tail. Eviction starts from the tail <em>node</em> and must delete the matching map entry, so the node carries its own key to recover it without a reverse lookup from node to key.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What corruption results from updating only one of the two structures on eviction?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Removing the tail node but leaving its key in the map produces stale keys that resolve to evicted nodes — false hits and an unreachable node that leaks. Deleting the map entry but leaving the node linked produces an orphan that occupies a recency slot yet can never be looked up, permanently reducing effective capacity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is every LRU operation worst-case <code>O(1)</code> rather than amortized, and where does that break down?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No operation traverses the list: the map turns lookup into a hash probe and the tail sentinel makes the victim a pointer read, so each op is a fixed number of rewrites. The one dependency is the map's own lookup, which degrades to <code>O(n)</code> under adversarial hash collisions — the same caveat as the underlying hash map.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Graph Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a disjoint set represented in memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A parent array stores a forest: <code>parent[i]</code> is the next index toward the representative, and each root points to itself. Rank or size is stored in a parallel array for roots. No linked node objects are required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>Union</code> link roots rather than the original elements?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A root represents an entire existing set. Linking one root under another merges both complete sets. Re-parenting an interior node can move only its subtree and leave other members behind, breaking the partition semantics.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the useful bound amortized rather than worst-case constant time?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">One <code>Find</code> can still traverse several parent indices. Path compression pays extra writes during that operation so later finds become shorter. Across a sequence, the total work is <code>O(m α(n))</code> for <code>m</code> operations, even though a particular operation is not guaranteed to be constant time.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a disjoint set beat BFS for connectivity, and what do you give up?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When edges arrive over time and connectivity queries interleave with insertions: each union/find is O(α(n)) ≈ O(1), versus O(V + E) to re-traverse per query. You give up everything except component identity — no paths, no distances, no directed reachability, and merges are irreversible (no edge deletion).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph Structures.md" class="internal-link">Graph Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the adjacency list make neighbor iteration cheap but the adjacency matrix make edge existence cheap?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The list physically stores only <code>u</code>'s actual neighbors, so enumerating them is <code>O(deg u)</code> with nothing wasted, but confirming a specific edge means scanning that list. The matrix reserves a fixed cell for every ordered pair, so <code>[u, v]</code> is a single <code>O(1)</code> index, but reading all of <code>u</code>'s neighbors means scanning a full row of <code>V</code> cells, most of them empty on a sparse graph.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes the adjacency matrix a bad fit for a sparse graph?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Its space is <code>O(V²)</code> regardless of how many edges exist. A graph with 10 000 vertices and 50 000 edges stores ~60 000 list entries but 100 million matrix cells, nearly all sentinel values. The matrix charges for every possible edge, so it only breaks even when <code>E</code> approaches <code>V²</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is an undirected edge encoded in each representation, and why does that matter for mutation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The adjacency list stores it twice, as mirrored entries in both endpoints' lists; the matrix stores it as two symmetric cells <code>[u, v]</code> and <code>[v, u]</code>; the edge list stores one tuple read in both directions. For the list and matrix, symmetry is a maintained invariant — removing or updating the edge must touch both stored copies, or the graph silently becomes directed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is an edge list the right storage despite its <code>O(E)</code> edge test?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the algorithm consumes every edge in a single pass and never queries an individual edge — relaxing all edges in Bellman-Ford, or sorting edges by weight for Kruskal's MST. Both want a flat, iterable edge set; neither benefits from per-vertex indexing, so the edge list's weakness never triggers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does union by rank alone bound tree height at <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A root's rank increases only when two trees of equal rank merge, so a tree of rank <code>r</code> holds at least <code>2^r</code> nodes. With <code>n</code> nodes, no rank — and therefore no height — can exceed <code>log₂ n</code>. Attaching the lower-rank root under the higher one never lengthens the taller tree's longest path.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Union-Find.md" class="internal-link">Union-Find</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the <code>O(α(n))</code> cost amortized rather than a single-operation guarantee?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single <code>find</code> can still traverse an <code>O(log n)</code> parent chain. What path compression buys is that the writes it performs during that walk flatten the path, so later finds on those nodes are cheap. The near-constant figure is the total work over a sequence of <code>m</code> operations divided across them, not a bound on any one call.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Union-Find.md" class="internal-link">Union-Find</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does path compression make a structure unsuitable for rollback?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Compression rewrites the parent of every node on a walked path, erasing the forest's earlier shape. There is no record of what a node pointed to before, so a merge cannot be reversed. Rollback DSU keeps union by rank but omits compression so each <code>union</code> mutates exactly one parent-and-rank pair, which it can then undo.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Union-Find.md" class="internal-link">Union-Find</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the variant chosen change the cost of <code>union</code> and <code>find</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Quick-find gives <code>O(1)</code> finds but <code>O(n)</code> unions; plain quick-union is <code>O(n)</code> for both in the worst case; union by rank alone makes both <code>O(log n)</code>; rank plus path compression drops both to <code>O(α(n))</code> amortized while leaving the single-operation worst case at <code>O(log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Union-Find.md" class="internal-link">Union-Find</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Hash-based Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a Bloom filter produce false positives but never false negatives?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Add</code> only ever sets bits to 1 and never clears them, so every bit a present element touched is still 1 and that element always passes its query — no false negatives. A queried element's <em>k</em> bits can all have been set to 1 by other elements, though, which makes a never-added element report "possibly present": a false positive.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the standard filter forbid deletion, and what changes to allow it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No bit belongs to a single element; bits are shared across everything that hashed to them. Clearing an element's bits could clear one another present element depends on, turning its next query into a false "definitely absent". A counting Bloom filter stores a small counter per slot instead of one bit, so a remove decrements rather than clears, at several times the space.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do <em>m</em> and <em>k</em> control, and what is the optimal <em>k</em>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em>m</em> is the bit-array size and <em>k</em> the number of hash functions. The false-positive rate is <code>p ≈ (1 − e^(−kn/m))^k</code> for <em>n</em> inserted elements. For a fixed <code>m/n</code>, <code>k = (m/n)·ln 2</code> minimises <em>p</em>, driving about half the bits to 1; larger <em>m</em> lowers <em>p</em> by adding room, while <em>k</em> balances too few probes against filling the bits too fast.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the space <code>O(m)</code> bits rather than <code>O(n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The filter stores no elements — only the <em>m</em>-bit array and <em>k</em> hash functions. Its footprint is fixed at construction and does not grow with <em>n</em>, which is why it can track billions of keys in megabytes. The cost of discarding the keys is the false-positive rate and the loss of enumeration, retrieval, and deletion.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do "open hashing" and "open addressing" mean opposite things?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They name different axes. "Open hashing" describes the <em>storage</em>: chaining's per-bucket lists grow open-endedly outside the array. "Open addressing" describes the <em>key's address</em>: the key may land in a slot other than its home, so its address is open. Chaining is open hashing but <em>closed</em> addressing (the bucket is fixed); probing is closed hashing (fixed array) but <em>open</em> addressing (the slot drifts).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Collision Resolution.md" class="internal-link">Collision Resolution</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a load factor exceed 1 with chaining but not with open addressing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Chaining stores entries in lists outside the array, so the array can hold more entries than it has slots — <code>α &gt; 1</code> just means the average chain is longer than one. Open addressing stores every entry <em>in</em> the array, so it cannot hold more entries than slots; it needs empty slots to terminate probe sequences, and its cost blows up as <code>α → 1</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Collision Resolution.md" class="internal-link">Collision Resolution</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does deleting from an open-addressed table need a tombstone?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A lookup follows a probe chain until it hits an empty slot, which it reads as "not present". If a delete simply emptied a slot in the middle of a chain, every key that had probed past it would become unreachable — the empty slot would end the search early. A tombstone marks the slot as "deleted, keep probing", preserving the chain; a later rehash clears the accumulated tombstones.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Collision Resolution.md" class="internal-link">Collision Resolution</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes bucketed hashing fast for on-disk and SIMD tables?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The bucket is sized to the expensive access unit — a disk page or a cache line — so one I/O or memory fetch brings in <code>B</code> slots at once and scanning them is nearly free. A bucket also absorbs up to <code>B</code> collisions before any overflow logic runs, so local hot spots that would cause long probe runs in a flat table stay contained in one block.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Collision Resolution.md" class="internal-link">Collision Resolution</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the <code>O(1)</code> membership bound an average rather than a guarantee?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It assumes the hash spreads elements roughly uniformly and the load factor caps expected chain length at a constant. When many elements collide into one bucket — weak <code>hashCode</code> or adversarial keys — that bucket becomes a linear list and <code>Contains</code>/<code>Add</code>/<code>Remove</code> degrade to <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash Set.md" class="internal-link">Hash Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a member become unreachable after insertion?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Membership routes an element to a bucket via <code>hashCode</code>, then confirms with <code>Equals</code>. Mutating a field that participates in <code>hashCode</code> after adding leaves the element in its original bucket while lookups probe the new one, so <code>Contains</code> returns <code>false</code> on an element that is still stored.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash Set.md" class="internal-link">Hash Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does putting a Bloom filter in front of a HashMap (or disk lookup) pay off?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When most queries are for <strong>absent</strong> keys and the authoritative lookup is expensive (disk read, network call, or a map too big for RAM). The filter answers "definitely not" from a few bits per element, skipping the expensive path; only "maybe" (true hits plus the ~1% false positives) pays full price. If most queries hit, the filter is pure overhead — every hit still does the real lookup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash-based Structures.md" class="internal-link">Hash-based Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What single failure mode degrades every hash-based structure at once?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Skewed hash distribution. A weak or non-uniform <code>GetHashCode</code> degrades all three: the map and set walk O(n) bucket chains, and the Bloom filter's false-positive rate inflates as correlated hashes concentrate bits. The hash-flooding <em>attack</em> specifically targets the bucketed structures — an attacker packs one chain with chosen keys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash-based Structures.md" class="internal-link">Hash-based Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What assumptions make hash-map operations <code>O(1)</code> on average?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A hash function that distributes keys close to uniformly, so buckets stay short, and a load factor bounded by resizing, so buckets do not fill up. Both must hold. Without them, keys concentrate in a few buckets and each operation walks a long chain toward <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is insert amortized <code>O(1)</code> rather than strictly <code>O(1)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single insert can push the load factor past its threshold and rehash every existing entry into a larger array, an <code>O(n)</code> step. Averaged over the inserts that grew the map to that size, the rehash cost is <code>O(1)</code> per insert. The guarantee is over a sequence; any individual insert can still cost <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens to an entry whose key is mutated after insertion?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The entry stays in the bucket the key hashed to at insertion time, but a lookup recomputes the bucket from the key's new hash and searches a different one. The entry is present in memory yet unreachable — orphaned. Keys must be immutable, or at least never change a hash-participating field after insertion.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a balanced tree preferable to a hash map?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the workload needs ordered iteration, range queries, or nearest-key lookups. A hash map scatters keys across buckets and cannot answer those without a full scan and sort; a balanced tree keeps keys sorted at <code>O(log n)</code> per operation, which is the price for that ordering.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Linear Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is array indexing <code>O(1)</code> and independent of the index?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The element address is computed directly as <code>base + i * elementSize</code> — a multiply and an add — so <code>a[i]</code> costs the same for any <code>i</code>. This works only because every element is the same width and the block is contiguous, which lets the offset be pure arithmetic instead of a walk.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a middle insert cost <code>O(n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An array keeps its slots packed with no gaps. Inserting at index <code>k</code> in an <code>n</code>-element array shifts the <code>n − k</code> following elements up by one slot to open the position, so the work is proportional to the tail length. The contiguity that makes indexing cheap is the same property that forces the shift.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an array not append, and what changes with a dynamic array?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Capacity is fixed at allocation, so there is no free slot past the last element; adding one requires a new, larger block and a full copy. A dynamic array owns that resize policy — typically doubling — which spreads the copy cost so that append is amortized <code>O(1)</code> while keeping the same contiguous layout and <code>O(1)</code> index.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an array scan beat an asymptotically better structure at small <code>n</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Contiguity keeps neighbors in the same cache lines, and the prefetcher streams the next lines ahead of a sequential scan, so most accesses hit L1 (~1 ns) rather than main memory (~100 ns). A scattered structure pays a near-full miss per node. Big-O counts operations; the array's constant factor is far smaller, so the crossover where a better bound wins can sit past the sizes a workload reaches.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do a full ring and an empty ring both satisfy <code>head == tail</code>, and how is the collision resolved?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Empty rings put the read and write cursors on the same slot with nothing between them; a full ring wraps <code>tail</code> all the way around until it lands back on <code>head</code>. The index pair is identical in both states. Resolutions: store an explicit <code>count</code> (empty is <code>0</code>, full is <code>capacity</code>), or leave one slot unused so full becomes <code>(tail + 1) % capacity == head</code> while empty stays <code>head == tail</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are the O(1) operation bounds worst-case rather than amortized, unlike a growable queue?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The backing array is allocated once and never resized, so no operation can ever trigger a copy of existing elements. Each enqueue or dequeue is a single slot access plus a modulo increment, with a constant cost every time. A growable queue's O(1) is amortized precisely because occasional writes pay for a resize.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">On reaching capacity, what distinguishes an overwrite ring from a reject ring, and when does each fit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Overwrite advances <code>head</code> over the write, dropping the oldest element so the newest N always remain — right for logs, telemetry, and frame buffers where old data is disposable. Reject leaves the buffer unchanged and signals the producer to back off — right for a work queue where every item must be processed. The structure is identical; only the full-buffer branch of enqueue differs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must a reference-type ring null out dequeued slots?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The backing array holds references for every physical slot, including ones whose logical element was already dequeued. Until a slot is overwritten by a later enqueue, its stale reference keeps the object alive, so a long-lived ring can pin objects long after they left the queue. Assigning <code>default</code> on dequeue releases the reference for collection.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do a <code>head</code> index and a <code>count</code> let a ring-buffer deque touch both ends in <code>O(1)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The occupied slots are <code>head</code> through <code>(head + count - 1) % cap</code>. <code>PushBack</code> writes at <code>(head + count) % cap</code> and increments <code>count</code>; <code>PushFront</code> decrements <code>head</code> (mod capacity), writes there, and increments <code>count</code>; each pop reads an end slot and adjusts <code>head</code> or <code>count</code>. Because every access wraps modulo capacity and only <code>head</code> and <code>count</code> change, no element is ever shifted.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the ring-buffer push <code>O(1)</code> amortized but not <code>O(1)</code> worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
