<p dir="auto">A push onto a full buffer must copy all <code>n</code> elements into a larger array — an <code>O(n)</code> single operation. Geometric doubling makes that copy happen rarely enough that a run of <code>m</code> pushes costs <code>O(m)</code> total, so the amortized cost is <code>O(1)</code>; but any individual overflowing push is still <code>O(n)</code>, which shows up as a latency spike.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Ring buffer versus doubly-linked list as the deque backing — how do they differ?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The ring buffer stores elements contiguously: <code>O(1)</code> index, good locality, no per-element allocation, but an occasional <code>O(n)</code> resize. The linked list gives unconditional <code>O(1)</code> ends with no resize spike, at the cost of a node allocation (~40 bytes overhead on x64) per element, no <code>O(1)</code> index, and pointer-chasing traversal. The ring buffer is the default; the linked list fits when worst-case per-op latency or <code>O(1)</code> removal of held interior nodes matters more than locality.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is append amortized <code>O(1)</code> even though a resize copies every element?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Doubling the buffer makes resizes exponentially rarer as the array grows. Over <code>n</code> appends the resizes copy <code>1 + 2 + 4 + … + n &lt; 2n</code> elements total — fewer than two copies per element — so the average cost per append is constant. The individual append that triggers a resize is still <code>O(n)</code>; the constant bound is a property of the whole sequence, not any single call.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What breaks if the buffer grows by a fixed amount instead of a constant factor?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Fixed-increment growth resizes every constant number of appends, so the copies sum to <code>O(n²)</code> across <code>n</code> appends and append degrades to <code>O(n)</code> amortized. Geometric growth (e.g. <code>2×</code>) is what spaces resizes far enough apart to keep the amortized cost constant.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a resize invalidate held references and iterators?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Growth allocates a new backing array and copies elements into it, then drops the old buffer. Any pointer, cached index target, or iterator bound to the old array now refers to a stale allocation, which is why appending during iteration is unsound.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the <code>Prev</code> ↔ <code>Next</code> invariant matter during a removal?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Adjacency is stored twice: <code>a.Next == b</code> must agree with <code>b.Prev == a</code>. A removal must re-point both directions across the gap. Updating only one leaves a half-linked chain where forward and backward traversal disagree about membership, corrupting the list.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/LinkedList.md" class="internal-link">LinkedList</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a queue use a circular buffer or linked list instead of a plain array?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A plain array that dequeues from index <code>0</code> must shift every remaining element down one slot, making each dequeue <code>O(n)</code> and a full drain <code>Θ(n²)</code>. A circular buffer moves a <code>head</code> index modulo capacity instead of moving data, and a linked list unlinks a node — both keep dequeue <code>O(1)</code> while preserving arrival order.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does "amortized <code>O(1)</code> enqueue" mean for a growable queue?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single enqueue is <code>O(1)</code> until the backing array is full; that enqueue triggers an <code>O(n)</code> copy into a larger array. Because the array doubles, the copy happens once per <code>n</code> insertions, so the total cost of <code>n</code> enqueues is <code>O(n)</code> and the per-operation average stays <code>O(1)</code>, even though one operation spikes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a queue the wrong structure, and what replaces it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the next item must be chosen by priority rather than by arrival time, a FIFO queue would serve an older low-priority item ahead of an urgent newer one. A priority queue backed by a <a class="internal-link" href="Heap">Heap</a> restores correct order at <code>O(log n)</code> per operation. When both ends must be read and written, a <a class="internal-link" href="Deque">Deque</a> fits instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> represented, and why is its size independent of the window length?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It is a value type holding two fields — a managed reference to the first element in view and an integer length. The elements stay in the memory it points at, so the span itself is two machine words whether it covers 2 elements or 2 million.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes <code>Slice</code> zero-copy, and what is the observable consequence?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Slice</code> returns a new span with the reference advanced to <code>start</code> and a new length; no memory is allocated and no element is copied. Because the result aliases the same backing store, a write through the slice changes the element seen through the original span.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> not cross an <code>await</code> boundary, and what replaces it there?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">As a <code>ref struct</code> it is confined to the stack; crossing <code>await</code> (or <code>yield</code>) would move it to the heap-allocated state machine, where the buffer's lifetime no longer guards it, so the compiler rejects it. <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> is the heap-storable handle for those cases, yielding a <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> through <code>.Span</code> at the synchronous point of use.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a copy into a fresh array required instead of a span or <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the data must outlive the buffer it came from. A span or <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> only references existing memory; once that backing store is freed or reused, the view is invalid, so surviving data has to be copied into an owned array.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is only the top of a stack reachable, and what does that buy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Every operation is fixed to a single end, so there is no addressing scheme for interior or bottom elements. That restriction is what keeps push, pop, and peek at <code>O(1)</code> — no shifting, no search, no index arithmetic. Reaching a buried element requires popping everything above it, which is the information the stack trades away.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is array-backed <code>Push</code> amortized <code>O(1)</code> rather than worst-case constant?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most pushes write one slot and bump a counter. When the backing array is full, that push allocates a doubled array and copies all <code>n</code> elements — an <code>O(n)</code> single call. Doubling makes any run of <code>n</code> pushes cost <code>O(n)</code> in total, so the per-push average stays constant, but a specific push can spike.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why convert deep recursion into an explicit stack?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The call stack is itself a LIFO stack on a fixed-size memory region; deep enough recursion overflows it with an uncatchable <code>StackOverflowException</code>. An explicit <code>Stack&lt;T&gt;</code> holds the same pending frames on the heap, where depth is bounded by available memory instead. The logic maps directly: push where the recursion would call, pop where it would return.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Trees</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the AVL invariant and when is it checked?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For every node, <code>height(left) − height(right)</code> must stay in <code>{−1, 0, +1}</code>. It is checked on the way back up after an insert or delete: each node's stored height is recomputed along the touched path, and the first node whose balance factor reaches ±2 is rotated.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an insert need at most one rebalance but a delete need <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An insert's single rebalance (one single or one double rotation) restores the rebalanced subtree to its pre-insert height, so nothing above it changed and the fix stops. A delete can leave the rotated subtree one level shorter, which can unbalance an ancestor, so rebalancing may cascade all the way to the root.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do the Left-Right and Right-Left cases require a double rotation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single rotation on a zig-zag shape only mirrors the imbalance to the other side. The inner (median) node has to be rotated outward into a straight chain first, after which a single rotation lifts it to the top.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What two structural changes turn a B-tree into a B+ tree, and which query do they serve?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All <code>(key, value)</code> pairs move to the leaves, leaving internal nodes as a pure routing key index, and the leaves are chained into a sorted linked list. Both changes serve the range scan: after one descent, matching keys are read by walking the leaf chain in order instead of re-ascending into internal nodes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a B+ range scan <code>O(log_m n + k)</code> rather than <code>O(k log_m n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The <code>log_m n</code> descent locates the first matching leaf once. From there the leaf <code>next</code> links yield the remaining <code>k</code> entries in order as a sequential walk, adding one term per result. Without leaf links, each successor would require its own descent, multiplying the height into the cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does removing internal values raise fan-out, and why does that help on disk?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A separator is just a key and a child pointer, far smaller than a full record, so a routing page packs many more entries. Higher fan-out means fewer levels, and the small routing levels tend to stay in the buffer pool, so a lookup often costs a single physical read of the leaf.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a B-tree stay balanced with all leaves at one depth, and without rotations?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Height changes only at the root. An overflowing node splits and pushes its median key into the parent; if the split cascades to the root, a new root adds a single level. Because growth happens only at the top and every split keeps both halves at least half full, all leaves remain at equal depth by construction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What fixes a node that drops below its minimum fill on delete?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">If an adjacent sibling has a spare key, the node borrows: the parent's separator rotates down and the sibling's key rotates up. If both siblings are minimal, the node merges with a sibling and the separating parent key into one node, which can cascade upward and shrink the tree by a level.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does an in-order traversal of a BST produce sorted keys?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The ordering invariant places every smaller key in the left subtree and every larger key in the right. Visiting left, then the node, then right therefore reaches the smallest key first and the largest last, in strictly increasing order — the invariant made observable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens when a plain BST receives keys in sorted order, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each new key is larger than everything present, so every insert descends right. The tree becomes a right-leaning chain of height <code>n</code> — functionally a linked list — and search, insert, and delete all degrade to <code>O(n)</code>. The invariant is preserved; only the height blows up, which is why balanced variants add rotations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a node with two children deleted?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Its key is replaced by the in-order successor — the minimum of the right subtree, found by stepping right once then left to the end. That successor has no left child, so removing it reduces to the leaf or one-child case. This keeps the ordering invariant intact without orphaning either subtree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a hash map beat a BST despite the BST's ordered access?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the workload is purely exact-match lookup and insertion with no need for range queries, successors, min/max, or sorted iteration. The hash map delivers <code>O(1)</code> average operations; the ordering a BST maintains is pure overhead if nothing queries it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a red-black tree's height at most 2·log₂(n+1)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Equal black counts on every root-to-leaf path (invariant 4) make the all-black skeleton balanced, and "no red parent with a red child" (invariant 3) means reds can at most double a path by interleaving between blacks. The longest path is therefore at most twice the shortest, so height stays within 2·log₂(n+1).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are new nodes inserted red rather than black?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A red node adds no black to any path, so it can only break the "no two reds" invariant, which is a local violation fixable near the insertion point. A black insert would add a black to one path only, breaking the equal-black-height invariant along an entire root-to-leaf path — a global violation that is far more expensive to repair.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can the rotation count per insert be bounded when the recoloring is not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Recoloring only rewrites color bits and can propagate up to the root, but it never reshapes the tree. Once the fixup reaches a black uncle, one or two rotations resolve the violation and the loop terminates. So the structural work (rotations) is capped at two per insert while the unbounded work (recoloring) stays cheap.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes red-black delete more error-prone than insert?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert only ever repairs a local red-red violation. Delete can remove a black node and break the black-height invariant along a whole path, producing "double-black" cases that branch on the sibling's color and its children's colors. A subtle mistake leaves BST order intact — so lookups still return correct results — while silently losing the height guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why isn't a quadtree an <code>O(log n)</code> balanced search tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It has no balancing invariant — nothing forces the four subtrees to hold comparable amounts of data, and there are no rotations or fill rules. A point quadtree can become a chain through insertion order. A PR quadtree repeatedly subdivides the occupied region and needs an explicit finite-resolution or overflow rule; a region quadtree is bounded by raster resolution. The payoff is spatial pruning of regions during range and nearest-neighbor queries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Quadtree.md" class="internal-link">Quadtree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you reach for a geohash instead of a quadtree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <a class="internal-link" href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Geohash" aria-label="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Geohash" data-tooltip-position="top">Geohash</a> when spatial candidates must ride ordinary sorted indexes, cache keys, or shard prefixes and exact filtering can repair cell-boundary error. Use a quadtree when adaptive subdivision and tree traversal are the mechanism: collision broad-phase, nearest-neighbor pruning, or image-region compression. Geohash pays with neighboring-cell expansion; quadtree pays with pointer structure and distribution-dependent depth.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Quadtree.md" class="internal-link">Quadtree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a point quadtree differ from a PR quadtree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A point quadtree splits the plane at the coordinates of each inserted point (like a 2D BST), so its shape depends on insertion order and a bad order degrades it. A PR (point-region) quadtree always splits space into four <em>equal</em> quadrants independent of point coordinates, subdividing a bucket only when it overflows — so its shape depends solely on where the points are, not the order they arrived.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Quadtree.md" class="internal-link">Quadtree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a segment tree laid out in memory, and why <code>4n</code> slots?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A flat array indexed heap-style: the root at <code>1</code>, node <code>i</code>'s children at <code>2i</code> and <code>2i + 1</code>. A recursive build over a non-power-of-two <code>n</code> can reach index <code>4n − 1</code> on an unbalanced spine, so <code>4 * n</code> is the safe allocation; a power-of-two <code>n</code> needs only <code>2n</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a range query cost <code>O(log n)</code> instead of touching every element in the range?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Any <code>[l, r]</code> decomposes into at most <code>O(log n)</code> canonical nodes whose ranges lie fully inside it. Each such node's aggregate is already computed, so the query reads it and stops descending, never reaching the leaves beneath.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does lazy propagation defer, and how does that failure show up?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A range update stores a pending tag on each covering node instead of rewriting its whole subtree; the tag is pushed to children only when a later operation descends past that node. Forgetting a push-down returns a stale aggregate — a plausibly-wrong number, not a crash — which makes it the bug-prone part of the structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does only the <code>eq</code> link advance to the next character?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Lo</code> and <code>Hi</code> answer "is the current character smaller or larger than this node's split?" — they move sideways within the BST of alternatives <em>at the same string position</em>. <code>Eq</code> fires only when the character matches the split, meaning that position is resolved, so it is the one link that steps forward to the next character. Counting <code>eq</code> links from the root gives a node's character position exactly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Ternary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a TST lookup <code>O(L + log n)</code> rather than the trie's <code>O(L)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A plain trie resolves "which child" with a single array index or hash, <code>O(1)</code> per character, giving <code>O(L)</code>. A TST resolves it by descending a BST of the alternatives at that position, which costs <code>O(log n)</code> when balanced. Summed over the <code>L</code> matched positions the descents amortise, and the standard result is roughly <code>L + log n</code> character comparisons for a hit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Ternary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does inserting keys in sorted order hurt a TST?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each string position is a BST built by insertion. Feeding characters in ascending order at some position makes that BST a right-leaning chain, so the <code>O(log n)</code> descent degrades to <code>O(n)</code> and lookups approach <code>O(L · n)</code>. Randomising the insertion order, or balancing the per-position BSTs, keeps the logarithmic factor — the same fix a degenerate <a class="internal-link" href="Binary Search Tree">Binary Search Tree</a> needs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Ternary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What can a TST do that a <code>Dictionary</code>-backed trie cannot do cheaply?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Emit keys in sorted order without a separate sort (in-order <code>lo</code>/<code>eq</code>/<code>hi</code> traversal), and run near-neighbour queries — partial-match wildcards and edit-distance-one spell-check — by descending <code>lo</code>, <code>eq</code>, and <code>hi</code> together at a mismatch position. A hash-map trie has no ordering among children, so both require extra work it doesn't natively support.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Ternary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a trie lookup <code>O(L)</code> and not affected by the number of stored keys?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The walk follows one labelled edge per character of the query, so the work equals the key length <code>L</code>. The path a query traces is fixed by the query string; adding more keys creates other branches but never lengthens that path. A comparison tree, by contrast, reads the key <code>O(log n)</code> times as <code>n</code> grows.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the same walk serve both exact search and a prefix query?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both follow the query's characters edge by edge from the root. Exact search additionally requires the terminal node's end-of-word flag, proving the path is a complete stored key. A prefix query stops at "did the path exist", since reaching the node already certifies that at least one stored key starts with the fragment.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can deleting one key not simply remove the nodes along its path?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Nodes are shared. <code>car</code> and <code>card</code> share the <code>c → a → r</code> path, so deleting <code>car</code> must clear the <code>r</code> node's end-of-word flag but keep the node, because <code>d</code> still descends from it. Only nodes that become both unflagged and childless may be pruned, walking up until that condition stops holding.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a radix (PATRICIA) trie change about the representation, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It collapses each chain of single-child nodes into one edge labelled with the whole substring. The plain trie reserves a <code>σ</code>-wide child slot at every node, so long sparse keys waste memory on near-empty arrays; compressing the chains cuts node count while keeping the <code>O(L)</code> walk and the same prefix and ordering queries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which built-in .NET collection is closest to a self-balancing tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code> (and <code>SortedDictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code> for key-value scenarios).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trees.md" class="internal-link">Trees</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you avoid recursive tree traversal?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">On unknown/deep depth, where iterative traversal with an explicit stack is safer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trees.md" class="internal-link">Trees</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What determines how many slots a Fenwick tree operation touches?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The set bits of the index. <code>Prefix(i)</code> reads one slot per set bit of <code>i</code> (<code>popcount(i)</code>), clearing the lowest set bit each step; <code>Update(i, …)</code> visits one slot per higher bit while adding the lowest set bit. Both are bounded by <code>⌊log₂ n⌋ + 1</code>, and the bound is deterministic rather than amortized.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a Fenwick tree answer range sums but not range minimums?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A range is reconstructed only as <code>Prefix(r) − Prefix(l − 1)</code>, which needs the aggregate to have an inverse. Sum has subtraction; minimum does not — <code>min(1..r)</code> and <code>min(1..l−1)</code> reveal nothing about <code>min(l..r)</code>. Non-invertible aggregates therefore need a segment tree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the array 1-indexed, and what breaks at index 0?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>i &amp; -i</code> isolates the lowest set bit, but <code>0</code> has none, so <code>0 &amp; -0 == 0</code>. The update loop advances by <code>i += i &amp; -i</code>, which never moves off <code>0</code>, and the prefix loop uses <code>i &gt; 0</code> as its terminator. A 0-based layout stalls the walk immediately.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a Fenwick tree handle a range update with a point query?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Not with the plain layout. It is built over a difference array: <code>Update(l, +delta)</code> and <code>Update(r + 1, -delta)</code> record the range increment, and a point query at <code>i</code> becomes <code>Prefix(i)</code>. Range update with range query extends this to two BITs run in parallel.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Heap-like</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does extract-min stay <code>O(log n)</code> when the heap is a forest rather than one tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The minimum is a root (heap order holds within each tree), so it is found by scanning the <code>≤ log n</code> roots. Removing a root of order <code>k</code> exposes its <code>k</code> children, which already have orders <code>k−1 … 0</code> — a valid binomial forest. Reversing them into a root list and melding that back costs another <code>O(log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Binomial Queues.md" class="internal-link">Binomial Queues</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is insert amortized <code>O(1)</code> when its worst case is <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert melds in a single <code>B₀</code>, which is a binary increment. A long carry chain that links at every order is the <code>O(log n)</code> worst case, but it can only happen because earlier cheap inserts left those orders filled. The potential argument that bounds a binary counter's increment at amortized <code>O(1)</code> per operation applies directly, so <code>m</code> inserts cost <code>O(m)</code> total.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Binomial Queues.md" class="internal-link">Binomial Queues</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the laziness actually defer, and to where?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert, merge, and decrease-key avoid any tree reorganization: insert and merge only splice into the root list, and decrease-key cuts the node loose instead of sifting it. The deferred work — linking trees so degrees become distinct — is done once by the next extract-min during consolidation, so many cheap operations prepay one expensive cleanup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are the mark bit and cascading cut necessary for the O(1) decrease-key bound?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Cutting nodes out on decrease-key can thin a high-degree node until its subtree is almost empty, which would let consolidation link wide, shallow trees and blow past <code>O(log n)</code>. Allowing each node to lose only one child before it too is cut guarantees a degree-<code>k</code> node keeps at least <code>F(k + 2)</code> descendants, capping the maximum degree at <code>O(log n)</code>. The marks are the stored potential that funds the cascade, keeping decrease-key <code>O(1)</code> amortized.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is find-min O(1) but extract-min O(log n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Heap order forces the global minimum to be a root, and the heap keeps a direct pointer to it, so find-min is a single read. Extract-min must remove that root, promote its children, and then consolidate the whole root list — linking equal-degree roots until degrees are distinct — which costs <code>O(log n)</code> amortized because the maximum degree is <code>O(log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a complete binary tree be stored without any pointers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Completeness means levels fill left to right with no gaps, so node positions are contiguous. A node at index <code>i</code> therefore has children at <code>2i+1</code> and <code>2i+2</code> and a parent at <code>(i-1)/2</code>, computed arithmetically. A tree with holes would break that indexing and force explicit links.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>build-heap</code> <code>O(n)</code> while inserting <code>n</code> elements one by one is <code>O(n log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bottom-up sift-down does at most <code>h</code> work for a node at height <code>h</code>, and there are only about <code>n / 2^{h+1}</code> nodes at that height. Summing <code>h · n / 2^{h+1}</code> over all heights converges to <code>O(n)</code>; most nodes are leaves that move zero or one level. Repeated insertion instead sifts each new element up toward the root, costing <code>O(log n)</code> apiece.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a plain binary heap need an external map to support decrease-key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The heap exposes elements only by heap-order position, not by identity, and offers no way to locate an arbitrary value without an <code>O(n)</code> scan. A side table mapping each element to its current array index — updated on every swap — is required to point at the node before re-keying and sifting it in <code>O(log n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the leftist invariant bound, and how does that make merge logarithmic?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>npl(left) ≥ npl(right)</code> at every node forces the right spine to length <code>≤ log(n + 1)</code>, since a right spine of length <code>r</code> requires at least <code>2^r − 1</code> nodes. Merge recurses only down the two right spines, so it does <code>O(log n)</code> work in the worst case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why swap children after each recursive merge step?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The recursive merge attaches the result as the right child, which may make the right subtree deeper than the left and violate the invariant. Swapping pushes the heavier subtree to the left — where no operation walks it — keeping the right spine short. Omitting the swap lets the right spine grow to <code>O(n)</code> and degrades every operation to linear.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do insert and extract-min reduce to merge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert merges the heap with a single-node heap. Extract-min removes the root and merges its left and right subtrees. One correct merge implements the whole API and every operation inherits its <code>O(log n)</code> worst-case bound.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can <code>O(log n)</code> amortized hold when one merge can be <code>O(n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A potential function counts heavy nodes — those whose right subtree outweighs their left. An expensive merge traverses many heavy nodes, but each unconditional swap makes a heavy node light. The costly traversal discharges potential accumulated by earlier cheap operations and leaves the heap cheap to merge again, so the per-operation cost averages to <code>O(log n)</code> even though a single call is not bounded by it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Skew Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Skew Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="data-persistence">Data Persistence</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you reduce cache stampede?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Add jitter to expirations so hot keys do not all expire simultaneously. Use request coalescing (singleflight pattern) so only one caller recomputes while others await the result. Consider stale-while-revalidate or background refresh to avoid blocking on recomputation entirely. <code>HybridCache</code> in .NET 9+ handles coalescing automatically.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a bigger connection pool often worse, not better?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each connection costs a server-side backend/worker plus memory, and once the database's CPU and disk are saturated, additional concurrent queries just contend and add latency — throughput can actually <em>drop</em>. The right size is a small multiple of the database's core count, sized against <code>max_connections</code> across the <em>whole fleet</em>, not maximized per app.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes connection-pool exhaustion and how do you fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All connections are checked out and new requests block until timing out. Usual causes: a pool too small for real concurrency, connections <strong>leaked</strong> by missing <code>Dispose()</code>/<code>using</code>, or connections held across slow I/O or long transactions. Fixes: scope every connection with <code>using</code>, keep the checkout window to just the query, right-size the pool, and add a server-side pooler if the fleet is large.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is connection pooling hard in serverless environments?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Serverless platforms run many short-lived, isolated function instances that can't share an in-process pool, and each may open its own connections — so a spike spawns thousands of connections and exhausts the database. The standard remedy is an external pooler (RDS Proxy, PgBouncer) that all instances share, multiplexing them onto a small set of real connections.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should you choose between SQL and NoSQL for a new service?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Default to a relational database: mature tooling, ACID transactions, flexible ad-hoc queries, and joins cover the majority of workloads and let the schema enforce invariants</li>
<li dir="auto">Reach for NoSQL when a specific access pattern dominates and relational cost is real: key-value or document stores for simple high-throughput lookups, wide-column for massive write volume, graph stores for relationship-heavy traversal</li>
<li dir="auto">The honest driver is usually the data model and query pattern, not scale — most services never outgrow a well-indexed relational database, and "web-scale" is rarely the actual constraint</li>
<li dir="auto">Combining them is common and often better than committing everything to one model: a relational system of record with a document or cache layer for a hot read path<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Data Persistence.md" class="internal-link">Data Persistence</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What isolation level should you use for a read-modify-write transaction, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Match the protection to the invariant. Repeatable Read stabilizes repeated reads and may protect a same-row update under the engine's semantics, but it does not generically prevent write skew. For a financial or inventory decision spanning rows or a predicate, use Serializable or explicitly lock the full invariant set. Optimistic row-version checks are a lower-contention option only when the transaction validates every record whose version informed the decision.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ACID.md" class="internal-link">ACID</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does write-ahead logging (WAL) implement durability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Before a changed data page may reach durable storage, the database must flush the WAL records needed to reconstruct it. Under a strict commit setting, the commit record is also flushed before success is acknowledged. Recovery replays WAL so committed changes missing from data pages are restored; engine-specific transaction metadata keeps uncommitted changes from becoming visible. Sequential log writes and group commit let data-page writes happen later without placing every commit behind a random page write.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ACID.md" class="internal-link">ACID</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-nosql">NoSQL</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a successful Elasticsearch write be invisible to search briefly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The primary has accepted the operation and recorded it in its indexing/translog path, but search runs against opened Lucene segments. A refresh publishes recent segments to search. Durability acknowledgement and search visibility are separate boundaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/Elasticsearch.md" class="internal-link">Elasticsearch</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why use both <code>text</code> and <code>keyword</code> for one field?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>text</code> is analyzed into terms for relevance-ranked full-text matching. <code>keyword</code> keeps the exact value for equality filters, sorting, and aggregations. One representation cannot efficiently provide both semantics.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/Elasticsearch.md" class="internal-link">Elasticsearch</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are SSTables immutable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Immutability lets the engine create SSTables and compaction outputs with sequential file writes, then install the completed files without overwriting data that concurrent readers are using. Recovery still has work to do: replay the WAL into a memtable, read the manifest or version set to identify the live SSTables, and discard incomplete output files that were never installed. Dense, static files also compress well. The price is that updates and deletes write new versions and tombstones, so background <strong>compaction</strong> must reconcile versions and reclaim space.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/LSM-Tree.md" class="internal-link">LSM-Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the read-vs-write amplification tradeoff, and how does compaction strategy control it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Write amplification is bytes written to disk per byte the app wrote; read amplification is disk reads per logical read; space amplification is on-disk bytes per byte of live data. Compaction cannot minimize all three at once. <strong>Size-tiered</strong> rewrites data rarely (low write amp) but leaves a key's live version spread across tiers (high read and space amp). <strong>Leveled</strong> keeps non-overlapping ranges per level so a read hits at most one file per level (low read and space amp) but rewrites each key as it descends levels (high write amp). You choose which amplification the workload can afford.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/LSM-Tree.md" class="internal-link">LSM-Tree</a></em></p>
