</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a B-tree beat an LSM-Tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When reads dominate and latency must be predictable. A B-tree point lookup follows one root-to-leaf path; a range lookup follows that path to the first matching leaf, then traverses the required leaf pages. An LSM range scan instead performs a k-way merge across the memtable and every overlapping SSTable run, while point reads may probe several Bloom-filter candidates. That difference favors B-trees for read-heavy OLTP and range scans; LSM-Trees favor write-heavy ingest but pay multi-run read and background compaction costs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/LSM-Tree.md" class="internal-link">LSM-Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between the four NoSQL families?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">By access pattern. <strong>Key-value</strong> when you only ever fetch by a single key (cache, session). <strong>Document</strong> when data forms self-contained aggregates with flexible schema (profiles, catalogs). <strong>Wide-column</strong> when you need extreme write throughput along a known partition key (time-series, logs). <strong>Graph</strong> when queries traverse relationships many hops deep (social, recommendations, fraud). The query shape, not the data size, drives the choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is NoSQL data modeling "query-first" instead of normalized?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">There are no joins, so you can't assemble data from many tables at read time cheaply. Instead you store data pre-shaped for each read — often duplicating it across multiple "tables"/documents (one per query). You trade storage and write-time duplication for fast, single-lookup reads. This is the opposite of relational <a class="internal-link" href="Normalization Denormalization" aria-label="Normalization Denormalization" data-tooltip-position="top">normalization</a>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does "polyglot persistence" mean and why is it common?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Using different databases for different jobs within one system — e.g. PostgreSQL as the system of record, Redis for caching/sessions, Elasticsearch for search, Neo4j for a recommendation graph. No single store is best at everything, so mature systems combine them, accepting the operational cost of running several.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which NoSQL family fits a user-profile API with very frequent reads by user id?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Key-value or document store, because the access pattern is dominated by point reads on a single id.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use key-value if it is almost entirely get/put by id with no rich querying.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use document if you read/update an aggregate (profile + preferences) and occasionally query a few indexed fields.</li>
<li dir="auto">A key-value API keeps point lookup semantics narrow; a document engine commonly adds secondary-query options at some indexing and storage cost. Exact latency and query support depend on the product.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is NoSQL a bad idea?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the core use case needs relational constraints and multi-entity ACID transactions, or queries are fundamentally join-heavy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Forcing those onto NoSQL pushes join logic and consistency into application code, which is error-prone.</li>
<li dir="auto">Often the better move is to keep SQL and add caching, read replicas, or a denormalized read model.</li>
<li dir="auto">If the specialized store cannot enforce the required joins, constraints, or transaction boundary, its access-pattern advantage does not pay for moving those guarantees into application code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does NoSQL push you toward denormalization and data duplication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the chosen store cannot execute an efficient join across the required data, the cheapest read is often one that fetches a whole aggregate in a single hit.</li>
<li dir="auto">You then model that read shape explicitly, sometimes duplicating fields across documents or rows instead of normalizing them once.</li>
<li dir="auto">That makes reads fast and partition-friendly but means a single logical change may touch many copies.</li>
<li dir="auto">You accept write-side duplication and a synchronization policy in exchange for cheaper reads; whether copies may be temporarily inconsistent is a separate consistency decision.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Redis safe as a system of record?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Only when the deployment's eviction, persistence acknowledgement, replication/failover loss window, backup, restore, and capacity contracts meet the data's requirements. Enabling AOF is not enough: asynchronous failover can still lose acknowledged writes, disk and rewrite failures still need handling, and <code>maxmemory</code> must not evict authoritative keys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/Redis.md" class="internal-link">Redis</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a big key hurt Redis even with <code>appendfsync everysec</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The main thread still receives, parses, mutates, and copies the large command into AOF and replication buffers. Background <code>fsync</code> removes one disk wait from the request path; it does not remove network, allocator, serialization, copy-on-write, rewrite, or deletion work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/Redis.md" class="internal-link">Redis</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-orms">ORMs</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does EF Core's change tracker work, and when should you disable it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">EF Core takes a snapshot of each loaded entity's property values. On <code>SaveChangesAsync()</code>, it compares current values to the snapshot and generates SQL for changed properties.</li>
<li dir="auto">Overhead: change tracking adds memory (snapshot storage) and CPU (comparison on save) per tracked entity.</li>
<li dir="auto">Disable with <code>.AsNoTracking()</code> for read-only queries (reports, API responses that don't modify data). This is the single most impactful EF Core performance optimization for read-heavy workloads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: <code>.AsNoTracking()</code> entities cannot be modified and saved — you must re-attach them or use <code>ExecuteUpdateAsync()</code> for bulk updates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ORMs/Entity Framework.md" class="internal-link">Entity Framework</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the N+1 query problem and how do you detect it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">N+1 occurs when loading N entities and then accessing a navigation property on each, triggering N additional queries.</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection: enable EF Core query logging (<code>LogTo(Console.WriteLine)</code>) or use MiniProfiler/Application Insights to see query counts per request.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: use <code>Include()</code> for eager loading, or split into two queries and join in memory for large result sets.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: <code>Include()</code> generates a JOIN, which can produce a large result set if the included collection is large. For collections with &gt;100 items per parent, consider <code>AsSplitQuery()</code> to use separate queries instead of a JOIN.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ORMs/Entity Framework.md" class="internal-link">Entity Framework</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-sql">SQL</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you use an Update (U) lock instead of a Shared (S) lock?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">During the <em>search phase</em> of a read-then-write operation such as an <code>UPDATE</code> or <code>DELETE</code> that must scan to find the row it will modify. If that scan took an S lock, two transactions could both hold S on the target row and then both try to upgrade S→X, each blocked by the other's S — an instant deadlock. A U lock prevents it: only one transaction may hold U on a resource at a time (so the upgrade race can't form), yet U is compatible with existing S locks (so it doesn't block concurrent readers during the scan). When the write finally happens, U converts to X.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Database Locks.md" class="internal-link">Database Locks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Locks vs MVCC — how does each enforce isolation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Locking blocks incompatible access, so a reader may wait for a writer under lock-based isolation. MVCC instead keeps older committed versions and can serve the reader from a snapshot, removing most read-write blocking. MVCC is not inherently optimistic: the same engine can use versioned reads, pessimistic write locks, and optimistic version checks. Locks add waiting and can deadlock; MVCC adds version bookkeeping (PostgreSQL's <code>VACUUM</code>, SQL Server's <code>tempdb</code> version store).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Database Locks.md" class="internal-link">Database Locks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do intent locks exist, and what does lock escalation trade away?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Intent locks (IS/IX/SIX) let a hierarchical lock manager detect conflicts <em>across granularities</em> cheaply: before locking a row, a transaction places an intent lock on the table above it, so a request for a table-level lock can check that single intent lock instead of scanning every row lock. Lock escalation goes the other way — it collapses thousands of fine row/page locks into one coarse table lock to reclaim lock-manager memory. The trade is concurrency: the escalated transaction now holds the whole table, so unrelated transactions that only wanted other rows suddenly block — the "concurrency cliff."<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Database Locks.md" class="internal-link">Database Locks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is “use a hash index because lookup is O(1)” incomplete database advice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The engine must support the hash index for the target table and operator, equality is the only useful ordering relation, and collisions, bucket growth, concurrency, durability, and cache behavior still affect cost. A B+ tree often wins for mixed equality and range work because one structure supports both.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Indexes.md" class="internal-link">Indexes</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a low-cardinality column still participate in a useful index?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A filtered index can store only the rare subset, a composite key can use the column after a useful leading prefix, and a covering index can avoid base-row reads. Measure the complete query plan; cardinality is evidence, not a standalone rule.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Indexes.md" class="internal-link">Indexes</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is normalization and why do most systems stop at 3NF/BCNF?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Normalization eliminates redundancy by decomposing tables so each fact is stored once, preventing update anomalies. Most production OLTP systems stop at 3NF or BCNF because higher forms (4NF, 5NF) address rare anomalies (multivalued and join dependencies) at the cost of more tables and more joins. The decomposition overhead rarely pays off outside temporal or analytical databases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you denormalize a table, and what risks does it introduce?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Denormalize when a read-heavy workload has expensive joins that indexes can't fix: common in reporting, analytics, or high-throughput APIs. Risks: update anomalies (copies get out of sync), data inconsistency if writes don't update all copies atomically, and increased write complexity. Always measure before denormalizing; premature denormalization adds maintenance cost for no proven benefit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between 2NF and 3NF, and how would you recognize a violation of each?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">2NF eliminates partial dependencies: every non-key attribute must depend on the entire composite primary key, not just part of it. A violation looks like a column that depends on only one column of a multi-column PK. 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies: non-key attributes must depend directly on the PK, not through another non-key attribute. A violation looks like column A depending on the PK, column B depending on A (not on the PK directly). Fixing both involves decomposing the offending columns into separate tables.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the three replication lag anomalies, and how do you mitigate each?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Read-your-writes</strong>: user writes then reads from a stale replica and sees their write missing. Fix: route post-write reads to the leader for a short window, or use LSN/timestamp tracking to wait for the replica to catch up.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Monotonic reads</strong>: user sees newer data, then an older replica. Carry the highest observed position or causal token and require the next source to have reached it; stickiness alone fails after replica failover.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Consistent prefix reads</strong>: a dependent write appears before its prerequisite. Preserve one ordered stream or causal dependency token and read from a position that includes the prerequisite; a shared partition key helps only when its ordering contract covers the failover path.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose synchronous vs asynchronous replication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Synchronous</strong>: when acknowledged commits must survive failover. The leader confirms only after the configured standby acknowledgement, and failover must select an eligible durable standby. This protects acknowledged durability; replica reads still need a replay-position check or primary routing to observe the commit.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Asynchronous</strong>: when avoiding the standby round trip justifies weaker failover durability. The leader does not wait for a standby, and replicas may be current or lagging. An acknowledged commit can be lost on failover only if no eligible surviving node received it.</li>
<li dir="auto">SQL Server Always On configures synchronous-commit or asynchronous-commit per availability replica. PostgreSQL uses <code>synchronous_standby_names</code> to select synchronous standbys and <code>synchronous_commit</code> to choose the acknowledgement boundary; standbys not selected for synchronous confirmation remain asynchronous.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does split-brain occur and how is it prevented?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A network partition isolates the primary from the rest of the cluster. A quorum of remaining nodes elects a new primary. When the partition heals, both nodes believe they are primary and have accepted divergent writes.</li>
<li dir="auto">A majority vote prevents two candidates in the same voting configuration from both being elected. Fencing separately terminates or revokes the old primary before the new one accepts writes.</li>
<li dir="auto">WSFC uses quorum witnesses; Patroni uses distributed coordination in systems such as etcd or Consul. Without endpoint revocation or fencing, election quorum alone is insufficient because a slow former leader can keep serving writes after losing authority.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>WHERE</code> filters rows before grouping and cannot use aggregate results. <code>HAVING</code> filters groups after <code>GROUP BY</code> and can use aggregates such as <code>COUNT(*)</code>. Put non-aggregate predicates in <code>WHERE</code> so fewer rows enter grouping.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a stored procedure and how is it different from a function?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A stored procedure can run multi-step data-changing logic and return result sets or output parameters. A function returns a scalar or table value for use in a query and is constrained by the engine's function rules. In SQL Server, eligible scalar UDFs can be inlined; older or ineligible scalar UDFs may execute row by row and inhibit parallel plans.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a Common Table Expression (CTE) and when should you use a temp table instead?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A CTE is a statement-scoped named query expression. Do not assume it is materialized or reused. Use a temp table when you need a stable intermediate result, indexes on that result, or guaranteed reuse across several operations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are SQL Server transaction isolation levels?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SQL Server provides <code>READ UNCOMMITTED</code>, <code>READ COMMITTED</code>, <code>REPEATABLE READ</code>, <code>SERIALIZABLE</code>, and <code>SNAPSHOT</code>. Read Committed Snapshot Isolation changes <code>READ COMMITTED</code> reads to statement-level row versions. <code>NOLOCK</code> is not a performance switch: it permits rolled-back, missing, and duplicate observations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you shard a database?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">After evidence shows that write throughput or storage exceeds one ownership domain and simpler measures cannot remove the ceiling. Read replicas help reads, caches remove repeated reads, and in-engine partitioning improves manageability without creating cross-database transactions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Sharding.md" class="internal-link">Sharding</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How much data should move when one equal node is added to a balanced consistent-hash cluster?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">With <code>N</code> balanced equal-capacity nodes before the addition, the new node's expected final share is about <code>1 / (N + 1)</code>, so that is the expected movement. Token imbalance changes the actual fraction; virtual nodes reduce variance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Sharding.md" class="internal-link">Sharding</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a useful shard key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It distributes bytes and request load, appears in the dominant operations, co-locates data that must transact together, and remains stable. High cardinality is useful only when the traffic and tenant-size distribution are also balanced.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Sharding.md" class="internal-link">Sharding</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="devops">DevOps</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a CI pipeline 'good' vs 'fast but unreliable'?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A good CI pipeline returns feedback before developers stop attending to it; under ten minutes is a useful target for many change-validation paths, not a universal threshold. It quarantines and repairs flaky tests, does not print sensitive data, promotes the same verified artifact through environments, and produces failures that identify the broken boundary. Track queue time and execution time separately: a fast job behind a long runner queue is still slow feedback.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/CI CD tools.md" class="internal-link">CI CD tools</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does DevOps actually optimize, beyond tooling?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The target is delivery throughput and instability. DORA's current five metrics are change lead time, deployment frequency, failed deployment recovery time, change fail rate, and deployment rework rate.</li>
<li dir="auto">Automation (CI/CD, infrastructure as code) exists to make releases boring and repeatable, removing the manual steps where humans introduce variance</li>
<li dir="auto">Observability (logs, metrics, traces) exists so failures are visible before users report them and diagnosable without a redeploy</li>
<li dir="auto">Ownership closes the loop: the team that ships a change runs it, so production feedback shapes the next change<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/DevOps.md" class="internal-link">DevOps</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is fast, safe rollback more valuable than trying to avoid failure?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">You cannot prevent all failures; you can make their blast radius and duration small — a one-click rollback turns an incident into a non-event</li>
<li dir="auto">Progressive delivery (canary, blue-green, feature flags) limits how many users a bad change reaches and lets you revert without a full redeploy</li>
<li dir="auto">Small, frequent deploys are easier to reason about and roll back than large batched ones — big-bang releases are where irreversible failures hide</li>
<li dir="auto">Design the pipeline so the <em>undo</em> path is as tested as the <em>deploy</em> path<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/DevOps.md" class="internal-link">DevOps</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why use multi-stage builds for .NET applications?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The .NET SDK stage includes compilers and build tools that the running application usually does not need.</li>
<li dir="auto">The runtime stage can start from the ASP.NET runtime image and copy only the published output.</li>
<li dir="auto">This separates build credentials and tooling from the production filesystem and usually reduces transfer and scan surface; measure the actual result for the chosen tags and architecture.</li>
<li dir="auto">Smaller images mean faster pulls, less attack surface, and lower registry storage costs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost: slightly more complex Dockerfile; worth it for any production workload.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Docker.md" class="internal-link">Docker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent secrets from leaking into Docker images?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Never use <code>ENV</code> or <code>ARG</code> for secrets in Dockerfiles — they are visible in <code>docker history</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep non-sensitive settings in environment variables; mount credentials at runtime from Docker secrets or a platform secret provider.</li>
<li dir="auto">For build-time secrets (e.g., NuGet feeds), use <code>RUN --mount=type=secret</code> (BuildKit).</li>
<li dir="auto">In Kubernetes, mount Secrets or external-secret-provider volumes as files and restrict access with RBAC.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: runtime injection adds operational complexity but is the only safe approach.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Docker.md" class="internal-link">Docker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between declarative and imperative IaC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Declarative</strong> IaC describes the desired end state and computes a diff. Reapplying should be a no-op only when inputs and observed external state are unchanged and the provider implements the operation safely. <strong>Imperative</strong> IaC specifies commands; it can still be idempotent when the program checks and converges state explicitly. The practical advantage of declarative tooling is an inspectable model and plan, not an unconditional safety guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the Terraform state file important, and how should it be managed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The state file maps Terraform resource addresses to remote objects and stored attributes. If it is lost or corrupted, planning and ownership become unsafe. Use a remote backend with access control, versioning, and supported locking. For the S3 backend, enable <code>use_lockfile</code>; DynamoDB locking is deprecated. Never commit state to Git because it can contain secrets and full resource details.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is configuration drift and how does IaC address it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Drift is when observed infrastructure differs from the declared definition. Refresh and plan can surface drift for attributes the provider reads and manages; apply can reconcile those fields. Ignored attributes, external systems, provider defaults, and emergency changes still require explicit handling, so IaC bounds drift rather than proving none exists.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between a Pod, Deployment, and Service?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Pod: the smallest unit; wraps one or more containers sharing a network namespace.</li>
<li dir="auto">Deployment: manages a set of identical pods; handles rolling updates and scaling.</li>
<li dir="auto">Service: stable network endpoint (DNS name + IP) that load-balances across pods.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pods are ephemeral (new IP on restart); Services provide stable addressing.</li>
<li dir="auto">A Deployment supports controlled rolling replacement, but does not guarantee zero downtime. Readiness, graceful shutdown, compatible versions, disruption budgets, and enough spare capacity must all hold.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do Kubernetes Secrets need additional protection beyond base64 encoding?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Base64 is encoding, not encryption — anyone with etcd access can decode secrets trivially.</li>
<li dir="auto">By default, etcd stores secrets in plaintext (base64 is just a transport format).</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigations: enable etcd encryption at rest, use Sealed Secrets (encrypted in git), or use Azure Key Vault with the Secrets Store CSI driver.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: external secret management adds operational complexity but is required for regulated workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes CrashLoopBackOff and how do you debug it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes keeps restarting it with exponential backoff.</li>
<li dir="auto">Common causes: app crashes on startup (missing config, DB connection failure), liveness probe failing before app is ready, OOMKilled (memory limit too low).</li>
<li dir="auto">Debug: <code>kubectl logs &lt;pod&gt; --previous</code> (logs from the crashed instance), <code>kubectl describe pod &lt;pod&gt;</code> (events and exit codes), <code>kubectl exec -it &lt;pod&gt; -- /bin/sh</code> (if the container starts briefly).</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: liveness probes are essential for self-healing but misconfigured probes cause the problem they are meant to solve.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you diagnose an intermittent p95/p99 latency bottleneck in a multi-service system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start from traces and filter to the slow requests — the p99, not the average — to see which span dominates. Correlate with per-service RED metrics to tell whether the latency is isolated or systemic, then walk the dependency spans (DB, cache, external API) to find where it originates. Pull structured logs by the same trace ID to check for edge-case payloads or retries on those requests. Traces locate the delay, metrics size its effect, and logs expose the state around it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use RED vs USE?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RED — Rate, Errors, Duration — is for customer-facing endpoints and request pipelines: it tells you what users are experiencing. USE — Utilization, Saturation, Errors — is for the resources underneath: CPU, thread pools, queue depth, DB connection pools. You want both, because they pair causally: RED is the symptom at the service boundary, USE is the pressure source beneath it. A rising p99 traced to a saturated connection pool is the canonical chain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why instrument with OpenTelemetry from day one instead of adding observability later?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Instrumenting early establishes telemetry contracts and historical baselines before the system becomes difficult to change. Retrofitting during an incident requires invasive changes across several services and still cannot recover signals that were never emitted. OpenTelemetry also reduces the application changes needed to switch backends, although exporters, collector routing, queries, dashboards, and sampling policies may still change.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="devops-deployment-strategies">Deployment Strategies</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose canary over blue-green deployment?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Blue-green gives instant rollback by switching traffic back to the old environment, but requires running two full environments simultaneously.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary gradually shifts traffic to the new version, catching issues in production with real users before full rollout.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose canary when you need production validation with real traffic patterns that staging cannot replicate.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose blue-green when you need atomic cutover and can afford double infrastructure cost during the switch.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary requires traffic splitting infrastructure (service mesh, load balancer rules, or a progressive delivery controller like Argo Rollouts).</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary shrinks blast radius but stretches the deployment window and leans on monitoring automation to catch regressions; blue-green is simpler but doubles infrastructure cost during the switch.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What failure mode makes rolling deployments unsafe for database schema changes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Rolling deployments run old and new code side by side during the rollout window.</li>
<li dir="auto">A destructive schema change (dropping a column, renaming a table, changing a constraint) breaks the old instances still serving traffic.</li>
<li dir="auto">Safe approach: expand-and-contract migrations — add the new column first, deploy code that writes to both, then remove the old column in a later release.</li>
<li dir="auto">If schema changes are not backward-compatible, rolling deployment causes partial failures, data corruption, or 500 errors from old instances.</li>
<li dir="auto">Expand-and-contract spreads one change across several deploys and adds migration bookkeeping, but it is the only safe path to zero-downtime schema evolution under rolling deploys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide between A/B testing and canary when both are available?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Canary validates operational health — does the new version crash, timeout, or degrade throughput.</li>
<li dir="auto">A/B testing validates user behavior — does the new version improve conversion, engagement, or other business metrics.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use canary first to confirm the release is safe, then A/B test to measure business impact.</li>
<li dir="auto">A/B testing requires statistically significant traffic volumes and longer observation windows than canary health checks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary can auto-rollback on error rate spikes in minutes; A/B tests typically run days or weeks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Running both gives the most confidence but demands traffic management, metric pipelines, and longer release cycles — justified for high-impact user-facing changes, overkill for an internal service.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="devops-version-control-systems">Version Control Systems</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does GitFlow create integration problems for teams practicing continuous deployment?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">GitFlow's long-lived <code>develop</code> branch accumulates divergence from <code>main</code> over days or weeks. Feature branches branch off <code>develop</code>, so they also diverge. When multiple features merge back, conflicts compound. The <code>release/*</code> stabilization phase adds a manual gate that prevents continuous deployment. For web services that deploy multiple times per day, GitFlow's overhead is pure cost with no benefit. Use trunk-based development or GitHub Flow instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Version Control Systems/Branching Stratagies.md" class="internal-link">Branching Stratagies</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does trunk-based development require that makes it unsuitable for all teams?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Trunk-based development needs small integrable changes, a protected and quickly repaired trunk, and feedback fast enough that developers do not stack work on an unknown result. Feature flags are one way to separate deployment from exposure, but branch-by-abstraction and dark code paths can serve the same purpose. A ten-minute CI target is a useful heuristic, not a definition. Teams with slow review or validation can use GitHub Flow while measuring and reducing that latency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Version Control Systems/Branching Stratagies.md" class="internal-link">Branching Stratagies</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="networks">Networks</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the practical difference between a Layer 4 and a Layer 7 load balancer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An <strong>L4</strong> balancer routes by IP and port only — it's fast and protocol-agnostic but can't see inside the connection, so it distributes whole TCP connections (and pins all multiplexed HTTP/2/gRPC streams to one backend). An <strong>L7</strong> balancer parses the application protocol (HTTP), so it can route by URL/host/header, balance individual requests, terminate TLS, and retry — at higher per-request cost. The choice hinges on whether you need application-aware routing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the OSI model map onto the actual TCP/IP stack?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TCP/IP has four layers and folds OSI 5–7 into a single Application layer: Application (HTTP/gRPC/TLS/DNS) ↔ OSI 5–7, Transport (TCP/UDP) ↔ OSI 4, Internet (IP) ↔ OSI 3, Link (Ethernet/Wi-Fi) ↔ OSI 1–2. In conversation, "Layer 4" means TCP/UDP and "Layer 7" means HTTP-level protocols.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">At which layer do IP addresses, ports, and MAC addresses each operate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>MAC addresses</strong> at Layer 2 (Data Link) identify a NIC on a local link; <strong>IP addresses</strong> at Layer 3 (Network) identify a host across networks and enable routing; <strong>ports</strong> at Layer 4 (Transport) identify the specific process/socket on that host. A connection is uniquely identified by the L3+L4 tuple (src IP:port, dst IP:port).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between REST/HTTP, gRPC, and WebSockets for service-to-service communication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">REST over HTTP is the default for public and cross-team APIs: cacheable, debuggable with ordinary tools, and universally supported, at the cost of verbose payloads and request/response-only semantics</li>
<li dir="auto">gRPC wins for high-volume internal calls: binary Protobuf encoding, HTTP/2 multiplexing, and generated typed clients cut latency and boilerplate, but it needs tooling and is awkward to call from a browser</li>
<li dir="auto">WebSockets fit genuinely bidirectional workloads like chat and collaborative editing; Server-Sent Events fit one-way server push — live updates, notifications — where the alternative is the client polling</li>
<li dir="auto">The deciding questions are audience (public vs internal), traffic shape (request/response vs streaming), and whether human-debuggable payloads are worth the extra bytes<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Networks.md" class="internal-link">Networks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="networks-architecture-ops">Architecture &amp; Ops</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a CDN reduce latency, and what determines a cache hit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Latency is dominated by distance (round-trip time grows with how far light/packets travel). A CDN serves content from an edge node geographically near the user instead of a distant origin, cutting RTT. Whether a request is a <strong>hit</strong> depends on the cache key (usually the URL) and freshness: if a fresh copy exists per the <code>Cache-Control</code>/<code>ETag</code> headers, it's served from the edge; otherwise the edge fetches from origin (a miss) and caches the result.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you push a change live when content is cached on a CDN?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Either purge/invalidate the specific URL or cache tag (explicit but rate-limited and not instantaneous everywhere), or — better for static assets — use <strong>content-hashed, immutable URLs</strong> so a new version is a brand-new URL that nothing stale points to. For HTML that references those assets, keep a short TTL or <code>no-cache</code> so it picks up the new asset URLs promptly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What content should you NOT cache on a CDN, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Per-user or sensitive responses (anything containing a logged-in user's data, or behind auth) must not sit in a <em>shared</em> edge cache, or one user's data can be served to another. Mark them <code>Cache-Control: private</code> / <code>no-store</code>, or only cache with a per-user cache key. Static, public, identical-for-everyone content is the ideal CDN candidate.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is consistency hard in P2P systems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">P2P systems have no central authority to act as the source of truth. When peers hold different versions of data and there is no coordinator to resolve conflicts, the system must rely on eventual consistency — peers eventually converge to the same state through gossip or reconciliation protocols. This is acceptable for file distribution (BitTorrent) or content-addressed storage (IPFS) where data is immutable, but it makes P2P unsuitable for systems requiring strong consistency (financial transactions, inventory).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/Peer-2-Peer.md" class="internal-link">Peer-2-Peer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does WebRTC use P2P, and what is the role of STUN/TURN servers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">WebRTC establishes direct peer connections between browsers for audio, video, and data. The challenge is NAT traversal: most browsers are behind NAT and don't have public IP addresses. STUN servers help peers discover their public IP/port. When direct connection fails (symmetric NAT), TURN servers relay traffic. The goal is to minimize relay usage — direct P2P connections reduce latency and server cost; TURN relay is the fallback.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/Peer-2-Peer.md" class="internal-link">Peer-2-Peer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is split tunneling and when would you use it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Split tunneling routes only traffic destined for private resources through the VPN; internet traffic goes directly. Use it to reduce gateway bandwidth cost and latency for internet-bound traffic. Avoid it when corporate policy requires all traffic to pass through security inspection (content filtering, DLP).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does WireGuard have a smaller attack surface than IPsec?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">WireGuard is ~4,000 lines of code (auditable by a small team) vs IPsec's ~400,000 lines. Fewer lines means fewer potential vulnerabilities. WireGuard also uses a fixed, modern cryptographic suite (ChaCha20, Curve25519, BLAKE2) with no negotiation — removing cipher selection as an attack vector.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes DNS leaks in a VPN setup, and how do you fix them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">DNS queries bypass the encrypted tunnel — typically because the OS sends DNS requests to the default interface rather than the VPN interface. Fix: route DNS through the tunnel by setting DNS to an internal server and ensuring DNS requests use the VPN's routing table, or by running a local DNS resolver that forces all queries through the tunnel.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="networks-protocols">Protocols</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does DNS migration feel delayed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because recursive and client caches hold old TTLs; lowering TTL at cutover does not rewrite already-cached answers instantly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/DNS.md" class="internal-link">DNS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can authoritative data be correct while clients still use old IPs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Those clients or resolvers still hold old cached records; the old value remains valid until TTL expiry and resolver policies permit refresh.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/DNS.md" class="internal-link">DNS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve compared to HTTP/1.1?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HTTP/1.1 has no independent request streams. Optional pipelining permits multiple outstanding requests on one connection, but responses must return in request order, so a slow earlier response delays later ones. Browsers typically work around this with 6–8 parallel connections. HTTP/2 multiplexes independent streams on one connection, removing that response-order dependency and reducing connection overhead.<br />
Cost: a single TCP connection means TCP-layer packet loss affects all streams simultaneously.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HTTP/2 still have head-of-line blocking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HTTP/2 removes HTTP/1.1's response-order dependency but not TCP's connection-wide ordered delivery. A missing TCP segment can delay later data for every stream on that connection. HTTP/3 limits transport loss recovery to the affected QUIC stream, while ordered delivery still applies inside that stream and shared compression/control dependencies can introduce other waits.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose HTTP/1.1 over HTTP/2?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the network has high packet loss (mobile, satellite) and you cannot use HTTP/3 — multiple HTTP/1.1 connections isolate packet loss better than a single HTTP/2 connection. Also when connecting to legacy servers or proxies that don't support HTTP/2.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a retry-time <code>412</code> mean for a conditional <code>PUT</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The first request may already have succeeded and changed the entity tag. The retry is allowed because <code>PUT</code> is idempotent, but its <code>412</code> cannot distinguish a successful first attempt from another writer's update. Read the current representation and reconcile before choosing a new precondition.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/REST.md" class="internal-link">REST</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should <code>POST</code> be idempotent in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Only when the client sends a durable idempotency key and the server enforces dedupe boundaries; otherwise <code>POST</code> can create duplicates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/REST.md" class="internal-link">REST</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is RPC's 'local call' abstraction considered leaky?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RPC hides the network boundary, but the network introduces failures that local calls never have: calls can time out, be delivered twice (at-least-once delivery), or fail with the server having already executed the operation. A failed RPC does not mean the server didn't execute — the response may have been lost. This forces callers to implement idempotency keys, retries with backoff, and circuit breakers — concerns that don't exist for local calls. The abstraction is useful but must not be trusted blindly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/RPC.md" class="internal-link">RPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose gRPC over REST?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose gRPC for internal service-to-service communication where: (1) you need high throughput or low latency (binary Protobuf is 3-10x smaller than JSON), (2) you need bidirectional streaming, or (3) you want strongly typed contracts enforced at compile time. Choose REST for public APIs, browser clients (gRPC requires a proxy for browsers), or when human-readable payloads matter for debugging.
