<p dir="auto">The call stack is a per-thread LIFO memory region that stores stack frames for active method calls (return address, parameters, locals, etc.).<br />
It can overflow (for example, due to deep or infinite recursion or very large stack allocations). In .NET this typically results in <code>StackOverflowException</code>, and the process is terminated (it cannot be reliably handled).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do we need <code>using {}</code> if there is a GC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>using</code> provides deterministic cleanup for resources that are not just managed memory (file handles, sockets, OS handles, unmanaged buffers). GC runs non-deterministically and does not guarantee timely release of such resources.<br />
The <code>using</code> statement compiles to <code>try/finally</code> so <code>Dispose()</code> is called even when exceptions occur.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are <code>IDisposable</code> and <code>Finalize</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>IDisposable</code> is an interface for explicit, deterministic cleanup via <code>Dispose()</code>.<br />
<code>Finalize</code> (a finalizer, written as <code>~TypeName()</code> in C#) is called by the GC for objects that have a finalizer, but it is non-deterministic and adds overhead. Finalizers should only be used to release unmanaged resources, and <code>Dispose()</code> typically calls <code>GC.SuppressFinalize(this)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the disposable (dispose) pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A standard way to implement <code>IDisposable</code> so both explicit cleanup (<code>Dispose()</code>) and (optionally) finalization are supported.<br />
Typical shape: <code>Dispose()</code> calls <code>Dispose(true)</code> and then <code>GC.SuppressFinalize(this)</code>; a finalizer (if needed) calls <code>Dispose(false)</code>; <code>Dispose(bool disposing)</code> releases unmanaged resources and, when <code>disposing</code> is true, also disposes managed fields.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the CLR do when your application starts, and why does startup behavior matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The CLR loads assemblies, verifies IL safety, JIT-compiles methods on first call (or uses tiered compilation to optimize hot paths later), sets up the GC, and initializes the thread pool.<br />
This matters because startup latency, JIT warmup effects, and thread pool sizing all affect real-world behavior — especially for serverless and containerized deployments with cold starts.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does garbage collection affect production latency, and what are the main tuning levers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">GC pauses application threads (in workstation GC) or background-collects (in server GC) to reclaim memory. Gen0/Gen1 collections are fast; Gen2 collections are expensive and can cause visible latency spikes.<br />
Main tuning levers: choose Server vs Workstation GC mode, minimize large object heap allocations (objects over 85KB), reduce Gen2 promotion rates by controlling object lifetimes, and use <code>GC.TryStartNoGCRegion</code> for latency-critical paths.<br />
Always measure with GC event traces (dotnet-counters, PerfView) before tuning — premature GC optimization often makes things worse.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Can managed code have memory leaks, and what are the common causes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes. Common causes: event handler subscriptions never unsubscribed, static collections that grow indefinitely, closures capturing references unexpectedly, and finalizer queue stalls blocking reclamation.<br />
These are not OS-level leaks but logical leaks — the GC cannot collect objects that are still reachable through a live reference chain, even if the application no longer needs them.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="security">Security</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is blockchain justified over a traditional database?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Blockchain is justified when you need a shared ledger across mutually distrusting parties with no central authority and no single party can be trusted to maintain the record. If all parties trust a central authority, a traditional database with append-only audit logging is simpler, faster, and GDPR-compliant. The cost of blockchain — low throughput, immutability conflicts with erasure rights, consensus overhead — is only worth paying when decentralization is a hard requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Block-chain.md" class="internal-link">Block-chain</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does PoW waste energy and what is the alternative?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Proof-of-Work requires miners to perform computationally expensive hash searches to earn the right to add a block. This energy expenditure is the security mechanism — attacking the chain requires outspending honest miners. Proof-of-Stake replaces energy expenditure with economic stake: validators lock up tokens as collateral and lose them if they act dishonestly. PoS achieves similar security guarantees at a fraction of the energy cost, which is why Ethereum migrated from PoW to PoS in 2022.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Block-chain.md" class="internal-link">Block-chain</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does signing use the private key to encrypt the hash, not the public key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Signing does not generally encrypt a hash. A signature scheme uses the private key to create a value that anyone with the public key can verify for the exact message. The asymmetry makes creation exclusive to the private-key holder while leaving verification public.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between signing and encryption?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Signing proves authenticity and integrity under a trusted key — it does not hide the content. Encryption hides the content but does not identify the sender. In TLS, the client validates the certificate chain, validity period, hostname, key usage, and configured revocation policy; the handshake proves the server holds the corresponding private key, and the negotiated session keys encrypt traffic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between ECDSA and RSA?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Follow the protocol's allowed algorithms and encodings, then check every signer, verifier, HSM, and rotation path. ECDSA P-256 offers shorter keys and signatures; RSA often has broader compatibility with existing infrastructure. Neither should be selected by an untrusted message or treated as a universal default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use symmetric vs asymmetric encryption?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use symmetric authenticated encryption for bulk data. Use a public-key encryption scheme only for the small payload and protocol it was designed for, a signature scheme for public verification, and authenticated key agreement to establish fresh shared secrets. Real systems compose these through protocols such as TLS or envelope encryption rather than choosing one family for the whole job.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Encryption.md" class="internal-link">Encryption</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is envelope encryption and when is it used?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Envelope encryption uses two keys: a Data Encryption Key (DEK) encrypts the data; a Key Encryption Key (KEK) encrypts the DEK. The encrypted DEK is stored alongside the ciphertext. The KEK lives in a key management service (Azure Key Vault, AWS KMS) and never leaves it. This pattern is used in cloud storage (Azure Blob Storage, S3 server-side encryption) and allows key rotation without re-encrypting all data — you only re-encrypt the DEK.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Encryption.md" class="internal-link">Encryption</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can't you use SHA-256 directly to store passwords?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SHA-256 is engineered to be <em>fast</em>, which is exactly wrong for passwords: an attacker who steals the hash database can compute billions of guesses per second and crack weak/common passwords quickly, and without a salt they can use precomputed rainbow tables. Password hashing needs a <strong>slow, memory-hard, salted</strong> function (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2) with a tunable work factor so each guess is expensive and every user's hash is unique.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between a hash, an HMAC, and a digital signature?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A <strong>hash</strong> is keyless and only detects accidental change — an attacker can alter data and recompute it. An <strong>HMAC</strong> mixes in a shared secret key, so it proves integrity <em>and</em> authenticity between parties who both hold the key. A <strong>digital signature</strong> hashes then signs with a <em>private</em> key, so anyone with the public key can verify, adding <strong>non-repudiation</strong> (the signer can't deny it) that HMAC lacks because its key is shared. Escalating from hash → HMAC → signature trades simplicity for stronger guarantees.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a salt protect against, and why must it be unique per user?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A salt is a per-password random value stored with the hash. It defeats <strong>rainbow tables</strong> (precomputed digest→password lookups can't be built without knowing each salt) and ensures that two users who pick the same password produce <em>different</em> stored hashes, so a cracker can't crack many accounts at once or spot shared passwords. A single global salt would still let one rainbow table target the whole database — uniqueness per user is what forces the attacker to attack each hash individually.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a JWT token and why is it not encrypted by default?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">JWT is a compact token format: <code>header.payload.signature</code> (base64url encoded).</li>
<li dir="auto">Signed (JWS) means the signature proves integrity and authenticity under the selected trusted key; issuer validation binds that key and token to the expected issuer. The payload is still readable by anyone with the token.</li>
<li dir="auto">Encryption (JWE) is a separate standard that wraps the JWT in an encrypted envelope.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: signed-only JWTs are simpler and faster to validate; JWE adds encryption overhead and key management complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/JWT Bearer.md" class="internal-link">JWT Bearer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should JWTs have short expiry times?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A locally validated bearer token remains usable until expiry unless the resource server also checks revocation or session state.</li>
<li dir="auto">Shorter lifetimes bound that replay window but increase renewal traffic and dependence on the issuer.</li>
<li dir="auto">Refresh tokens or another renewal credential need stronger storage, rotation, replay detection, and revocation than access tokens.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose the lifetime from the operation's impact and the system's ability to detect and terminate compromise, not a universal minute value.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/JWT Bearer.md" class="internal-link">JWT Bearer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does passing an OWASP Top 10 checklist not prove an application is secure?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The Top 10 groups broad, common risk classes; it does not enumerate the application's assets, trust boundaries, business rules, or attackers.</li>
<li dir="auto">A system can prevent listed injection patterns and still let a valid customer refund another customer's order through broken workflow authorization.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use the list to seed threat models and verification, then add abuse cases, architecture-specific controls, dependency review, and incident exercises.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/OWASP.md" class="internal-link">OWASP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent SQL injection in a .NET application?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use parameterized queries always — EF Core and Dapper with parameters are safe by default.</li>
<li dir="auto">Never concatenate user input into SQL strings, even for dynamic ORDER BY or table names.</li>
<li dir="auto">For dynamic SQL, use allowlists (validate column names against a known set) rather than sanitization.</li>
<li dir="auto">Do not assume an ORM makes interpolated raw SQL safe; verify which APIs parameterize and which execute literal command text.</li>
<li dir="auto">Raw SQL is appropriate for performance-sensitive paths when every value remains parameterized and dynamic identifiers come from a closed allowlist.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/OWASP.md" class="internal-link">OWASP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A secret was accidentally committed and pushed. What's the correct response?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treat it as compromised and <strong>rotate (revoke + reissue) it immediately</strong> — that's the only real fix, because the value persists in Git history, clones, forks, and CI caches even after you delete it. Rewriting history (e.g. <code>git filter-repo</code>) is useful cleanup to stop further exposure, but it does not un-leak a secret that others may already have. Afterward, add push protection / secret scanning so it can't recur.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the "secret zero" (bootstrapping) problem and how is it solved?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">If all your secrets live in a vault, the app still needs <em>one</em> credential to authenticate to the vault — secret zero. Storing it just moves the problem. The modern solution is <strong>platform-provided identity</strong>: a managed identity (Azure) / IAM role (AWS) / workload identity (Kubernetes/GCP) that the infrastructure vouches for, letting the workload obtain a short-lived token with no stored secret. CI pipelines do the same via OIDC federation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are environment variables a weak place to store secrets despite being common?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They keep secrets out of the image (good, and the 12-factor standard), but the whole process and any child processes can read them, they're easy to leak accidentally (a crash dump, a <code>printenv</code> in a build log, an APM that captures env), they aren't audited or versioned, and rotating them means redeploying. A dedicated secret store adds access control, auditing, versioning, and rotation that env vars lack — so env vars are fine for dev but a managed store is better for production.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the practical difference between authentication and authorization, and why do bugs cluster at the boundary?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Authentication establishes identity (who); authorization enforces permission (what) — a system can authenticate perfectly and still leak data through a missing authz check</li>
<li dir="auto">The classic failure is the insecure direct object reference: a valid, logged-in user changes an ID in a URL and reads someone else's record because the code checked authn but not ownership</li>
<li dir="auto">Authorization must be enforced server-side on every request, close to the data — never in the UI, never assumed from a prior step</li>
<li dir="auto">Tokens (JWTs, sessions) carry both concerns, so validating a token is necessary but not sufficient; you still authorize the specific action<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Security.md" class="internal-link">Security</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should secrets and cryptography be handled so they don't become the incident?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Never hand-roll crypto: use vetted libraries and standard algorithms — the failures are almost always in usage (nonce reuse, ECB mode, weak KDFs), not the primitives</li>
<li dir="auto">Hash passwords with a slow, salted password hash (bcrypt/argon2/PBKDF2), never a plain hash; encrypt data in transit and at rest with managed keys</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep secrets out of source, logs, and config: use a secrets manager, rotate them, and scope them narrowly</li>
<li dir="auto">Assume any secret that reaches a log or an error message is compromised — design so it never does<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Security.md" class="internal-link">Security</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does parameterizing a query fully prevent SQL injection, where input filtering does not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A parameter is transmitted to the database <strong>separately from the SQL text</strong>, and the query is parsed/planned <em>before</em> the value is bound — so the value can never change the statement's structure, no matter what characters it contains. Filtering tries to anticipate every dangerous input and always misses encodings/edge cases. Parameterization removes the entire class of attack rather than playing whack-a-mole with payloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between XSS and CSRF?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>XSS</strong> runs the attacker's <em>code</em> in the victim's browser within your site's origin (so it can read the DOM, cookies, and act with full privilege) — caused by unescaped untrusted data in your output. <strong>CSRF</strong> runs no code in your origin; it tricks the victim's browser into <em>sending a request</em> to your site using its existing cookies, and the attacker can't even read the response — caused by browsers auto-attaching cookies. XSS is fixed by output encoding/CSP; CSRF by anti-forgery tokens and <code>SameSite</code>. Notably, an XSS hole can defeat CSRF tokens, so XSS is the more severe of the two.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are header-token (JWT) APIs less exposed to CSRF than cookie-session apps?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">CSRF works because browsers automatically include cookies on cross-site requests without the attacker's page needing to read or set anything. A bearer token sent in the <code>Authorization</code> header is <em>not</em> auto-attached — JavaScript on the attacker's origin can't read your token (same-origin policy) and can't set the header on a forged cross-site request. So pure header-token APIs sidestep CSRF; the risk returns the moment you store the token in a cookie, which is why cookie storage (good against XSS) must be paired with <code>SameSite</code> + anti-forgery.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="security-authentication">Authentication</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Basic Auth unsafe over HTTP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Base64 is encoding, not encryption — it is trivially reversible. Over HTTP, the <code>Authorization</code> header is sent in plaintext and visible to any network observer. Over HTTPS, the header is encrypted by TLS, making Basic Auth safe to use.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Basic Auth.md" class="internal-link">Basic Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Basic Auth acceptable in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For machine-to-machine calls between trusted services on an internal network over HTTPS, Basic Auth is acceptable when simplicity matters and the credential is a service account (not a user password). For user-facing authentication, use OAuth 2.0 / JWT Bearer — Basic Auth requires sending credentials on every request, which increases exposure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Basic Auth.md" class="internal-link">Basic Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an access token not be used as an ID token?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The access token is issued for a resource server and carries delegated authority. Its format and claims are controlled by that API contract. The ID token is issued for the OIDC client and has explicit authentication claims and validation rules, including audience and nonce.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect).md" class="internal-link">Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do <code>state</code>, <code>nonce</code>, and PKCE each bind?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>state</code> binds the callback to the initiating browser session, <code>nonce</code> binds the ID token to the OIDC request, and PKCE binds the authorization code to the client instance that created the verifier. One does not substitute for the others.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect).md" class="internal-link">Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between role-based and resource-based authorization?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Role-based authorization checks what type of user you are (e.g., Admin, Editor). Resource-based authorization checks your relationship to a specific resource instance (e.g., are you the owner of this document?). Use role-based for coarse-grained access control; use resource-based when the decision depends on data.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Resource-based Auth.md" class="internal-link">Resource-based Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why inject <code>IAuthorizationService</code> instead of checking ownership in the controller directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>IAuthorizationService</code> centralizes authorization logic in handlers, making it testable and reusable across controllers. Direct ownership checks in controllers scatter authorization logic, making it easy to miss a check or apply it inconsistently. The handler pattern also supports multiple requirements composing into a single policy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Resource-based Auth.md" class="internal-link">Resource-based Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the server not hash a TOTP secret like a password?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Verification requires the server to compute HMAC with the same secret as the authenticator. Store it encrypted with a separately protected key, restrict decrypt access, and rotate it after suspected exposure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/TOTP.md" class="internal-link">TOTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why remember the last accepted time step?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A code remains mathematically valid for its entire accepted window. Recording the step and updating it atomically turns a valid captured code into a single-use value at that verifier.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/TOTP.md" class="internal-link">TOTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does WebAuthn resist a real-time phishing proxy better than TOTP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TOTP is a transferable number that a proxy can relay before it expires. WebAuthn signs data bound to the browser-observed origin and RP ID, so a credential for the real site will not produce a valid assertion for the phishing origin.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Two-Factor Auth.md" class="internal-link">Two-Factor Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is a synced passkey the same trust model as a hardware security key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No. Both use WebAuthn origin-bound public-key credentials, but a synced passkey relies on a platform account and encrypted synchronization/recovery, while a device-bound security key keeps the private key on one authenticator and needs a separate backup path.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Two-Factor Auth.md" class="internal-link">Two-Factor Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does SSO not mean one shared session?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The IdP and each relying application remain distinct session and security boundaries. The IdP session can make authentication at another application silent, but that application must validate a new federation result and issue its own session.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/SSO (Single Sign-On).md" class="internal-link">SSO (Single Sign-On)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which identity should a relying party store?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Store the pair of configured issuer and stable <code>sub</code> claim. Email and display names can change or be reassigned; they are attributes, not durable federation keys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/SSO (Single Sign-On).md" class="internal-link">SSO (Single Sign-On)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="software-architecture">Software Architecture</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between a monolith, a modular monolith, and microservices?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The decision is mainly organizational: microservices buy independent deployment and team autonomy at the cost of network calls, distributed failure modes, and operational overhead</li>
<li dir="auto">A modular monolith captures most of the boundary benefits — clear module seams, enforced dependencies — while keeping one deployable and in-process calls; it's the right default for most teams</li>
<li dir="auto">Reach for microservices when independent scaling or deployment or team boundaries genuinely demand it, not because it's fashionable, and rarely before you understand the domain boundaries</li>
<li dir="auto">Migration is one-directional and cheap the right way round: a well-structured modular monolith can later be carved into services along its existing seams<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Software Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Software Architecture</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="software-architecture-application-architecture">Application Architecture</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Clean Architecture differ from traditional N Layer, and when does the extra indirection pay off</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">N Layer often keeps downward dependencies from UI to business to data, while Clean Architecture enforces inward dependencies toward policy.</li>
<li dir="auto">In Clean Architecture, inner layers define contracts and outer layers implement them, so domain logic survives framework changes.</li>
<li dir="auto">The indirection pays off when business rules are complex, testing speed matters, and infrastructure churn is expected over multiple years.</li>
<li dir="auto">For short lived services with shallow rules, the same indirection can slow delivery with little resilience gain.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff statement: Clean Architecture buys adaptability and testability by paying upfront complexity and wiring overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Clean Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Clean Architecture</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens when you apply Clean Architecture to a simple CRUD service</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">You often create command handlers ports and adapters that do little more than pass data through, so cycle time drops without stronger correctness.</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams spend effort maintaining abstractions instead of shipping user value because domain complexity never materializes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Operationally it can still work, but the architecture tax shows up in onboarding and debugging cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">A pragmatic approach is to keep boundaries light and evolve toward stricter clean boundaries only where invariants and integration volatility appear.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff statement: using full Clean Architecture too early protects against hypothetical future change while charging real present complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Clean Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Clean Architecture</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the Dependency Rule and why does it matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All source code dependencies must point inward — toward higher-level policies (domain). Outer layers (infrastructure, UI) depend on inner layers; inner layers never depend on outer layers. This means you can change databases, frameworks, or delivery mechanisms without touching business rules.<br />
Cost: requires defining interfaces in inner layers and wiring implementations in outer layers — more upfront structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Layered Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Layered Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between traditional layered and Onion/Clean Architecture?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Traditional layered: UI → Business Logic → Data Access → Database. Changing the DB affects everything above. Onion/Clean: Infrastructure → Application → Domain. The Domain has zero dependencies; Infrastructure implements Domain interfaces. The key difference is dependency inversion at the data access boundary.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Layered Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Layered Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent version conflicts between plug-ins that depend on different versions of the same library?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>AssemblyLoadContext</code> to isolate each plug-in's dependencies. Each context has its own assembly resolution scope, so plug-in A's dependency on <code>Newtonsoft.Json</code> 12.x does not conflict with plug-in B's dependency on 13.x. Cost: each context adds memory overhead and prevents type sharing across contexts — objects from one context cannot be cast to types from another.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you version extension point interfaces without breaking existing plug-ins?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treat the extension point interface as a public API with semantic versioning. For breaking changes, introduce a new interface version (<code>IPlugin</code>, <code>IPlugin2</code>) and support both simultaneously via an adapter. Existing plug-ins implement the old interface; new plug-ins implement the new one. The core adapts old plug-ins to the new contract. Cost: adapter proliferation over time — set a deprecation timeline and remove old interfaces after a migration window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is plug-in architecture the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When all features are known upfront and no third-party extensibility is needed. The loading complexity, versioning challenges, and security surface (untrusted code running in-process) are not justified for internal applications. A monolith with feature flags is simpler and safer. Plug-in architecture earns its complexity when customers or third parties need to extend the product without modifying the core.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the key difference between MVC and MVVM?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">MVC uses a controller to handle a request and select a view. MVVM exposes observable state and commands that a long-lived view binds to. MVVM pays for binding infrastructure; MVC fits stateless request/response rendering with less ceremony.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Presentation Architecture Variants.md" class="internal-link">Presentation Architecture Variants</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a coordinator earn its place?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Add one when navigation has branching policy that must be tested independently of screen state, such as checkout flows that can continue to confirmation, authentication, or payment recovery.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Application Architecture/Presentation Architecture Variants.md" class="internal-link">Presentation Architecture Variants</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="software-architecture-distributed-systems">Distributed Systems</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design gateway aggregation endpoints for client efficiency, and what do you keep out of the gateway?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use gateway routing for normal traffic and add a few targeted aggregation endpoints where a client — usually mobile — would otherwise make five round trips for one screen. The gateway composes those reads and tunes the payload, but it stays thin: auth, throttling, routing, transformation, observability, and nothing else. Business rules, transactions, and domain invariants live in the backend services, with correlation IDs flowing across the fan-out so you can trace a slow screen. The line to hold: aggregation is response-shaping, not orchestration — the moment decision logic creeps in, you have a distributed monolith.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/API Gateway.md" class="internal-link">API Gateway</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where do API Gateway and service mesh responsibilities belong in one architecture?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They handle different traffic planes, so they sit side by side rather than compete. The gateway owns north-south traffic — clients entering the system — so edge auth, TLS termination, external rate limits, and API surface control belong there. The mesh owns east-west traffic between internal services: mTLS, retries, traffic shifting, and per-service telemetry. The gateway guards the front door; the mesh governs the hallways.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/API Gateway.md" class="internal-link">API Gateway</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is a system "CP" or "AP" as a whole?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Don't think of it system-wide — decide per operation, because different endpoints have different correctness budgets. A <code>PlaceOrder</code> or ledger write wants CP: refuse it under partition rather than risk split-brain or a double charge. A <code>GetRecommendations</code> read wants AP: keep serving slightly stale data because availability beats freshness there. A profile read might only need session consistency. So the same system is CP on some paths and AP on others; labeling the whole platform and applying one rule everywhere is the mistake. Map each operation to its business invariant and its allowed staleness window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/CAP theorem.md" class="internal-link">CAP theorem</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is "pick two of three" a misleading way to state CAP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because in any system that replicates data across machines, partition tolerance isn't something you opt out of — networks drop packets and isolate nodes whether you like it or not. So "CA" isn't really on the menu: the moment a partition hits, a system that didn't plan for it just stops being correct or stops responding. The honest framing is that P is a given, and the real decision is what you do <em>during</em> a partition — sacrifice consistency to stay available (AP), or sacrifice availability to stay consistent (CP). With no partition you can have both; CAP only forces the choice inside the failure window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/CAP theorem.md" class="internal-link">CAP theorem</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you guarantee read-your-writes when writes go to a strong store but reads come from an eventually consistent cache?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The problem is the gap between a committed write and a cache that hasn't caught up. The cleanest fix is to route that user's immediate post-write reads past the cache to a strong or session-consistent path, just while freshness matters. Pair it with write-through or explicit invalidation on update so the cache converges quickly, and carry a version or timestamp so you can reject a stale follow-up write. Keep the writes idempotent so a retry is harmless. You don't need global strong consistency — only read-your-writes for the one user who just acted.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which chat features should use linearizable, causal, and eventual consistency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Map each feature to the weakest model that still feels correct. Message ordering and reply chains need at least causal consistency — a reply must never show up before the message it answers. Typing indicators can be eventual; a dropped or reordered one costs nothing, and the latency win is worth it. Read receipts in a compliance-sensitive context might justify something stronger. The skill on display is refusing a one-size-fits-all level: spend strong-consistency latency only where a user would actually notice the inconsistency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can linearizability reduce availability during a network partition, and how do you contain the blast radius?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Linearizable operations need a quorum to agree on one order, and a partition can cut a node off from that quorum. To stop two sides committing conflicting truths, the isolated side must reject or delay those operations — that refusal is the availability you lose (the CP corner of CAP). Contain the blast radius by scoping strong consistency to the writes that truly need it — payments, inventory decrements — and serving everything else from weaker models with explicit degraded-mode behavior. Partition tolerance isn't optional; the real choice is which operations fail closed and which keep serving.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is 2PC problematic in microservices?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">2PC requires all participants to hold locks during the prepare-to-commit window. In microservices, this window spans network calls, making lock duration unpredictable.</li>
<li dir="auto">The coordinator is a single point of failure. A crash after PREPARE but before COMMIT leaves participants locked indefinitely.</li>
<li dir="auto">Most microservice infrastructure (HTTP APIs, NoSQL, cloud queues) doesn't support XA transactions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: 2PC gives strong consistency but at the cost of availability and throughput. CAP theorem: under a partition, you must choose consistency or availability — 2PC chooses consistency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions.md" class="internal-link">Distributed Transactions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the Outbox pattern guarantee at-least-once event delivery?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The event is written to an <code>OutboxMessages</code> table in the same DB transaction as the domain change. If the transaction commits, the event is durable.</li>
<li dir="auto">A background worker reads unprocessed outbox entries and publishes them to the broker, retrying until the broker acknowledges.</li>
<li dir="auto">This guarantees at-least-once delivery (the event may be published more than once if the worker crashes after publishing but before marking the entry as processed).</li>
<li dir="auto">Consumers must be idempotent to handle duplicate events.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: adds a background worker and an extra DB table. The cost is worth it for any event that must not be lost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions.md" class="internal-link">Distributed Transactions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you pick a load-balancing algorithm for requests with highly variable duration, like AI inference?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Round robin is the right default when instances are similar and requests cost about the same, but it falls apart when duration varies — exactly the inference case, where one long completion ties up an instance while round robin keeps handing it new work. Least-connections handles that better by routing to the instance with the fewest in-flight requests, which tracks real load when durations are uneven. The catch is that connection count doesn't capture per-request CPU cost, so a few short-but-expensive prompts can still skew it. For inference you validate the choice by measuring p95/p99 latency and backend saturation under representative load rather than trusting any default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you choose an L4 load balancer over L7?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">L4 balances on connection metadata — IP, port, protocol — without reading the payload, so it's fast and works for any TCP/UDP traffic, but it can't decide based on HTTP path, host, or headers. L7 parses HTTP, so it can route by path or header and do canary, A/B, and edge auth, at the cost of more processing per request. Choose L4 when you need raw throughput and generic transport routing; choose L7 the moment routing depends on request content. Plenty of systems use both — L4 at the edge for raw distribution, L7 deeper for content-aware routing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must readiness checks differ from liveness checks behind a load balancer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They drive different actions. Liveness asks whether the process can make progress and commonly triggers restart, so it stays local and dependency-free. Readiness asks whether this instance should receive new traffic. It can include instance-specific initialization or a dependency failure unique to this replica, but blindly checking a database shared by every replica can evict the entire fleet. Keep shared-dependency health observable and put it in readiness only when routing elsewhere can help.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design webhook consumers to prevent event loss and duplicate processing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Verify the provider-defined signature or MAC over its documented input and enforce its replay-protection contract. Store the provider event ID and inbox/queue payload durably before returning an accepted response, then process it idempotently in a worker. Since the producer can exhaust its retry window while the consumer is unavailable, reconcile against the provider's event-list API or replay facility.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose webhooks over a shared message broker for inter-service event delivery?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Webhooks win when you cross an organizational boundary — a SaaS or third-party provider you don't control can't publish into your broker, but it can POST to a URL. Inside your own platform a message broker is usually better: durable fan-out, back-pressure, replay, and per-key ordering that raw HTTP callbacks don't give you. They aren't exclusive, though — the common pattern is to receive external webhooks at the edge and immediately republish them onto an internal broker, so you get HTTP reach at the boundary and broker guarantees everywhere inside.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you protect a webhook endpoint against replay attacks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Follow the provider's replay-protection contract: it may sign a timestamp, use a nonce, or issue short-lived asymmetric signatures. For an HMAC contract with a signed timestamp, verify the MAC over the exact documented payload and timestamp, reject requests outside the permitted clock window, and compare the MAC in constant time. A durable delivery ID still catches duplicate deliveries inside that window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is idempotency essential for reliable retry strategies, and what happens without it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Retries are a certainty in distributed systems because clients cannot always distinguish failure from delayed success.</li>
<li dir="auto">Idempotency converts ambiguous outcomes into deterministic behavior, so retries do not amplify side effects.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without it, transient faults become correctness bugs such as duplicate charges or duplicate shipment commands.</li>
<li dir="auto">Idempotency also improves operational recovery because replay jobs and dead letter reprocessing become safe.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stronger idempotency guarantees cost extra storage, key lifecycle management, and stricter request validation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Idempotency.md" class="internal-link">Idempotency</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you implement idempotency for a payment endpoint that processes charges through a third party provider?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Reserve a durable local attempt, commit, then call a provider that honors the same key outside the database transaction. A timeout becomes an unknown outcome that is reconciled with the same provider key; it is not evidence that no charge happened. <a class="internal-link" href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Payment Systems#Durable Idempotency" aria-label="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Payment Systems &gt; Durable Idempotency" data-tooltip-position="top">Payment Systems</a> contains the complete sequence and its boundary-specific guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Distributed Systems/Idempotency.md" class="internal-link">Idempotency</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Message Queues</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design a Kafka topic to keep per-customer ordering while handling high throughput?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>customer_id</code> when all events for one customer require one partition order.
