</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use Template Method vs Strategy for algorithm variation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Template Method uses inheritance — the variation is in a subclass. Strategy uses composition — the variation is in an injected object. Use Template Method when: the algorithm skeleton is stable, the variations are tightly coupled to the base class, and you don't need to swap algorithms at runtime. Use Strategy when: you need to swap algorithms at runtime, the algorithm is independent of the class using it, or you want to avoid inheritance. The tradeoff: Template Method is simpler (no extra interface) but creates tight inheritance coupling; Strategy is more flexible but requires an extra interface and injection.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Template Method.md" class="internal-link">Template Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the "Hollywood Principle" and how does Template Method implement it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">"Don't call us, we'll call you." The base class calls the subclass's methods (abstract steps), not the other way around. The subclass doesn't control when its methods are called — the template method does. This inverts the typical inheritance relationship: instead of the subclass calling <code>super.method()</code>, the base class calls <code>this.abstractStep()</code>. The benefit: the algorithm's structure is controlled by the base class; subclasses can't accidentally skip steps or change the order. The cost: subclasses are tightly coupled to the base class's algorithm structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Template Method.md" class="internal-link">Template Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is double dispatch and why does Visitor need it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Single dispatch: the method called depends on the runtime type of ONE object (the receiver). Double dispatch: the method called depends on the runtime types of TWO objects (the element AND the visitor). Without double dispatch, <code>visitor.Visit(item)</code> would call the <code>Visit(ICartItem)</code> overload — the compiler resolves overloads at compile time based on the declared type. <code>item.Accept(visitor)</code> → <code>visitor.Visit(this)</code> forces the compiler to resolve the overload based on <code>this</code>'s concrete type at runtime. The cost: two virtual calls instead of one; the pattern is non-obvious to developers unfamiliar with it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does EF Core use ExpressionVisitor, and what does it do?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">EF Core's query translator is an <code>ExpressionVisitor</code> that walks the LINQ expression tree and converts it to SQL. <code>dbContext.Orders.Where(o =&gt; o.Total &gt; 100).Select(o =&gt; o.Id)</code> builds an expression tree; EF Core visits each node: <code>VisitMethodCall</code> for <code>Where</code> and <code>Select</code>, <code>VisitBinary</code> for <code>o.Total &gt; 100</code>, <code>VisitMember</code> for <code>o.Total</code> and <code>o.Id</code>. Each visit emits SQL fragments. The final SQL is assembled from the visited nodes. This is why EF Core can translate LINQ to SQL but throws <code>InvalidOperationException</code> for expressions it can't translate — the visitor doesn't know how to visit that node type.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use pattern matching instead of Visitor?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the element hierarchy is small (2-4 types), changes frequently (new types added regularly), or the operations are simple (one-liners per type). Pattern matching is more readable, requires no <code>Accept()</code> method on elements, and handles new element types with a compiler warning (exhaustiveness checking with <code>_</code> catch-all). Visitor earns its complexity when: you have 5+ element types, 5+ operations, and the hierarchy is stable. The signal: if adding a new element type requires editing more than 3 visitor classes, the hierarchy is too unstable for Visitor.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Creational</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Builder's <code>Build()</code> method justify its existence over a constructor?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When <code>Build()</code> does work a constructor shouldn't: cross-field validation (gift wrap requires a message), computed fields (total = subtotal - discount), default derivation (billing = shipping if not set), or async initialization. Constructors should be fast and never throw business logic exceptions. <code>Build()</code> is the right place for invariant enforcement that spans multiple fields. The tradeoff: <code>Build()</code> can throw at runtime; <code>required</code> properties fail at compile time. Use <code>required</code> when all fields are independent; use Builder when fields interact.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Builder.md" class="internal-link">Builder</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the Director role work, and when do you need it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A Director encapsulates a specific construction sequence, calling builder methods in a fixed order. Example: <code>StandardOrderDirector.BuildGiftOrder(builder, customer, items)</code> always calls <code>ShipTo</code>, <code>WithGiftWrap</code>, <code>WithShipping(Express)</code> in the right order. Directors are useful when the same construction sequence is reused across multiple call sites, or when the sequence itself is a business rule (gift orders always use express shipping). Without a director, each call site must know the correct sequence — a form of knowledge duplication. The tradeoff: directors add a class; without them, call sites are more flexible but less consistent.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Builder.md" class="internal-link">Builder</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>WebApplicationBuilder</code> use a builder instead of a constructor with parameters?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because <code>WebApplication</code> construction is a multi-step process where each step can affect subsequent steps: adding a service can change how middleware behaves; configuration sources are layered in priority order; logging providers must be registered before the host starts. A constructor can't express this ordering or allow conditional registration. The builder accumulates all configuration, then <code>Build()</code> wires everything together in the correct dependency order. The cost: <code>Build()</code> can fail at runtime if configuration is invalid — there's no compile-time guarantee that all required services are registered.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Builder.md" class="internal-link">Builder</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you add a new product type (e.g., IFraudDetector) to an existing Abstract Factory without breaking all existing factories?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">You can't without modifying the interface — that's the fundamental tension. Options: (1) Add the method with a default implementation in the interface (<code>default IFraudDetector CreateFraudDetector() =&gt; new NoOpFraudDetector()</code>), which avoids breaking existing factories but hides missing implementations. (2) Use a separate <code>IFraudDetectorFactory</code> interface and compose it with <code>IPaymentProviderFactory</code> at the call site. (3) Accept the breaking change and update all factories — justified when the product is truly part of the family. The tradeoff: default implementations hide gaps; separate interfaces reduce cohesion; breaking changes are honest but costly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Abstract Factory overkill compared to a simpler approach?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you have only one product family (no need to swap providers), or when products don't need to stay compatible with each other. A single <code>IPaymentProcessor</code> interface with DI registration is sufficient if you never need <code>IReceiptGenerator</code> to match the processor's provider. Abstract Factory's value is the <strong>family constraint</strong> — if that constraint doesn't exist in your domain, you're adding indirection for no benefit. Cost: every new product type requires updating the factory interface and all implementations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Abstract Factory relate to the DI container in modern .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The DI container IS an Abstract Factory at runtime. <code>services.AddSingleton&lt;IPaymentProcessor, StripePaymentProcessor&gt;()</code> registers a factory for <code>IPaymentProcessor</code>. The container creates compatible families when you register all related services together. The difference: DI containers don't enforce family consistency at compile time — you can register <code>StripePaymentProcessor</code> with <code>PayPalReceiptGenerator</code> and the compiler won't complain. A typed Abstract Factory interface enforces this at compile time. Use DI for flexibility; use a typed factory when family consistency is a hard requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Factory Method become the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you need to create a <strong>family of related objects</strong> that must stay compatible — use Abstract Factory instead. Factory Method creates one product type; if <code>PaymentProcessor</code> and <code>ReceiptGenerator</code> must always come from the same provider (Stripe or PayPal), a single factory method can't enforce that constraint. Also avoid Factory Method when the creation logic is trivial and unlikely to vary — the extra abstraction adds indirection without benefit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Factory Method.md" class="internal-link">Factory Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Factory Method support the Open/Closed Principle?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The creator class is closed for modification: its <code>NotifyOrderConfirmedAsync</code> algorithm never changes. It's open for extension: adding a new channel means a new subclass of <code>NotificationCreator</code>, not an edit to existing code. The tradeoff is class proliferation — each new product type requires a new creator subclass. For many variants, Abstract Factory or a registry-based approach scales better.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Factory Method.md" class="internal-link">Factory Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use Prototype instead of just calling <code>new</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When construction is expensive (loading from DB, computing derived fields) and you need many similar objects. Prototype amortizes the construction cost: build one template, clone it N times with small variations. Also use it when the exact type isn't known at compile time — <code>prototype.Clone()</code> works without knowing the concrete type. The tradeoff: cloning can be as expensive as construction if the object graph is deep; profile before assuming it's faster.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Prototype.md" class="internal-link">Prototype</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between shallow and deep copy, and when does it matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Shallow copy duplicates the object's fields but shares reference-type values. Deep copy recursively duplicates the entire object graph. It matters when the copied object contains mutable reference types: two shallow copies sharing a <code>List&lt;OrderItem&gt;</code> will interfere with each other. Use deep copy when the clone must be fully independent. Use shallow copy when shared references are intentional (e.g., <code>Customer</code> is shared across orders — that's correct). The cost of deep copy scales with graph depth; for large graphs, consider serialization-based cloning (<code>JsonSerializer.Deserialize(JsonSerializer.Serialize(obj))</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Prototype.md" class="internal-link">Prototype</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the classical Singleton considered an anti-pattern in DI-based applications?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three reasons: (1) <strong>Hidden dependency</strong> — <code>AppConfig.Instance</code> doesn't appear in the constructor, so the dependency is invisible to callers and can't be mocked. (2) <strong>Testability</strong> — tests can't inject a different implementation; they're forced to use the real singleton, making tests environment-dependent. (3) <strong>Lifetime control</strong> — the class controls its own lifetime, bypassing the DI container's lifetime management. The DI-managed singleton solves all three: the dependency is explicit, injectable, and mockable. The tradeoff: DI-managed singletons require a DI container; classical singletons work without one (useful in library code or console apps without a host).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Singleton.md" class="internal-link">Singleton</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a captive dependency and how do you detect it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A captive dependency occurs when a longer-lived service holds a reference to a shorter-lived service. Example: a singleton <code>OrderService</code> injected with a scoped <code>DbContext</code> — the <code>DbContext</code> is captured for the application's lifetime, not the request's lifetime. This causes stale EF Core change tracking, connection pool exhaustion, and concurrency bugs across requests. Detection: enable <code>ValidateScopes = true</code> in <code>AddSingleton</code> options (default in development). The container throws at startup if a singleton depends on a scoped service. In production, also enable <code>ValidateOnBuild = true</code>. The fix: inject <code>IServiceScopeFactory</code> and create a scope per operation, or redesign the dependency to be singleton-safe.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Singleton.md" class="internal-link">Singleton</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is the classical Singleton still appropriate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In library code without a DI container, or when you need a truly global, immutable resource that must be accessible without injection (e.g., a logging sink initialized before the DI container starts). <code>Lazy&lt;T&gt;</code> is the correct implementation in these cases. Also appropriate for value objects that are expensive to construct and inherently stateless (e.g., a compiled <code>Regex</code> pattern). The signal: if the singleton holds mutable state or has dependencies, use DI. If it's an immutable, stateless resource, classical form is acceptable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Singleton.md" class="internal-link">Singleton</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Structural</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you test code that uses an Adapter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Test the consumer (<code>OrderService</code>) by injecting a mock <code>IInventoryService</code> — the adapter is invisible to the test. Test the adapter itself with integration tests against the real legacy system (or a recorded response). Unit-testing the adapter with a mock <code>LegacyInventorySystem</code> is valid but limited — the real value is verifying the XML translation is correct, which requires the actual legacy format. The tradeoff: integration tests are slower and environment-dependent; unit tests are fast but may miss translation bugs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Adapter.md" class="internal-link">Adapter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does an Adapter become a Facade?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the adapter starts simplifying the interface rather than just translating it. An Adapter preserves the full capability of the adaptee — every method on <code>IInventoryService</code> maps to a corresponding legacy operation. A Facade intentionally hides complexity, exposing only a subset of the subsystem's capabilities. If your "adapter" only exposes 3 of the legacy system's 20 operations and adds orchestration logic, it's a Facade. The distinction matters for maintenance: an Adapter should be a thin translation layer; a Facade can contain business logic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Adapter.md" class="internal-link">Adapter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Composite relate to the Visitor pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They're complementary. Composite defines the tree structure and uniform traversal. Visitor adds new operations to the tree without modifying the node classes. Example: <code>TaxVisitor</code>, <code>ShippingVisitor</code>, and <code>DiscountVisitor</code> can each traverse the same <code>IOrderComponent</code> tree without adding methods to <code>SingleProduct</code> or <code>ProductBundle</code>. Use Composite when the structure varies; use Visitor when the operations vary. Together, they handle both dimensions of variation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Composite.md" class="internal-link">Composite</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a Composite tree become a performance problem?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the tree is large, deep, or frequently traversed. <code>GetPrice()</code> on a bundle with 10,000 SKUs traverses all 10,000 nodes on every call. Mitigations: (1) cache computed values and invalidate on mutation, (2) use lazy evaluation (compute only when accessed), (3) denormalize — store the precomputed total and update it incrementally. The signal: profiling shows <code>GetPrice()</code> appearing in hot paths. The cost of caching: stale values if the tree is mutated without invalidation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Composite.md" class="internal-link">Composite</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide whether to use Bridge or Strategy for payment provider selection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Strategy selects an algorithm at runtime — the client chooses which strategy to inject. Bridge separates two dimensions of variation that both need to evolve independently. If you only need to swap payment providers (one dimension), Strategy is sufficient: inject <code>IPaymentGateway</code> and let the client choose. Bridge adds value when you also need payment types to vary independently — when both dimensions grow. The structural difference: Strategy has one interface; Bridge has two (abstraction + implementation). Use Strategy first; introduce Bridge when the second dimension appears.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Bridge.md" class="internal-link">Bridge</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does ADO.NET use Bridge instead of just having SqlCommand implement ICommand directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because the abstraction (<code>DbCommand</code>) and the implementation (<code>SqlConnection</code>) need to vary independently. <code>DbCommand</code> defines what operations are possible (execute, prepare, cancel); <code>SqlCommand</code> defines how they're executed against SQL Server. A new database provider (PostgreSQL) can implement <code>DbConnection</code>/<code>DbCommand</code> without changing the abstraction. A new command type (batch command) can be added to the abstraction without changing providers. If <code>SqlCommand</code> directly implemented <code>ICommand</code> without the Bridge hierarchy, adding PostgreSQL support would require duplicating the entire command abstraction. The cost: the hierarchy is complex; understanding ADO.NET requires understanding both layers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Bridge.md" class="internal-link">Bridge</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between Bridge and Dependency Injection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">DI is a mechanism for providing dependencies; Bridge is a structural pattern for organizing class hierarchies. They're complementary: you use DI to inject the <code>IPaymentGateway</code> implementation into the <code>PaymentOperation</code> abstraction. DI doesn't tell you how to structure the classes — Bridge does. Without Bridge, DI would inject a single <code>IPaymentService</code> that handles both dimensions; with Bridge, DI injects the gateway into the abstraction, and the abstraction handles the payment type logic. Bridge defines the structure; DI wires it together.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Bridge.md" class="internal-link">Bridge</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does ASP.NET Core Middleware implement the Decorator pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each middleware is a decorator over <code>RequestDelegate next</code>. <code>app.UseAuthentication()</code> registers a middleware that calls <code>next(context)</code> after authenticating. The pipeline is built by composing these decorators at startup: each <code>Use()</code> call wraps the current pipeline in a new decorator. The outermost middleware runs first. This is exactly the Decorator pattern: each middleware implements the same interface (<code>RequestDelegate</code>), holds a reference to the next, and adds behavior before/after. The cost: middleware ordering bugs are runtime errors, not compile-time errors.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Decorator.md" class="internal-link">Decorator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use Decorator vs inheritance for adding behavior?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Decorator when: (1) you need to add behavior at runtime or compose behaviors dynamically, (2) the class is sealed or from a third-party library, (3) you need multiple independent behaviors that can be combined in different ways. Inheritance when: the new behavior is a fundamental specialization of the type, not a cross-cutting concern. The key difference: inheritance is static (decided at compile time); Decorator is dynamic (decided at composition time). Decorator also avoids the fragile base class problem — changes to the base class don't affect decorators.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Decorator.md" class="internal-link">Decorator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the performance cost of a deep decorator chain?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each decorator adds one virtual dispatch and one async state machine (if async). For a 5-layer chain, that's 5 virtual calls and 5 async allocations per request. In practice, this is negligible compared to I/O (DB queries, HTTP calls). Profile before optimizing. If the chain is genuinely hot (millions of calls/second with no I/O), consider collapsing the chain into a single class for that specific path. The tradeoff: performance vs maintainability. Premature optimization of decorator chains is a common mistake.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Decorator.md" class="internal-link">Decorator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you identify intrinsic vs extrinsic state?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Intrinsic state is the same for all objects in a group — it doesn't change based on context. Extrinsic state is unique per object or changes based on context. For <code>Product</code>: <code>TaxRate</code> is intrinsic (same for all Electronics); <code>Price</code> is extrinsic (unique per SKU). The test: if two objects can share a piece of state without one affecting the other, it's intrinsic. If sharing would cause one object's change to affect another, it's extrinsic. The tradeoff: the more state you classify as intrinsic, the more memory you save, but the flyweight becomes less flexible.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Flyweight.md" class="internal-link">Flyweight</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Flyweight not worth the complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the memory savings are negligible relative to the system's total memory use, or when the shared objects are already small. Flyweight adds a factory, a cache, and the intrinsic/extrinsic split — meaningful complexity. Profile first: if 100,000 products each hold a 50-byte category struct, that's 5MB — probably not worth the pattern. If each holds a 10KB category object with images and rules, that's 1GB — Flyweight is justified. The signal: memory profiler shows many identical large objects.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Flyweight.md" class="internal-link">Flyweight</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does EF Core's lazy-loading proxy differ from a manually written virtual proxy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">EF Core generates the proxy class at runtime using Castle DynamicProxy — you don't write it. The proxy overrides every virtual navigation property; accessing the property triggers a DB query if the value isn't loaded. The difference from a manual proxy: EF Core's proxy is generated per entity type, not per instance; it uses the same <code>DbContext</code> that loaded the entity. A manual proxy is written once and works for all instances. The tradeoff: EF Core's proxy is automatic but requires virtual properties and a parameterless constructor; a manual proxy is explicit but requires more code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Proxy.md" class="internal-link">Proxy</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use a caching proxy vs <code>IMemoryCache</code> directly in the service?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a caching proxy when you want caching to be transparent to the service — the service doesn't know it's being cached. This is useful when the service is a third-party class you can't modify, or when you want to swap caching strategies (in-memory vs distributed) without changing the service. Use <code>IMemoryCache</code> directly in the service when caching is a core concern of the service (e.g., a product catalog service that always caches), or when you need fine-grained control over cache keys and expiration. The tradeoff: proxy keeps the service pure but adds indirection; direct caching is explicit but couples the service to the caching strategy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Proxy.md" class="internal-link">Proxy</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between a Proxy and a Decorator in terms of intent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Intent is the key difference — the structure is identical. A Proxy controls access: it decides whether to forward the call at all (protection proxy), when to forward it (virtual proxy), or whether to use a cached result instead (caching proxy). A Decorator always forwards the call and adds behavior around it. A logging decorator always calls <code>next.HandleAsync(order)</code> — it never skips the call. A protection proxy may throw before calling the real service. If the wrapper can prevent the call from reaching the real object, it's a Proxy. If it always calls through and adds behavior, it's a Decorator.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Proxy.md" class="internal-link">Proxy</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a Facade become a "god class" anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When it starts containing business logic instead of just orchestrating subsystems. A Facade should be a thin coordinator — it calls subsystems in the right order but doesn't make business decisions. If <code>OrderFacade</code> starts calculating discounts, validating business rules, or managing state, it's accumulating responsibilities it shouldn't have. The signal: the facade has more than 200-300 lines, or it's the hardest class to test. The fix: extract business logic into domain services; keep the facade as a pure orchestrator. The tradeoff: a thin facade is easy to test (mock all subsystems); a fat facade is hard to test and hard to change.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Facade.md" class="internal-link">Facade</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Should a Facade expose the subsystems it wraps, or hide them completely?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hide them. If callers can access <code>orderFacade.Payment.ChargeAsync()</code> directly, they bypass the facade's orchestration and the workflow guarantee breaks. The facade's value is the guaranteed sequence: check stock → charge → reserve → ship → notify. Exposing subsystems lets callers skip steps. The tradeoff: hiding subsystems means callers can't do advanced operations that the facade doesn't expose. In that case, add a new method to the facade rather than exposing the subsystem — the facade's interface should grow to cover legitimate use cases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Structural/Facade.md" class="internal-link">Facade</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Resilience Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is retry placement relative to circuit breaker important?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Retry should execute inside the same resilience pipeline before breaker decisions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Outer retries around an already open breaker create extra pressure and useless attempts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Proper ordering gives cleaner failure accounting and earlier protection.</li>
<li dir="auto">In Polly's outer-to-inner model this means placing retry <em>outside</em> the breaker, so each failed retry still passes through breaker evaluation and counts toward tripping it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Circuit Breaker.md" class="internal-link">Circuit Breaker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you avoid a half-open thundering herd in Kubernetes-scale deployments?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Limit probe concurrency and keep half-open trial volume small.</li>
<li dir="auto">Add jitter to retry and recovery timing.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use global controls (rate limits, queueing, bulkheads) so per-pod recovery does not synchronize spikes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Watch fleet-wide metrics, not only single-instance breaker events.</li>
<li dir="auto">The core trap: breaker state is process-local, so per-pod recovery must be coordinated with fleet-level controls or every pod probes in lockstep.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Circuit Breaker.md" class="internal-link">Circuit Breaker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which failures should trip the breaker, and which should not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Trip on dependency-health signals: timeouts, connection failures, HTTP <code>5xx</code>, and <code>429</code> when the client cannot absorb it.</li>
<li dir="auto">Do not trip on caller-side <code>4xx</code> like <code>400</code>/<code>404</code> — those reflect bad input, not an unhealthy dependency.</li>
<li dir="auto">Encode this in <code>ShouldHandle</code> so the breaker measures the dependency, not your users' mistakes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Get it wrong and the breaker opens on validation errors, blocking healthy traffic to a perfectly good dependency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Circuit Breaker.md" class="internal-link">Circuit Breaker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Your AI service wraps OpenAI APIs with per-tenant limits and runs on 4 instances. How do you enforce limits accurately, and which algorithm do you choose?</div></div><div class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use distributed shared state, usually Redis, because per-instance memory breaks global accuracy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Partition by tenant ID so quotas align with billing and fairness.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose token bucket when tenants need controlled burst capacity with stable average throughput.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use atomic operations (Lua or transaction pattern) for refill and consume to avoid race conditions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Return <code>429</code> with <code>Retry-After</code> and remaining quota headers to support client backoff.<br />
<strong>Why this question matters</strong></li>
<li dir="auto">It tests algorithm choice plus distributed systems correctness, not just definition recall.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Rate Limiting.md" class="internal-link">Rate Limiting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you prefer sliding window counter over fixed window in a public API?</div></div><div class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefer sliding window counter when edge fairness matters and fixed window boundary bursts are unacceptable.</li>
<li dir="auto">It gives near-rolling behavior with lower memory than sliding log.</li>
<li dir="auto">Accept approximation error in exchange for better operational cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep fixed window only where simplicity dominates and traffic patterns are predictable.<br />
<strong>Why this question matters</strong></li>
<li dir="auto">It checks whether the candidate can justify tradeoffs under realistic constraints.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Rate Limiting.md" class="internal-link">Rate Limiting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What failure mode should you choose if Redis-based rate limiting is unavailable: fail-open or fail-closed?</div></div><div class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Decide by endpoint risk profile, not globally.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fail-open for low-risk endpoints when availability is the top priority.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fail-closed for sensitive operations where abuse or cost explosion is unacceptable.</li>
<li dir="auto">Document and test the behavior with chaos drills.<br />
<strong>Why this question matters</strong></li>
<li dir="auto">It tests operational judgment and explicit risk tradeoff reasoning.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Rate Limiting.md" class="internal-link">Rate Limiting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does retry without jitter make outages worse and how does jitter fix it</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without jitter each client computes nearly identical retry times so failures synchronize into periodic traffic spikes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Those spikes hit while the dependency is already degraded which increases queue depth and recovery time.</li>
<li dir="auto">Jitter randomizes each delay so retries spread over time and reduce synchronized pressure.</li>
<li dir="auto">This improves recovery odds and stabilizes shared infrastructure such as load balancers and connection pools.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Tradeoff</strong> jitter reduces herd effects but increases per request timing variance and makes behavior slightly harder to predict.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Retry and Timeout Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Retry and Timeout Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent retry amplification in a multi layer microservices system</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Assign retry ownership to one layer per call path usually the edge caller or the service nearest the user boundary.</li>
<li dir="auto">Propagate cancellation tokens and deadlines so downstream services respect the remaining time budget.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep low retry counts and combine with circuit breaker and rate limits to avoid multiplicative pressure.</li>
<li dir="auto">Measure effective attempts per request in telemetry and alert when fan out exceeds budget.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Tradeoff</strong> centralizing retries improves control and cost but can reduce local autonomy for service teams.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/Patterns/Resilience Patterns/Retry and Timeout Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Retry and Timeout Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="software-architecture-system-architecture">System Architecture</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose orchestration over choreography in an event-driven workflow?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Orchestrate when the workflow is long-running and needs real ordering or compensation — a checkout that charges payment, reserves inventory, then ships. A central process manager keeps that flow easy to follow and roll back, at the cost of one more component that can bottleneck. Choreography fits loosely-related reactions, like "order placed" fanning out to email, analytics, and search: autonomous and decoupled, but no single place knows the whole story, so tracing gets harder as subscriptions grow. Rule of thumb — orchestrate transactions you must reason about end to end; choreograph independent reactions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Event-Driven Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Event-Driven Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you evolve integration event contracts without breaking consumers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treat the event as a public API — other teams deploy against it on their own schedule. Keep changes additive; never rename or drop a field in place. For a genuine breaking change, version it (<code>OrderPlaced.v2</code>) and publish both through a migration window until consumers move over. Consumer-driven contract tests in CI catch regressions before release, and deserialization-failure metrics surface a bad change in minutes. The mindset that keeps you safe: an event schema is a long-lived contract, not an internal DTO you can refactor freely.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Event-Driven Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Event-Driven Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you process events reliably under at-least-once delivery?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Duplicates and reordering are normal, so consumers need durable idempotency keys and conditional state changes. An outbox closes the local database-to-publish gap; per-key partitioning plus sequence numbers protects scoped order. An exactly-once claim is valid only for an explicit transactional boundary, such as a processor atomically checkpointing offsets and writing one supported sink. External calls still need their own idempotency contract.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Event-Driven Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Event-Driven Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can microservices lead to distributed data consistency problems, and how do you address them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each service owns its data, so cross-service business actions cannot assume one local ACID transaction. A distributed transaction can coordinate supported resources, but its coupling, latency, and recovery costs make sagas and local transactions the usual design.</li>
<li dir="auto">A later step can fail after an earlier local commit, producing partial state.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use sagas with compensating actions across local transactions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use outbox/inbox and idempotent handlers to survive retries and duplicates.</li>
<li dir="auto">Accept eventual consistency and make process state observable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Microservices.md" class="internal-link">Microservices</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide between monolith, modular monolith, and microservices for a new product?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Decide from team size, release pressure, domain volatility, and ops maturity.</li>
<li dir="auto">One team in discovery phase usually benefits most from monolith speed.</li>
<li dir="auto">Clear domains with limited platform capacity often fit modular monolith.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose microservices when independent deploy/scale constraints are proven.</li>
<li dir="auto">Re-evaluate architecture periodically as constraints change.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Microservices.md" class="internal-link">Microservices</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you enforce module boundaries in a modular monolith to prevent it from degrading into a traditional monolith?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Split each module into contracts, core, and infrastructure assemblies; allow cross-module references only to contracts. Give tables an owner, block cross-module joins, and use architecture tests to fail CI on forbidden project or namespace dependencies. The friction is intentional: a boundary that cannot reject a shortcut is only documentation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Modular Monolith.md" class="internal-link">Modular Monolith</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose a modular monolith over microservices, and what signals tell you it is time to extract?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose the modular monolith while domains can be owned as modules and one deployment remains reliable. Extract when a module repeatedly needs independent scaling, release cadence, security isolation, or reliability posture. Before cutover, preserve the domain contract but redesign the interaction for remote deadlines, retries, observability, and transaction boundaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Modular Monolith.md" class="internal-link">Modular Monolith</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a modular monolith and when is it better than microservices?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A modular monolith enforces explicit module boundaries (clear interfaces, no internal coupling) within a single deployment. It gives you maintainability and team autonomy without distributed systems complexity. It is better than microservices when independent deployment is not yet a hard requirement — which is most early-stage products.<br />
Cost: requires discipline to maintain module boundaries; without enforcement, it degrades into a big ball of mud.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Monolith Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Monolith Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do microservices become justified over a monolith?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When independent deployment is a hard requirement and the team can support the operational complexity (distributed tracing, network failures, eventual consistency, service mesh). The signal is usually: teams are blocked by each other's deployments, or a specific component needs independent scaling that the monolith cannot provide.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Monolith Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Monolith Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you mitigate cold start latency in serverless functions?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Measure initialization and scale-out latency on the chosen product, runtime, plan, package, and network path. Reduce initialization work, reuse clients in warm environments, and buy minimum/provisioned capacity when the latency objective justifies its fixed cost. Native AOT can help .NET startup when reflection and library constraints are acceptable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Serverless Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Serverless Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you avoid vendor lock-in with serverless functions?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Isolate business logic from the function host. The function handler should be a thin adapter that reads the trigger event, calls a provider-agnostic service, and returns a response. The core logic lives in a class library with no provider-specific dependencies. This makes the business logic portable even if the host (Azure Functions, AWS Lambda) is not. Cost: requires discipline to keep the adapter thin; teams often let provider-specific bindings leak into business logic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Serverless Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Serverless Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you model cost for a serverless workload vs a container-based one?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use the product's actual dimensions: requests, duration, allocated CPU/memory, provisioned or minimum instances, gateway, storage, egress, logging, and downstream connections. Some services scale to zero and some do not. Run the representative traffic shape through both pricing models and include latency and operating labor rather than assuming one universal crossover.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Serverless Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Serverless Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is SOA still the right choice over microservices?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SOA remains appropriate for enterprise integration scenarios: connecting heterogeneous legacy systems (SAP, Salesforce, custom ERP) via a shared integration layer, shared services used by multiple business units where microservice decomposition overhead isn't justified, and regulated industries requiring centralized governance and audit trails. Microservices are better for greenfield cloud-native systems where teams can own independent services end-to-end.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Architecture/System Architecture/Service-Oriented Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Service-Oriented Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="software-design">Software Design</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does software design end and architecture begin?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Software design operates at the module and class scale: naming, responsibilities, coupling, and the contracts between objects — decisions you can refactor cheaply</li>
<li dir="auto">Architecture operates at the system scale: service boundaries, data ownership, and communication styles — decisions that are expensive to reverse once other teams depend on them</li>
<li dir="auto">The line is fuzzy and shifts with scope: a class boundary in a monolith becomes a service boundary once it is extracted, so good design habits (clear responsibilities, low coupling) are what make that promotion possible</li>
<li dir="auto">Principles, paradigms, and testing are the levers of design; they show up in architecture too, but the blast radius of getting them wrong grows with the scale<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Design/Software Design.md" class="internal-link">Software Design</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="software-design-paradigms">Paradigms</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does event-driven architecture differ from event sourcing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Event-driven architecture is a communication style: components publish events and others react asynchronously. Event sourcing is a persistence pattern: the state of an entity is derived by replaying its event history rather than storing current state. You can use event-driven communication without event sourcing (most systems do). Event sourcing requires event-driven communication but adds the constraint that events are the source of truth for state.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Design/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you guarantee ordering of events in a distributed event-driven system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most message brokers guarantee ordering only within a partition or queue. Kafka guarantees ordering within a partition (use a consistent partition key, e.g., order ID). Azure Service Bus sessions guarantee ordering within a session. Cross-partition ordering is not guaranteed — design consumers to be idempotent and handle out-of-order delivery. If strict global ordering is required, use a single partition (which limits throughput) or a sequencer service.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Design/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must event consumers be idempotent in an at-least-once delivery system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most message brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery: a message may be delivered more than once if the consumer crashes after processing but before acknowledging. Without idempotency, duplicate delivery causes double-charging, double-reserving, or duplicate records. Mitigation: track processed event IDs in a <code>ProcessedEvents</code> table and skip duplicates, or design operations to be naturally idempotent (SET stock = X instead of stock -= Y).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Design/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a function "pure" and why does purity matter for testing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent: same inputs always produce the same output.</li>
<li dir="auto">No side effects means no I/O, no global state reads/writes, no exceptions from external dependencies.</li>
<li dir="auto">Testing cost: pure functions need zero mocks — just call with inputs and assert outputs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Parallelism: pure functions are safe to run concurrently without locks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: real systems need side effects (DB writes, HTTP calls). The FP discipline is to push side effects to the edges and keep the core logic pure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Software Design/Paradigms/Functional Programming.md" class="internal-link">Functional Programming</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does immutability prevent bugs in concurrent code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Shared mutable state is the root cause of race conditions: two threads read-modify-write the same object.</li>
<li dir="auto">Immutable objects can be shared freely across threads — no lock needed because no mutation is possible.</li>
<li dir="auto">In C#: <code>record</code> types with <code>init</code>-only properties, <code>ImmutableDictionary&lt;K,V&gt;</code>, <code>string</code> (already immutable).</li>
